topics.title: Concepts and Techniques - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts and Techniques - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Blockchain** is a distributed, decentralized, immutable ledger technology that records transactions across a peer-to-peer (P2P) network in a verifiable and tamper-resistant manner. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PECCT412), blockchain is fundamentally a chain of cryptographically linked blocks, where each block contains a batch of validated transactions, a timestamp, and a reference (hash) to the preceding block.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A blockchain is a *distributed digital ledger* implemented as a *chronologically ordered, cryptographically chained list of records* (blocks), maintained by a consensus protocol across a decentralized network of nodes, such that altering any historical record requires prohibitive computational effort.

### Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Distributed Ledger** | A replicated, shared, and synchronized digital data structure spread across multiple sites, countries, or institutions. |
| **Decentralization** | Transfer of control and decision-making from a centralized entity to a distributed network of participants. |
| **Immutability** | The property that once data is recorded on the blockchain, it cannot be altered retroactively without invalidating subsequent blocks. |
| **Consensus Protocol** | A fault-tolerant mechanism used to achieve agreement on a single data value among distributed processes. |
| **Cryptographic Hash** | A one-way mathematical function that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size bit string. |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Public Notice Board with Super Glue"**
> Imagine a community notice board in a village:
> 1. **Distributed Copies** — Every villager has an identical copy of the notice board in their home. There is no single "master" board.
> 2. **Sequential Pages** — Notices are written on pages, and each new page contains a unique summary (fingerprint) of the *previous* page. This creates a chain.
> 3. **Public Verification** — Before a new page is added, villagers collectively check and verify its contents.
> 4. **Permanent Ink** — Once written, the ink becomes permanent (immutable). If someone tries to change an old page, the fingerprint (hash) won't match, and everyone notices immediately.
>
> This is essentially how blockchain works — except the "village" is a global P2P network, and the "ink" is cryptography.

### Fundamental Properties (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four pillars of blockchain (high-frequency KTU short-answer topic):
> 1. **Decentralization** — No single point of control or failure.
> 2. **Transparency** — All participants can view the ledger (on public chains).
> 3. **Immutability** — Records cannot be modified after consensus.
> 4. **Security** — Cryptographic primitives protect data integrity and authenticity.

---

### Distinguishing Blockchain from Traditional Databases

| Feature | Traditional Database | Blockchain |
|---------|---------------------|------------|
| Architecture | Centralized (Client-Server) | Decentralized (P2P) |
| Control | Single administrator | Distributed consensus |
| Immutability | Mutable (admin can edit) | Practically immutable |
| Transparency | Restricted to admins | Publicly auditable |
| Performance | High TPS (Transactions Per Second) | Lower TPS (consensus overhead) |
| Trust Model | Trusted third party | Trustless (cryptographic proof) |
| Failure Point | Single point of failure | Fault-tolerant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** Blockchain is *not* a replacement for databases — it is best suited for scenarios requiring trust minimization, auditability, and multi-party consensus over a shared state.

---

### Why Blockchain Matters — Engineering Utility

- **Finance**: Cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum), cross-border payments.
- **Supply Chain**: Provenance tracking of goods (e.g., IBM Food Trust).
- **Healthcare**: Secure, interoperable patient records.
- **Governance**: Smart contracts for automated legal agreements.
- **IoT Security**: Decentralized identity and device authentication.
- **Academic Credentials**: Tamper-proof degree verification (relevant to KTU students!).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Anatomy of a Block

Every block in a blockchain is a structured data container. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding the **internal structure** of a block.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              BLOCK HEADER               │
│  • Block Version Number                 │
│  • Previous Block Hash (Parent Hash)    │
│  • Merkle Root (transaction summary)    │
│  • Timestamp                            │
│  • Difficulty Target (nBits)            │
│  • Nonce                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           BLOCK BODY                    │
│  • Transaction Counter                  │
│  • List of Validated Transactions       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           BLOCK METADATA                │
│  • Block Size (bytes)                   │
│  • Block Height (chain position)        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Cryptographic Hash Functions — The Backbone of Blockchain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A cryptographic hash function $H(\cdot)$ takes an input $m$ of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-length output $h$ called the **digest** or **hash**.

$$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$

where $n$ is the output bit length (e.g., $n = 256$ for SHA-256).

#### Properties of Cryptographic Hash Functions (KTU Board Essential)

1. **Determinism** — The same input always produces the same output: $H(m) = H(m)$ for identical $m$.
2. **Pre-image Resistance** — Given $h$, it is computationally infeasible to find $m$ such that $H(m) = h$.
3. **Second Pre-image Resistance** — Given $m_1$, it is infeasible to find $m_2 \neq m_1$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
4. **Collision Resistance** — It is infeasible to find any two distinct inputs $m_1, m_2$ such that $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$.
5. **Avalanche Effect** — A small change in input drastically changes the output (e.g., flipping one bit alters ~50% of output bits).
6. **Puzzle Friendliness** — For a given target $T$, finding $m$ such that $H(m) < T$ has no shortcut.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Description |
|---------|--------------------|-------------|
| Hash Function | $H(m) = h$ | Maps arbitrary input to fixed-length output |
| Hash Length | $\vert h \vert = n$ bits | Output bit size (e.g., 256 for SHA-256) |
| Collision Probability (Birthday) | $P \approx 1 - e^{-n^2/(2 \cdot 2^N)}$ | Probability of collision in $n$ samples from $2^N$ space |
| Mining Difficulty | $D = \frac{D_{\text{max}}}{T}$ | Ratio of maximum target to current target |
| Block Reward | $R = R_{\text{base}} + \sum \text{Fees}$ | Base reward + transaction fees |
| Merkle Root Depth | $d = \lceil \log_2(t) \rceil$ | Depth for $t$ transactions in Merkle tree |
| Chain Immutability Cost | $\text{Cost} = q \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{k} c(i)$ | Cost to alter $k$ consecutive blocks with hash rate $q$ |
| Genesis Block | $H(\text{prev}) = 0$ | First block has no predecessor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Hash Functions:** Beyond blockchain, SHA-256 and Keccak-256 are used in digital signatures, password storage (with salting), file integrity verification (checksums), and TLS/SSL handshakes.

### The Chain Structure — Cryptographic Linking

The "chain" in blockchain arises from each block storing the hash of the previous block's header.

$$H_i = H(\text{Block}_i \text{ Header})$$

$$\text{Block}_{i+1}.\text{prev\_hash} = H_i$$

This creates a **cryptographic dependency cascade**: altering $\text{Block}_i$ changes $H_i$, which invalidates $\text{Block}_{i+1}.\text{prev\_hash}$, which in turn changes $H_{i+1}$, and so on.

### Merkle Tree — Efficient Transaction Verification

A **Merkle Tree** is a binary tree of hashes used to summarize all transactions in a block efficiently.

- **Leaves**: $L_j = H(T_j)$ where $T_j$ is the $j$-th transaction.
- **Internal Nodes**: $N_{i,j} = H(N_{i-1,2j} \parallel N_{i-1,2j+1})$
- **Root**: $M = N_{\text{depth},0}$ — single hash representing *all* transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Merkle Trees?** They enable **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — a light client can verify a transaction's inclusion with only $O(\log n)$ hashes instead of downloading the entire block.

### Consensus Mechanisms (KTU Module 1 Core Topic)

A consensus algorithm ensures all honest nodes agree on the ledger state.

| Mechanism | Used By | Key Idea | Energy Usage |
|-----------|---------|----------|--------------|
| **Proof of Work (PoW)** | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Solve computational puzzle; first to find nonce wins | Very High |
| **Proof of Stake (PoS)** | Ethereum (post-Merge) | Validators stake tokens; chosen probabilistically | Low |
| **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** | EOS, Tron | Token holders vote for delegates | Low |
| **Proof of Authority (PoA)** | VeChain, testnets | Approved validators | Minimal |
| **Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)** | Hyperledger | Multi-round voting among known nodes | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Byzantine Generals Problem:** Consensus must tolerate *malicious* or *faulty* nodes that may behave arbitrarily. PoW solves this probabilistically; PBFT solves it deterministically (requires $< \frac{1}{3}$ faulty nodes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Bound)

**Context:** Why are 256-bit hashes considered secure? This derivation shows the probability of a collision as a function of the hash output size and number of samples.

**Given:**
- A hash function $H$ producing $N = 2^n$ possible outputs.
- $q$ distinct inputs are hashed.
- We seek the probability $P$ that at least two inputs produce the same hash (collision).

**Step 1:** The probability that the first input produces a unique hash is 1.
$$P_1 = 1$$

**Step 2:** The probability that the second input does *not* collide with the first:
$$P_2 = 1 - \frac{1}{N}$$

**Step 3:** The probability that the third input does not collide with any previous:
$$P_3 = 1 - \frac{2}{N}$$

**Step 4:** In general, the $i$-th input avoids all previous collisions with probability:
$$P_i = 1 - \frac{i-1}{N}$$

**Step 5:** The probability of *no collision* after $q$ inputs is the product of all individual avoidance probabilities:
$$P_{\text{no collision}} = \prod_{i=1}^{q} \left(1 - \frac{i-1}{N}\right) = \prod_{k=0}^{q-1} \left(1 - \frac{k}{N}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Applying the approximation $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$ (valid when $k \ll N$):
$$\ln P_{\text{no collision}} \approx \sum_{k=0}^{q-1} \left(-\frac{k}{N}\right) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{q-1} k = -\frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{q(q-1)}{2}$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiating to recover $P_{\text{no collision}}$:
$$P_{\text{no collision}} \approx e^{-q(q-1)/(2N)} \approx e^{-q^2/(2N)}$$

**Step 8:** The probability of *at least one collision* is:
$$P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - P_{\text{no collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-q^2/(2N)}$$

**Step 9:** Setting $P_{\text{collision}} = 0.5$ and solving for $q$ gives the **birthday bound**:
$$q \approx \sqrt{2N \ln 2} \approx 1.18 \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 10:** For SHA-256, $N = 2^{256}$, so:
$$q_{\text{critical}} \approx 1.18 \cdot 2^{128}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Finding a collision in SHA-256 by brute force requires approximately $2^{128}$ operations — computationally infeasible with current technology. This justifies 256-bit security in Bitcoin and Ethereum.

---

### Derivation 2: Proof-of-Work Difficulty Target

**Context:** In Bitcoin, miners must find a nonce $N$ such that:
$$H(\text{Header} \parallel N) < T$$
where $T$ is the current **target** threshold.

**Step 1:** The target $T$ is derived from the compact "nBits" field stored in the block header. The relationship is:
$$T = \text{nBits} \times 256^{(\text{exp} - 3)}$$

**Step 2:** Difficulty $D$ is defined relative to the maximum target $T_{\max}$ (the target for the genesis-era block):
$$D = \frac{T_{\max}}{T}$$

**Step 3:** The expected number of hash attempts to find a valid nonce is:
$$E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{T_{\max}}{T} = D$$

**Step 4:** For Bitcoin's genesis block, $D = 1$. For Bitcoin in 2024, $D \approx 80 \times 10^{12}$ (80 trillion).

**Step 5:** The probability of success in a single hash attempt is:
$$P(\text{success}) = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

**Step 6:** The expected time to find a block at network hash rate $H$ (hashes per second):
$$E[\text{time}] = \frac{D}{H}$$

**Step 7:** Bitcoin targets a 10-minute block interval, so the difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks (~$14$ days):
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{expected}}}$$

**Step 8:** This self-correcting mechanism ensures predictable block production regardless of total network hash rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Application:** Ethereum's "difficulty bomb" before the Merge to PoS exponentially increased $D$ to incentivize the transition away from PoW.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Blockchain vs. Other Distributed Systems

| Property | Blockchain | Traditional Distributed DB | Centralized Cloud DB |
|----------|------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Consensus Requirement | Mandatory (e.g., PoW/PoS) | Often leader-based (Raft, Paxos) | None — single authority |
| CAP Theorem Trade-off | Favored: Consistency + Partition tolerance | Tunable | Favored: Availability |
| Trust Model | Trustless | Trust-based consortium | Trust in operator |
| Latency (typical) | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds | Milliseconds |
| Throughput (TPS) | 7 (BTC), 30 (ETH L1), 4000+ (Solana) | Thousands | Hundreds of thousands |
| Storage Model | Full replication (Grows linearly) | Sharding / partitioning | Centralized / replicated |
| Auditability | Native & cryptographically enforced | Application-level logs | Operator-controlled |
| Censorship Resistance | High (public chains) | Low | Very Low |
| Energy Consumption | High (PoW), Low (PoS) | Low | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### Diagram 4: Blockchain Transaction Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Step1["User Initiates Transaction\nSigned with Private Key"] --> Step2["Transaction Broadcast\nto P2P Network"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Full Nodes Validate\nSignature Balance Rules"]
    Step3 --> Step4["Valid Transactions\nEnter Mempool"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Miner or Validator\nSelects Transactions"]
    Step5 --> Step6["Construct Block Header\nWith Merkle Root and Prev Hash"]
    Step6 --> Step7["Execute Consensus\nPoW Mining or PoS Selection"]
    Step7 --> Step8["Block Found\nBroadcast to Network"]
    Step8 --> Step9["Other Nodes Verify\nConsensus Rules and Signatures"]
    Step9 --> Step10{"Consensus Achieved?"}
    Step10 -->|Yes| Step11["Block Appended\nto Local Ledger"]
    Step10 -->|No| Step12["Block Rejected\nFork Resolved by Longest Chain Rule"]
    Step11 --> Step13["Transaction Confirmed\nAfter N Block Confirmations"]
    
    style Step1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Step7 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Step11 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style Step13 fill:#c3e6cb,stroke:#155724
    style Step12 fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**
*Define blockchain. List any four key characteristics of blockchain technology.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Blockchain is a distributed, decentralized, append-only data structure that stores records (transactions) in cryptographically linked blocks, maintained across a peer-to-peer network through a consensus mechanism.

**Four Key Characteristics:**
1. **Decentralization** — No central authority; control is distributed among network participants.
2. **Immutability** — Once recorded, transactions cannot be altered without invalidating all subsequent blocks.
3. **Transparency** — All participants on public blockchains can view transaction history.
4. **Consensus** — Agreement on ledger state is achieved through algorithms like PoW, PoS, or PBFT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "distributed" with "decentralized." A distributed database may still have a central administrator. Blockchain is *both* distributed **and** decentralized.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Expected Pattern]**
*What is a cryptographic hash function? Mention any three properties it must satisfy for use in blockchain.*

**Model Answer:**

A **cryptographic hash function** is a deterministic mathematical function that maps an input of arbitrary length to a fixed-length output (digest), designed to be one-way and collision-resistant.

**Three Essential Properties:**
1. **Pre-image Resistance** — Given hash $h$, finding input $m$ such that $H(m) = h$ is computationally infeasible.
2. **Collision Resistance** — Finding two distinct inputs $m_1 \neq m_2$ with $H(m_1) = H(m_2)$ is computationally infeasible.
3. **Avalanche Effect** — A one-bit change in input changes approximately 50% of output bits, ensuring non-predictability.

*(Additional acceptable properties: determinism, second pre-image resistance, puzzle friendliness.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do *not* list "speed" as a property — although hash functions are fast, that is not a security-critical property. Stick to the four canonical security properties (pre-image, second pre-image, collision resistance, avalanche effect).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern Note:** KTU Module 1 typically includes a 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the internal structure of a block in a blockchain with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the Merkle root. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A block in a blockchain consists of two main parts: the **block header** and the **block body**.

**Block Header contains:**
- **Block Version Number** — Indicates which set of block validation rules to follow.
- **Previous Block Hash (Parent Hash)** — 256-bit hash of the previous block's header, creating the chain link.
- **Merkle Root** — 256-bit hash summarizing all transactions in the current block.
- **Timestamp** — Unix time of block creation (seconds since Jan 1, 1970 UTC).
- **Difficulty Target (nBits)** — Compact encoding of the PoW difficulty threshold.
- **Nonce** — 32-bit number miners vary to find a valid hash below the target.

**Block Body contains:**
- **Transaction Counter** — Number of transactions in the block.
- **List of Validated Transactions** — Each transaction includes sender, receiver, amount, signature, etc.

**Role of the Merkle Root:**
The Merkle root is a single hash that cryptographically summarizes *all* transactions in a block. It is computed by:
1. Hashing each transaction $T_j$ to obtain leaf $L_j = H(T_j)$.
2. Pairwise hashing of leaves to form internal nodes.
3. Repeating until a single root hash $M$ is obtained.

**Functions of the Merkle Root:**
- **Integrity Verification** — Any change to any transaction alters the Merkle root.
- **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — Light clients can prove a transaction's inclusion using only $O(\log n)$ hashes (Merkle proof).
- **Efficient Block Validation** — Nodes can verify block contents without storing all transactions.

**Diagram:** (Refer to the Block Structure diagram in Section 4.)

---

**(b)** Describe the Proof of Work (PoW) consensus mechanism. Explain why PoW makes blockchain tamper-resistant. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Proof of Work (PoW) Concept:**
PoW is a consensus algorithm where participants (miners) compete to solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a valid solution earns the right to propose the next block and receives a block reward.

**The PoW Puzzle:**
Miners vary a 32-bit **nonce** $N$ in the block header until the resulting hash satisfies:
$$H(\text{Header} \parallel N) < T$$
where $T$ is the network-defined difficulty target.

**Steps in PoW:**
1. Miners collect pending transactions from the mempool.
2. They construct a block header with these transactions, the previous block's hash, and a candidate Merkle root.
3. Miners iterate through billions of nonces, hashing the header repeatedly.
4. The first miner to find a hash below the target broadcasts the block to the network.
5. Other nodes verify the solution trivially (one hash check) and accept the block.
6. The winning miner receives the **block subsidy** (newly minted coins) plus **transaction fees**.

**Why PoW Makes Blockchain Tamper-Resistant:**

1. **Computational Cost of Alteration** — To modify Block $i$, an attacker must re-mine Block $i$, then re-mine *all* subsequent blocks $(i+1, i+2, ..., \text{tip})$ faster than the honest network adds new blocks. This requires controlling more than 50% of the total network hash rate (a **51% attack**).

2. **Economic Disincentive** — A 51% attack on Bitcoin would require hardware and electricity costing billions of dollars, with the attacker's own holdings losing value if confidence in the chain drops — making honest mining more profitable.

3. **Cryptographic Chain Dependency** — Each block's hash depends on the previous block's header. Altering any historical block changes its hash, which propagates forward, invalidating all subsequent blocks.

4. **Probabilistic Finality** — The deeper a block is buried under subsequent blocks, the exponentially harder it becomes to reverse. After 6 confirmations on Bitcoin, reversal is considered practically infeasible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Section (b):** Students often *define* 51% attack but fail to explain the *cost analysis*. Always include the economic argument: a 51% attack is not just *computationally* hard but also *economically* irrational for a rational attacker whose own stake depreciates upon attack success.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** Differentiate between centralized, distributed, and decentralized systems. Explain how blockchain achieves decentralization with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

| System Type | Definition | Control |
|-------------|-----------|---------|
| **Centralized** | Single entity (server, organization) holds authority and processes all transactions. | One master node controls the system. |
| **Distributed** | Multiple nodes cooperate to process workload, but may still have a central coordinator. | Logically centralized, physically distributed. |
| **Decentralized** | No central authority; all nodes are equal peers that coordinate via consensus. | No single point of control. |

**Example of Centralized:** A traditional bank maintains a centralized ledger. All customers must trust the bank.

**Example of Distributed:** Google's search infrastructure — physically distributed across thousands of servers, but Google centrally controls the algorithms and data.

**Example of Decentralized:** Bitcoin — every full node holds an identical copy of the ledger. No single entity controls the network. New blocks are added only when consensus is reached via PoW.

**How Blockchain Achieves Decentralization:**

1. **Peer-to-Peer Network** — All nodes are equal peers; no privileged server exists.
2. **Full Replication** — Every full node stores a complete copy of the ledger.
3. **Consensus-Based Updates** — New blocks are added only when the majority of nodes agree (via PoW, PoS, etc.).
4. **Open Participation** — Anyone can join the network, run a node, and participate in validation (permissionless blockchains).
5. **Cryptographic Authority** — Identity and authority are established through private keys, not through a central registrar.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of a Merkle tree. How does it enable efficient transaction verification in a blockchain? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Merkle Tree Concept:**
A Merkle tree (or binary hash tree) is a data structure that summarizes a large set of data items (e.g., transactions) into a single fixed-size hash called the **Merkle root**. It was invented by Ralph Merkle in 1979.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Take $n$ transactions $T_1, T_2, ..., T_n$.
2. Hash each transaction to form leaves: $L_i = H(T_i)$.
3. If $n$ is odd, duplicate the last leaf to make it even.
4. Concatenate adjacent leaf pairs and hash them to form the next level: $N_{i,j} = H(L_{2j} \parallel L_{2j+1})$.
5. Repeat level by level until only one hash remains — the **Merkle root** $M$.

**Diagram:** (Refer to Merkle Tree diagram in Section 4.)

**Efficient Transaction Verification (SPV):**

In a blockchain with thousands of transactions per block, a light client (e.g., a mobile wallet) cannot store or download all transactions. Merkle trees enable **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)**:

- To prove transaction $T_j$ is included in a block, a full node provides a **Merkle proof** — a list of $O(\log_2 n)$ sibling hashes along the path from $L_j$ to the root.
- The light client computes the root hash from the path and compares it to the Merkle root stored in the block header.
- If the computed root matches the header, the transaction's inclusion is verified.
- **Verification cost:** $O(\log_2 n)$ hashes instead of $O(n)$ storage.

**Mathematical Form:**
For a block with $t$ transactions, the depth of the Merkle tree is:
$$d = \lceil \log_2 t \rceil$$
Thus, a Merkle proof requires at most $d$ sibling hashes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Section (b):** Students often draw the tree but forget to explain *why* it matters. Always link the structure to a practical benefit — namely, SPV, bandwidth savings, and logarithmic verification complexity. Drawing the tree alone earns only ~3 of 7 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Topic: Concepts and Techniques - Introduction**

- **Definition:** Blockchain = Distributed + Decentralized + Immutable + Cryptographically Chained Ledger.
- **Four Pillars:** Decentralization, Transparency, Immutability, Security (memorize for short answers).
- **Block Structure:** Header (version, prev_hash, merkle_root, timestamp, nBits, nonce) + Body (transactions).
- **Genesis Block:** The first block; has `prev_hash = 0` and no parent.
- **Hash Function:** $H(m) = h$, fixed-length, one-way, collision-resistant, deterministic, avalanche effect.
- **SHA-256:** Used by Bitcoin; output = 256 bits, collision probability ~$2^{128}$ attempts.
- **Cryptographic Chain:** $\text{prev\_hash}_{i+1} = H(\text{Header}_i)$; altering one block invalidates all subsequent blocks.
- **Merkle Tree:** Binary hash tree summarizing transactions; enables SPV with $O(\log n)$ proof size.
- **PoW:** Miners find nonce such that $H(\text{Header} \parallel N) < T$; difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks in Bitcoin.
- **PoS:** Validators chosen probabilistically based on staked tokens; energy-efficient vs. PoW.
- **Byzantine Generals Problem:** Requires consensus in presence of malicious nodes; solved probabilistically by PoW, deterministically (with $< \frac{1}{3}$ faulty) by PBFT.
- **51% Attack:** Controlling >50% of hash rate allows transaction reversal; economically irrational on mature chains.
- **Decentralization Layers:** P2P network layer + Consensus layer + Ledger layer + Application layer (smart contracts).
- **Public vs. Private Blockchains:** Public = permissionless (Bitcoin, Ethereum); Private = permissioned (Hyperledger, Corda).
- **CAP Theorem:** Blockchain typically favors **Consistency + Partition tolerance** over raw Availability.
- **TPS:** Bitcoin ~7, Ethereum L1 ~30, Solana ~4000+, Hyperledger Fabric ~3000+ — note trade-offs with decentralization.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Tips:**
> 1. Always define technical terms *before* explaining them.
> 2. For 7-mark questions, include a diagram (extra 1–2 marks).
> 3. For 14-mark questions, structure with sub-headings: Definition → Architecture → Working → Applications → Diagram.
> 4. Memorize the four hash function security properties verbatim.
> 5. Be ready to compare PoW vs. PoS (favourite 7-mark question).
> 6. KTU Module 1 frequently asks for the *Genesis Block* special case — mention `prev_hash = 0`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plaintext and Cipher Text
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Plaintext and Cipher Text # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of cryptography and blockchain technologies, **plaintext** and **ciphertext** are the two fundamental data states through which information transitions during a cryptographic operation. **Plaintext (P):** Plaintext, often denoted by the symbol **P**, is the original, human-readable, unencrypted message, data, or information in its natural, intelligible form. It is the input to any cryptographic process and the output of a successful decryption operation. In blockchain terminology, this could be a transaction payload, a smart contract value, account balances, or any data that is meant to be processed or stored on the distributed ledger. **Ciphertext (C):** Ciphertext, conventionally denoted by the symbol **C**, is the scrambled, unintelligible, and seemingly random output produced when an encryption algorithm is applied to plaintext using a specific key. Ciphertext is designed such that, without possession of the correct decryption key, recovering the original plaintext should be computationally infeasible. In blockchain, while the blocks themselves may not be encrypted, hashing functions produce ciphertext-like fixed-length outputs from plaintext transaction data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Plaintext and ciphertext form the foundational vocabulary of Module 1. Every subsequent concept (hashing, encryption, digital signatures, Merkle trees) relies on the student being able to identify which state a piece of data is in at any point of a cryptographic pipeline. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Locked Steel Box Imagine you want to send a confidential document to a friend living in another city. 1. The **plaintext** is the original document lying openly on your desk, readable by anyone who walks by. 2. You place the document inside a **heavy steel box** and lock it with a key that only your friend possesses a copy of. 3. The locked steel box travelling through the courier network is the **ciphertext**. The courier, the sorting hub, and every intermediary sees only a sealed metallic box — they cannot read the contents without the key. 4. When the box reaches your friend, they use their copy of the key to unlock it, and the original document (plaintext) is restored. In this analogy: - The **document** = Plaintext - The **locked steel box** = Ciphertext - The **lock and key** = Cryptographic algorithm and secret key - The **unlocking action at the receiver** = Decryption function > [!NOTE] > **Plaintext** is *not* limited to text. It can be any form of data — binary files, images, audio, transaction records, executable code, or numerical values. Similarly, **ciphertext** is a binary blob of seemingly random bytes regardless of whether the original plaintext was text or multimedia. ## 1.3 Standard Cryptographic Notation The following notation is universally accepted in cryptography literature and is what KTU examiners expect: | Symbol | Meaning | Type | |:------:|:-------:|:----:| | $P$ | Plaintext | Input | | $C$ | Ciphertext | Output | | $K$ | Cryptographic Key | Secret/Public parameter | | $E$ | Encryption Function | Transformation | | $D$ | Decryption Function | Inverse Transformation | | $\mathcal{M}$ | Message Space | Set of all possible plaintexts | | $\mathcal{C}$ | Ciphertext Space | Set of all possible ciphertexts | ## 1.4 Why Plaintext and Ciphertext Matter in Blockchain In a blockchain network: - Transaction details (sender, receiver, amount) are **plaintext** stored on the public ledger. - A cryptographic hash function converts this plaintext transaction into a fixed-length **ciphertext-like digest** (e.g., a **256-bit** SHA-256 output). - Digital signatures operate on the plaintext message digest, producing a signature that can be verified against the original plaintext. - Public-key encryption (used in some blockchain layers) directly transforms a recipient's plaintext into ciphertext that only their private key can unlock. Without a clear understanding of plaintext and ciphertext, the student cannot progress to understanding hashing, block validation, or consensus mechanisms. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Plaintext → Ciphertext Transformation The transition from plaintext to ciphertext follows a deterministic, repeatable procedure governed by a cryptographic algorithm. Below is the breakdown of every logical step in this pipeline: ### Step 1: Plaintext Identification The sender or system identifies the data that requires protection or transformation. This plaintext $P$ is drawn from a defined **message space** $\mathcal{M}$, where every possible plaintext is a valid member. $$P \in \mathcal{M}$$ ### Step 2: Key Selection A cryptographic key $K$ is selected from the **key space** $\mathcal{K}$. The key controls exactly how the transformation occurs. Different keys, even with the same plaintext and algorithm, will produce entirely different ciphertexts. $$K \in \mathcal{K}$$ ### Step 3: Encryption Operation The encryption function $E$ takes both the plaintext $P$ and the key $K$ as inputs, and produces the ciphertext $C$ as output. $$C = E_K(P)$$ The encryption function $E$ must be: - **Deterministic:** The same $P$ and $K$ always yield the same $C$. - **Injective (one-to-one):** No two distinct plaintexts should map to the same ciphertext under a given key — this prevents ambiguity during decryption. - **Efficiently computable:** Encryption should be fast and resource-friendly for the legitimate user. ### Step 4: Ciphertext Transmission or Storage The ciphertext $C$ is now transmitted over an insecure channel (network, public ledger) or stored in an unprotected medium. An adversary observing $C$ gains no meaningful information about $P$ without the key. ### Step 5: Decryption Operation The legitimate receiver, who possesses the key $K$ (or a mathematically related key in asymmetric systems), applies the decryption function $D$ to recover the original plaintext. $$P = D_K(C)$$ ### Step 6: Verification The recovered plaintext $P$ is validated against checksums, message authentication codes, or hash digests to confirm integrity and authenticity. ## 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' | Question | Answer | |:---------|:-------| | **Why** do we need ciphertext? | To protect confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity of plaintext when it travels through untrusted environments. | | **How** is ciphertext produced? | By applying a mathematically reversible (encryption) or one-way (hashing) transformation to plaintext using a key. | | **Why** is the key separate from the algorithm? | So that security rests on the secrecy or complexity of the key, not on the secrecy of the algorithm (Kerckhoffs's Principle). | | **How** is plaintext recovered? | By applying the inverse function $D$ using the correct key $K$ in the case of encryption, or by recomputing and comparing in the case of hashing. | > [!NOTE] > **Kerckhoffs's Principle (1883):** A cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system, except the key, is public knowledge. This principle underpins modern blockchain design — algorithms like SHA-256 and ECDSA are fully open-source, yet the security comes from the keys. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Equation / Notation | Meaning | Domain of Use | |:-------------------|:--------|:--------------| | $C = E_K(P)$ | Encryption of plaintext $P$ under key $K$ produces ciphertext $C$ | Symmetric & Asymmetric Encryption | | $P = D_K(C)$ | Decryption of ciphertext $C$ under key $K$ recovers plaintext $P$ | Symmetric & Asymmetric Encryption | | $h = H(P)$ | Hash of plaintext $P$ produces fixed-length digest $h$ | Hashing (SHA-256, Keccak-256) | | $P \in \mathcal{M}$ | Plaintext $P$ belongs to message space $\mathcal{M}$ | Cryptographic System Definition | | $C \in \mathcal{C}$ | Ciphertext $C$ belongs to ciphertext space $\mathcal{C}$ | Cryptographic System Definition | | $\vert P \vert = n$ | Length of plaintext in bits | Information-Theoretic Analysis | | $\vert C \vert = m$ | Length of ciphertext in bits (may equal $n$ for stream ciphers, or differ for block ciphers) | Information-Theoretic Analysis | | $E : \mathcal{M} \times \mathcal{K} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ | Encryption as a mapping from plaintext-key pair to ciphertext | Formal Cryptographic Model | | $D : \mathcal{C} \times \mathcal{K} \rightarrow \mathcal{M}$ | Decryption as a mapping from ciphertext-key pair to plaintext | Formal Cryptographic Model | | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_K(P)$ | Signature $\sigma$ produced from plaintext $P$ using private key $K$ | Digital Signatures in Blockchain | | Verify$(P, \sigma, K_{pub})$ $\rightarrow$ True/False | Verification of signature against plaintext using public key | Transaction Validation | > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU board exams, the examiner expects you to write the canonical equations $C = E_K(P)$ and $P = D_K(C)$ at minimum. Failing to use proper subscripts or omitting the key notation costs easy marks. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Blockchain & Distributed Ledgers:** Transaction payloads (plaintext) are hashed into digests (ciphertext-like) and linked into Merkle trees for tamper detection. - **Banking & Financial Systems:** Account credentials, PINs, and transaction data are stored as ciphertext to protect against database breaches. - **Healthcare Systems:** Patient records (plaintext) are encrypted before cloud storage to comply with HIPAA/GDPR-style data protection norms. - **Internet of Things (IoT):** Sensor readings (plaintext) are encrypted before wireless transmission to prevent eavesdropping and spoofing attacks. - **Military & Defence Communications:** Strategic messages are end-to-end encrypted so that intercepted ciphertext reveals nothing about operational plans. - **Software Engineering:** Passwords are never stored as plaintext — they are hashed (one-way transformation) before being saved in authentication databases. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Fundamental Encryption-Decryption Round Trip The complete cycle of plaintext-to-ciphertext-to-plaintext is mathematically expressed as the composition of the decryption function $D$ applied to the output of the encryption function $E$. Let us derive the canonical round-trip identity step by step. ### Derivation 1: Round-Trip Identity for Symmetric Encryption We start with the encryption operation: $$C = E_K(P)$$ Now apply the decryption function $D_K$ to both sides: $$D_K(C) = D_K(E_K(P))$$ By the definition of decryption as the mathematical inverse of encryption under the same key: $$D_K(E_K(P)) = P$$ Substituting back: $$D_K(C) = P$$ $$\therefore \quad D_K(E_K(P)) = P \quad \text{for all } P \in \mathcal{M}$$ This is the **round-trip identity**. It states that decrypting an encrypted message must yield the original plaintext. ### Derivation 2: Substitution Cipher Mini-Derivation (Toy Example) Consider a simple **Caesar Cipher** (shift by 3) on the alphabet $\mathcal{A} = \{A, B, C, \ldots, Z\}$. Each letter is mapped to its position: $$P_{i} = \text{position of } i\text{-th plaintext letter}, \quad 0 \leq P_i \leq 25$$ The encryption function shifts each letter forward by 3 positions modulo 26: $$C_i = (P_i + 3) \mod 26$$ For example, plaintext letter **'H'** ($P_i = 7$): $$C_i = (7 + 3) \mod 26 = 10 \mod 26 = 10$$ Position 10 corresponds to the ciphertext letter **'K'**. Decryption reverses the shift: $$P_i = (C_i - 3) \mod 26$$ Verification: $$P_i = (10 - 3) \mod 26 = 7 \mod 26 = 7$$ Position 7 corresponds to the plaintext letter **'H'** ✓ — the original plaintext is recovered. ### Derivation 3: Hashing — A One-Way Plaintext-to-Ciphertext Transformation Hashing is unique because it is **not invertible**. It transforms plaintext of arbitrary length into a fixed-length ciphertext-like output: $$h = H(P)$$ For SHA-256: $$\vert P \vert \leq 2^{64} - 1 \text{ bits (input)}$$ $$\vert h \vert = 256 \text{ bits (output, fixed)}$$ The avalanche property ensures that flipping a single bit in $P$ changes approximately **50%** of the bits in $h$ on average — this is why ciphertext (or hash output) appears random. ### Derivation 4: Information-Theoretic Relationship If plaintext $P$ has entropy $H(P)$ and ciphertext $C$ has entropy $H(C)$, a perfectly secure encryption (one-time pad) satisfies: $$H(C \mid P) = H(C)$$ This means the ciphertext reveals no information about the plaintext — they are statistically independent. In practical systems: $$H(C) \geq H(P) - \epsilon$$ where $\epsilon$ is a small leakage term. KTU students are not expected to compute entropies numerically but should know the conceptual relationship. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Substitution Walkthrough **Problem:** Apply the Caesar cipher with shift $k = 5$ to the plaintext $P =$ "BLOCK". Decrypt the result to recover "BLOCK". **Step 1:** Convert each plaintext letter to its numeric position (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25). | Letter | B | L | O | C | K | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | $P_i$ | 1 | 11 | 14 | 2 | 10 | **Step 2:** Apply the encryption formula $C_i = (P_i + 5) \mod 26$. $$C_1 = (1 + 5) \mod 26 = 6 \rightarrow G$$ $$C_2 = (11 + 5) \mod 26 = 16 \rightarrow Q$$ $$C_3 = (14 + 5) \mod 26 = 19 \rightarrow T$$ $$C_4 = (2 + 5) \mod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H$$ $$C_5 = (10 + 5) \mod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P$$ **Ciphertext:** $C =$ "GQTHP" **Step 3:** Apply decryption $P_i = (C_i - 5) \mod 26$. $$P_1 = (6 - 5) \mod 26 = 1 \rightarrow B$$ $$P_2 = (16 - 5) \mod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L$$ $$P_3 = (19 - 5) \mod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O$$ $$P_4 = (7 - 5) \mod 26 = 2 \rightarrow C$$ $$P_5 = (15 - 5) \mod 26 = 10 \rightarrow K$$ **Recovered Plaintext:** $P =$ "BLOCK" ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Symmetric vs Asymmetric vs Hashing | Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | Hashing | |:--------|:--------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------:| | **Input** | Plaintext $P$ | Plaintext $P$ | Plaintext $P$ | | **Output** | Ciphertext $C$ | Ciphertext $C$ | Digest $h$ | | **Key Used** | Single shared secret key $K$ | Public key $K_{pub}$ for encryption | No key | | **Reversibility** | Reversible via $D_K$ | Reversible via private key $K_{priv}$ | One-way, irreversible | | **Output Length** | Same or proportional to input | Same or proportional to input | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256) | | **Equation** | $C = E_K(P)$ | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$ | $h = H(P)$ | | **Blockchain Use** | Wallet file encryption, secure channels | Transaction signatures, address generation | Block hashing, Merkle trees | | **Example Algorithm** | AES-256 | RSA, ECC (secp256k1) | SHA-256, Keccak-256 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Plaintext-to-Ciphertext Transformation Flow The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete life-cycle of a piece of data transitioning from plaintext to ciphertext and back. This block-level architecture is the canonical mental model every KTU student should internalise for Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Plaintext P
Original Data] --> B[Select Key K
from Key Space] B --> C[Encryption Function E] A --> C C --> D[Ciphertext C
Scrambled Output] D --> E[Insecure Channel
Network or Ledger] E --> F[Decryption Function D] F --> G[Recovered Plaintext P
Original Data Restored] H[Adversary / Eavesdropper] -.->|Observes only C
Cannot recover P| D I[Legitimate Receiver
Holds Key K] --> F ``` ## 4.2 Encryption-Decryption Round-Trip Architecture The following diagram presents the same flow with explicit functional decomposition, suitable for answering descriptive KTU board questions that ask for "explain with block diagram". ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SenderSide["Sender Domain"] P1[Plaintext Source P] --> E1[Encryption Algorithm E] K1[Secret Key K] --> E1 E1 --> C1[Ciphertext C] end C1 --> CHAN[Untrusted Transmission Channel] CHAN --> C2[Ciphertext Received C] subgraph ReceiverSide["Receiver Domain"] C2 --> D1[Decryption Algorithm D] K2[Secret Key K] --> D1 D1 --> P2[Recovered Plaintext P] end ``` ## 4.3 Blockchain Transaction Hashing Flow (Application Context) In a blockchain, plaintext transaction data is not encrypted in the traditional sense but is transformed into ciphertext-like hashes that anchor the integrity of every block. ```mermaid flowchart LR TX[Plaintext Transaction Data
Sender, Receiver, Amount] --> H[SHA-256 Hash Function] H --> DIG[256-bit Digest
Ciphertext-like Hash] DIG --> MRK[Merkle Tree Construction] MRK --> ROOT[Merkle Root
Stored in Block Header] ROOT --> BLK[Linked to Next Block
via Hash Pointer] ``` > [!NOTE] > In blockchain, the term "ciphertext" is used loosely. Strictly speaking, blockchain uses **cryptographic hash digests** (one-way, keyless transformations) rather than reversible ciphertexts. The conceptual category is identical: a plaintext input is transformed into an unintelligible output that protects data integrity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Define plaintext and ciphertext with one example each.** **Model Answer:** **Plaintext (P):** Plaintext is the original, human-readable, unencrypted form of a message or data before any cryptographic transformation is applied. It is the input to an encryption or hashing operation. *Example:* The string "Send 5 BTC to Alice" is a plaintext transaction message before it is hashed for inclusion in a blockchain block. **Ciphertext (C):** Ciphertext is the scrambled, unintelligible output produced when a cryptographic algorithm is applied to plaintext. Without the correct decryption key, the original plaintext cannot be recovered from ciphertext. *Example:* The SHA-256 hash of the string "Send 5 BTC to Alice" produces a 256-bit digest like `8f3b...c7a2`, which is the ciphertext-like output. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample] **Write the mathematical expressions for encryption and decryption. State the round-trip identity.** **Model Answer:** The encryption of plaintext $P$ under key $K$ producing ciphertext $C$ is expressed as: $$C = E_K(P)$$ The decryption of ciphertext $C$ under the same key $K$ recovering the plaintext is expressed as: $$P = D_K(C)$$ The **round-trip identity** states that decrypting an encrypted message must return the original plaintext: $$D_K(E_K(P)) = P \quad \text{for all } P \in \mathcal{M}$$ This identity holds for all valid plaintexts in the message space and is the foundational correctness property of any cryptographic system. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the concepts of plaintext and ciphertext with suitable real-world analogy. Differentiate between reversible encryption and one-way hashing with reference to blockchain applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a worked numerical example, demonstrate plaintext-to-ciphertext transformation using the Caesar cipher (shift by $k = 7$). Verify decryption recovers the original plaintext. State the round-trip identity. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Plaintext and Ciphertext Definitions:** Plaintext is the original, readable data. Ciphertext is the encrypted, unreadable form produced by a cryptographic algorithm. **Real-World Analogy:** Consider a sealed envelope containing a confidential exam answer sheet. - The **answer sheet** = Plaintext - The **sealed envelope** = Ciphertext - The **seal and signature** = Cryptographic key - The act of **sealing** = Encryption - The act of **opening by the examiner** = Decryption The postal worker transporting the envelope sees only the sealed cover and cannot read the answer — just as an adversary observing ciphertext cannot read the plaintext without the key. **Reversible Encryption vs One-Way Hashing:** | Aspect | Reversible Encryption | One-Way Hashing | |:-------|:---------------------:|:----------------:| | Operation | $C = E_K(P)$, $P = D_K(C)$ | $h = H(P)$ | | Key Required | Yes | No | | Output Length | Variable (depends on input) | Fixed (e.g., 256 bits) | | Recoverable? | Yes, with correct key | No, computationally infeasible | | Blockchain Use | Wallet file encryption | Block hashing, Merkle trees, transaction IDs | In **reversible encryption**, plaintext is recovered via decryption. In **one-way hashing**, the plaintext cannot be recovered from the digest — this property is what makes hashing suitable for password storage and block integrity verification in blockchain. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Plaintext:** Let $P =$ "CHAIN" and key $k = 7$. **Step 1 — Letter to Numeric Conversion:** | Letter | C | H | A | I | N | |:------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | $P_i$ | 2 | 7 | 0 | 8 | 13 | **Step 2 — Encryption:** Apply $C_i = (P_i + 7) \mod 26$. $$C_1 = (2 + 7) \mod 26 = 9 \rightarrow J$$ $$C_2 = (7 + 7) \mod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O$$ $$C_3 = (0 + 7) \mod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H$$ $$C_4 = (8 + 7) \mod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P$$ $$C_5 = (13 + 7) \mod 26 = 20 \rightarrow U$$ **Ciphertext:** $C =$ "JOHPU" **Step 3 — Decryption Verification:** Apply $P_i = (C_i - 7) \mod 26$. $$P_1 = (9 - 7) \mod 26 = 2 \rightarrow C$$ $$P_2 = (14 - 7) \mod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H$$ $$P_3 = (7 - 7) \mod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A$$ $$P_4 = (15 - 7) \mod 26 = 8 \rightarrow I$$ $$P_5 = (20 - 7) \mod 26 = 13 \rightarrow N$$ **Recovered Plaintext:** $P =$ "CHAIN" ✓ **Round-Trip Identity:** $$D_K(E_K(P)) = P$$ --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **(a)** Explain the role of plaintext and ciphertext in blockchain transactions. How does SHA-256 hashing transform plaintext transaction data into a fixed-length digest? Why is this transformation considered a one-way function? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, and hashing using a tabular matrix. For each, write the canonical mathematical equation and identify one blockchain use-case. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Role in Blockchain Transactions:** In a blockchain network, every transaction carries plaintext data fields — sender's address, recipient's address, amount, timestamp, and nonce. Before this plaintext is committed to a block, it is processed by a cryptographic hash function (typically **SHA-256** in Bitcoin). The plaintext transaction is also digitally signed using the sender's private key, producing a ciphertext-like signature that can be verified by anyone using the sender's public key. The block header itself contains the plaintext representation of the previous block's hash (a ciphertext-like digest), the Merkle root of all transactions (another digest), and a nonce. The entire block is then hashed again to produce the block's unique identifier. **SHA-256 Transformation:** SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm with 256-bit output) accepts plaintext of any length up to $2^{64} - 1$ bits and produces a fixed-length 256-bit digest: $$h = \text{SHA-256}(P), \quad \vert h \vert = 256 \text{ bits}$$ The transformation involves: 1. **Padding** the plaintext to a multiple of 512 bits. 2. **Parsing** into 512-bit message blocks. 3. **Iterative compression** using 64 rounds of bitwise operations, modular additions, and logical functions. 4. **Output** of a 256-bit final hash value. **One-Way Function Property:** SHA-256 is one-way because: - Computing $h$ from $P$ is fast and trivial. - Reversing — finding $P$ given only $h$ — requires brute-force search through $2^{256}$ possible plaintexts, which is computationally infeasible with current technology. - The internal compression functions are non-invertible by design (they use non-reversible operations like bitwise rotations and modular additions). #### Model Solution for Part (b): | Feature | Symmetric Encryption | Asymmetric Encryption | Hashing | |:--------|:--------------------:|:---------------------:|:-------:| | **Equation** | $C = E_K(P)$ | $C = E_{K_{pub}}(P)$ | $h = H(P)$ | | **Key** | Single shared secret | Key pair: public + private | No key | | **Reversible** | Yes, with same key | Yes, with private key | No, one-way | | **Output Length** | Same as input | Same as input | Fixed length | | **Speed** | Very fast | Slower due to math complexity | Fast | | **Blockchain Use-Case** | Encrypting wallet.dat files | Generating digital signatures for transactions | SHA-256 for block hashing and Merkle trees | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Plaintext and Ciphertext Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing hash output with ciphertext** — In blockchain, the hash digest is *not* a true ciphertext because it is not produced by an encryption algorithm with a key, and it cannot be decrypted. Examiners deduct marks if you call a SHA-256 digest "ciphertext" without qualifying the distinction. > 2. **Omitting the key from the equation** — Writing $C = E(P)$ instead of $C = E_K(P)$ loses at least 1 mark. The key is mandatory notation. > 3. **Writing $E(D(P)) = P$** — This is a notation trap. The correct order is $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$, i.e., the *outermost* function is decryption, not encryption. > 4. **Forgetting modulo arithmetic** — Caesar cipher derivations without $\mod 26$ are incomplete and will be penalised. > 5. **Not stating Kerckhoffs's Principle** — When asked about encryption security, mentioning that algorithm secrecy is not required (only key secrecy) earns a bonus mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Plaintext ($P$)** is the original, readable data; **Ciphertext ($C$)** is the encrypted, unreadable output. - The canonical encryption equation is **$C = E_K(P)$** and decryption is **$P = D_K(C)$**. - The **round-trip identity** $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ defines correctness of any cryptographic system. - In **symmetric encryption**, the same key encrypts and decrypts. - In **asymmetric encryption**, a public key encrypts and a private key decrypts (or vice versa for signatures). - **Hashing** is a **one-way** transformation: plaintext → fixed-length digest, with no key and no inversion. - **Blockchain** uses hashing extensively (SHA-256 for Bitcoin, Keccak-256 for Ethereum), but reversible encryption only in auxiliary layers like wallet encryption. - **Kerckhoffs's Principle:** Algorithm can be public; only the key must remain secret. - **Message space** $\mathcal{M}$ and **ciphertext space** $\mathcal{C}$ formally define the domains and ranges of the encryption function. - The Caesar cipher is the simplest teaching example: $C_i = (P_i + k) \mod 26$. - Always remember: **plaintext is the "before" state, ciphertext is the "after" state** of any cryptographic transformation.

topics.title: Substitution Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substitution Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Substitution Techniques** are classical symmetric-key encryption schemes in which the units of the plaintext (letters, digraphs, or blocks) are systematically replaced with other symbols, numbers, or letters according to a deterministic rule controlled by a secret key. The fundamental property is that the order of the plaintext symbols is preserved unchanged, but their identities are mapped (i.e., "substituted") using a mathematical function.

Formally, a substitution cipher is defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, E, D)$, where:

- $\mathcal{P}$ : Set of all possible plaintext symbols (e.g., $\{A, B, \dots, Z\}$)
- $\mathcal{C}$ : Set of all possible ciphertext symbols
- $\mathcal{K}$ : Set of all possible keys (the substitution rules)
- $E(k, p)$ : Encryption function mapping $\mathcal{P} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ using key $k$
- $D(k, c)$ : Decryption function mapping $\mathcal{C} \rightarrow \mathcal{P}$ using key $k$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Substitution Techniques form the foundational pillar of classical cryptography. Although modern block-chain systems (SHA-256, ECDSA, RIPEMD-160) rely on far more advanced mathematics, every symmetric key in a blockchain wallet is conceptually rooted in these substitution-permutation principles.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine two childhood friends, **Alice** and **Bob**, who invent a "secret language" by agreeing on a single rule:
*"Every letter of the English alphabet is shifted by **3 positions forward**. So A → D, B → E, C → F, and so on."*

When Alice wants to write **"HELLO"**, she substitutes each letter:
- H → K
- E → H
- L → O
- L → O
- O → R

Result: **"KHOOR"**. When Bob receives "KHOOR", he knows the secret rule and simply shifts each letter back by 3 to recover the original message. This is exactly how a **Caesar Cipher** works — the simplest substitution cipher in human history, reportedly used by **Julius Caesar** for military correspondence around 50 B.C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Distinction:** In *Substitution Ciphers*, plaintext letters are **replaced** (identity changes, position preserved). In *Transposition Ciphers* (covered later), letters are **rearranged** (position changes, identity preserved). Substitution Techniques are the focus of this module.

### 1.3 Classification of Substitution Techniques

| Category | Cipher Type | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Monoalphabetic** | Single fixed mapping for all letters | Caesar, Affine, Monoalphabetic |
| **Polyalphabetic** | Multiple mappings rotating by key | Vigenère, Beaufort, Autokey |
| **Digraphic / Polygraphic** | Substitution operates on letter pairs/blocks | Playfair, Hill Cipher |
| **Theoretically Unbreakable** | Key length = Message length, truly random | One-Time Pad (Vernam Cipher) |

The most important security metric is the **Key Space Size** — the total number of possible keys.

- Caesar Cipher: only **25** effective keys
- Monoalphabetic: **26!** $\approx 4.03 \times 10^{26}$ keys
- Vigenère (key length $n$): **$26^n$** keys
- One-Time Pad (key length = plaintext length $L$): **$26^L$** keys

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Caesar Cipher — Operational Logic
The Caesar Cipher is a monoalphabetic substitution where each plaintext letter $p$ is shifted by a fixed integer key $k$ (where $0 \le k \le 25$).

**Encryption (Step-by-Step):**
1. Convert each plaintext letter $p$ into its numerical equivalent $P \in \{0, 1, \dots, 25\}$ (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25).
2. Compute the ciphertext value $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$.
3. Convert $C$ back into a letter.

**Decryption:**
1. Convert each ciphertext letter $c$ to its numerical form $C$.
2. Compute the plaintext $P = (C - k) \bmod 26$.
3. Convert $P$ back to a letter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students incorrectly write $(P + k) \bmod 25$. The modulus is always **26** because the English alphabet has exactly **26** letters (modulo **26** arithmetic governs all classical ciphers).

### 2.2 Monoalphabetic Cipher — Generalized Mapping
Each plaintext letter $a \in \{0, 1, \dots, 25\}$ is mapped to a unique ciphertext letter $\pi(a)$ via a fixed permutation $\pi$. Since $\pi$ is a permutation, the inverse $\pi^{-1}$ uniquely decrypts.

The key space is $26! \approx 4.03 \times 10^{26}$ — enormous in size, but trivially broken using **frequency analysis** (in English, E, T, A, O are the most frequent letters).

### 2.3 Playfair Cipher — Digraphic Substitution
Invented in **1854 by Charles Wheatstone** but popularized by **Lord Playfair**, this cipher encrypts **letter pairs (digraphs)** using a $5 \times 5$ keyword matrix constructed as follows:

1. Write the keyword letters in a $5 \times 5$ grid (left to right, top to bottom), skipping duplicates.
2. Fill the remaining cells with the unused letters of the alphabet. Conventionally, **I and J share the same cell** (since 26 letters cannot fit into 25 cells).

**Encryption Rules for a Digraph $(p_1, p_2)$:**
- **Same Row:** Replace each letter with the one immediately to its **right** (wrapping around).
- **Same Column:** Replace each letter with the one immediately **below** (wrapping around).
- **Rectangle Rule:** Each letter is replaced by the letter in its **own row** but in the **column of the other letter** of the digraph.

If both letters of a digraph are identical, an **X** (or another filler like Q) is inserted between them. If the plaintext has odd length, a filler is appended at the end.

### 2.4 Hill Cipher — Polygraphic Matrix Cipher (1929)
The Hill Cipher, invented by **Lester S. Hill**, encrypts blocks of $m$ letters using linear algebra over $\mathbb{Z}_{26}$. The key is an $m \times m$ invertible matrix $K$.

**Encryption:**
$$C = K \cdot P \bmod 26$$

**Decryption:**
$$P = K^{-1} \cdot C \bmod 26$$

The matrix $K$ must be invertible modulo 26, which means $\gcd(\det(K), 26) = 1$. The inverse is computed modulo 26 using the **modular inverse of the determinant**.

### 2.5 Vigenère Cipher — Polyalphabetic Substitution (1553)
The Vigenère Cipher uses a keyword to generate a repeating key stream. Each plaintext letter is shifted by a different amount depending on the corresponding key letter.

**Encryption:** $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$
**Decryption:** $P_i = (C_i - K_i) \bmod 26$

where $K_i$ is the numerical value of the $i$-th key letter (cycling if needed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The Vigenère Cipher was long called *le chiffre indéchiffrable* ("the indecipherable cipher") and remained unbroken for nearly **300 years** until Friedrich Kasiski published his frequency-analysis attack in **1863**.

### 2.6 One-Time Pad (Vernam Cipher)
The One-Time Pad (OTP), patented by **Gilbert Vernam in 1919**, is the only known encryption scheme that is **information-theoretically secure** (proven by **Claude Shannon in 1949**).

**Conditions for Perfect Secrecy:**
1. The key must be **truly random**.
2. The key length must be **at least equal** to the plaintext length.
3. The key must be used **exactly once** and then discarded.

**Encryption:** $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$
**Decryption:** $P_i = (C_i - K_i) \bmod 26$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** If the key is reused (i.e., not truly "one-time"), the OTP collapses into a Vigenère Cipher and becomes vulnerable to Kasiski-style attacks.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Domain |
|---------|----------------|--------|
| Caesar Encryption | $C = (P + k) \bmod 26$ | $P, C \in \{0, \dots, 25\}$, $k \in \{1, \dots, 25\}$ |
| Caesar Decryption | $P = (C - k) \bmod 26$ | Modulo 26 |
| Affine Cipher | $C = (aP + b) \bmod 26$ | $\gcd(a, 26) = 1$ |
| Vigenère Cipher | $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$ | Key repeats cyclically |
| Hill Cipher | $C = K \cdot P \bmod 26$ | $K$ must be invertible mod 26 |
| Hill Decryption | $P = K^{-1} \cdot C \bmod 26$ | $\gcd(\det K, 26) = 1$ |
| One-Time Pad | $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$ | $K_i$ truly random, never reused |
| Modulo Property | $(a \bmod n) = a - n \lfloor a/n \rfloor$ | General integer arithmetic |
| Modular Inverse | $a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \bmod m$ | $\gcd(a, m) = 1$ |
| Playfair Rule | Same Row $\rightarrow$ Shift Right; Same Column $\rightarrow$ Shift Down; Else $\rightarrow$ Rectangle | $5 \times 5$ matrix, I/J merged |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

### 3.1 Caesar Cipher — Complete Numerical Walkthrough

**Problem:** Encrypt the plaintext **"ATTACK"** using Caesar Cipher with key $k = 7$, then decrypt to verify.

**Step 1: Convert plaintext letters to numerical values.**
$$A \rightarrow 0, \quad T \rightarrow 19, \quad T \rightarrow 19, \quad A \rightarrow 0, \quad C \rightarrow 2, \quad K \rightarrow 10$$

**Step 2: Apply encryption formula $C = (P + 7) \bmod 26$ letter by letter.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7 \bmod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H \\
C_2 &= (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 26 \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_3 &= (19 + 7) \bmod 26 = 26 \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_4 &= (0 + 7) \bmod 26 = 7 \bmod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H \\
C_5 &= (2 + 7) \bmod 26 = 9 \bmod 26 = 9 \rightarrow J \\
C_6 &= (10 + 7) \bmod 26 = 17 \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R
\end{aligned}$$

**Resulting Ciphertext:** **"HAAHJR"**

**Step 3: Decrypt using $P = (C - 7) \bmod 26$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= (7 - 7) \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
P_2 &= (0 - 7) \bmod 26 = -7 \bmod 26 = 19 \rightarrow T \\
P_3 &= (0 - 7) \bmod 26 = 19 \rightarrow T \\
P_4 &= (7 - 7) \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
P_5 &= (9 - 7) \bmod 26 = 2 \rightarrow C \\
P_6 &= (17 - 7) \bmod 26 = 10 \rightarrow K
\end{aligned}$$

**Recovered Plaintext:** **"ATTACK"** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $-7 \bmod 26 = 19$?** Because $-7 + 26 = 19$. In modular arithmetic, a negative result is always corrected by adding the modulus repeatedly until the value falls in the non-negative range $\{0, \dots, 25\}$.

---

### 3.2 Vigenère Cipher — Full Encryption & Decryption Walkthrough

**Problem:** Encrypt **"BLOCKCHAIN"** with key **"KEY"**. Then decrypt the ciphertext using the same key.

**Step 1: Convert plaintext and key into numerical values.**

Plaintext: B L O C K C H A I N → 1, 11, 14, 2, 10, 2, 7, 0, 8, 13

Key: K E Y K E Y K E Y K → 10, 4, 24, 10, 4, 24, 10, 4, 24, 10

(The key **"KEY"** repeats cyclically to match the length of the plaintext.)

**Step 2: Apply $C_i = (P_i + K_i) \bmod 26$ position-wise.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (1 + 10) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
C_2 &= (11 + 4) \bmod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P \\
C_3 &= (14 + 24) \bmod 26 = 38 \bmod 26 = 12 \rightarrow M \\
C_4 &= (2 + 10) \bmod 26 = 12 \rightarrow M \\
C_5 &= (10 + 4) \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O \\
C_6 &= (2 + 24) \bmod 26 = 26 \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_7 &= (7 + 10) \bmod 26 = 17 \rightarrow R \\
C_8 &= (0 + 4) \bmod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E \\
C_9 &= (8 + 24) \bmod 26 = 32 \bmod 26 = 6 \rightarrow G \\
C_{10} &= (13 + 10) \bmod 26 = 23 \rightarrow X
\end{aligned}$$

**Ciphertext:** **"LPMMOAREGX"**

**Step 3: Decrypt using $P_i = (C_i - K_i) \bmod 26$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= (11 - 10) \bmod 26 = 1 \rightarrow B \\
P_2 &= (15 - 4) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
P_3 &= (12 - 24) \bmod 26 = -12 \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O \\
P_4 &= (12 - 10) \bmod 26 = 2 \rightarrow C \\
P_5 &= (14 - 4) \bmod 26 = 10 \rightarrow K \\
P_6 &= (0 - 24) \bmod 26 = -24 \bmod 26 = 2 \rightarrow C \\
P_7 &= (17 - 10) \bmod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H \\
P_8 &= (4 - 4) \bmod 26 = 0 \rightarrow A \\
P_9 &= (6 - 24) \bmod 26 = -18 \bmod 26 = 8 \rightarrow I \\
P_{10} &= (23 - 10) \bmod 26 = 13 \rightarrow N
\end{aligned}$$

**Recovered Plaintext:** **"BLOCKCHAIN"** ✓

---

### 3.3 Hill Cipher — Complete $2 \times 2$ Example

**Problem:** Given key matrix $K = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$, encrypt plaintext block **"HI"** (i.e., $P = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}$).

**Step 1: Verify key invertibility.**

$$\det(K) = (3)(5) - (3)(2) = 15 - 6 = 9$$

Since $\gcd(9, 26) = 1$, the matrix is invertible modulo 26.

**Step 2: Compute encryption $C = K \cdot P \bmod 26$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (3)(7) + (3)(8) \\ (2)(7) + (5)(8) \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 21 + 24 \\ 14 + 40 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 45 \\ 54 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 45 - 26 \\ 54 - 52 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Ciphertext:** 19 → T, 2 → C → **"TC"**

**Step 3: Compute $K^{-1} \bmod 26$ for decryption.**

First find $9^{-1} \bmod 26$. Since $9 \times 3 = 27 \equiv 1 \pmod{26}$, we have $9^{-1} \equiv 3 \pmod{26}$.

The adjugate (classical adjoint) of $K$ is:

$$\text{adj}(K) = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now compute $K^{-1} = 9^{-1} \cdot \text{adj}(K) \bmod 26$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K^{-1} &= 3 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -3 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 15 & -9 \\ -6 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Verify decryption $P = K^{-1} \cdot C \bmod 26$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \begin{bmatrix} 15 & 17 \\ 20 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 19 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 285 + 34 \\ 380 + 18 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 319 \\ 398 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26 \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 319 - 12 \times 26 \\ 398 - 15 \times 26 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 319 - 312 \\ 398 - 390 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 7 \\ 8 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recovered:** 7 → H, 8 → I → **"HI"** ✓

---

### 3.4 Playfair Cipher — Full Digraphic Example

**Problem:** Construct the Playfair matrix for keyword **"MONARCHY"** and encrypt the digraph **"HE"**.

**Step 1: Build the $5 \times 5$ matrix.**

Place unique letters of MONARCHY first: M, O, N, A, R, C, H, Y (8 letters).
Fill remaining cells with unused letters B, D, E, F, G, I/J, K, L, P, Q, S, T, U, V, W, X, Z.

| | Col 0 | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 | Col 4 |
|--|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| **Row 0** | M | O | N | A | R |
| **Row 1** | C | H | Y | B | D |
| **Row 2** | E | F | G | I/J | K |
| **Row 3** | L | P | Q | S | T |
| **Row 4** | U | V | W | X | Z |

**Step 2: Locate the digraph "HE".**
- H is at row 1, column 1.
- E is at row 2, column 0.

H and E form a **rectangle** (different row, different column).

**Step 3: Apply the rectangle rule.**
- H (row 1) is replaced by the letter in **row 1, column of E (column 0)** → C.
- E (row 2) is replaced by the letter in **row 2, column of H (column 1)** → F.

**Encrypted Digraph:** **"CF"**

> [!NOTE]
> **Modular Cipher Scope:** In modern block-chain systems, SHA-256 performs a non-linear substitution using S-Boxes (Substitution Boxes). Conceptually, every S-Box is a giant monoalphabetic lookup table — a direct engineering descendant of the simple substitution techniques studied in this module.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generalized Substitution Encryption Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plaintext Message P] --> B[Convert Letters to Numbers P 0 to 25]
    B --> C{Apply Substitution Rule}
    C -->|Caesar Cipher| D1[C = P + k mod 26]
    C -->|Vigenere Cipher| D2[C_i = P_i + K_i mod 26]
    C -->|Hill Cipher| D3[C = K times P mod 26]
    C -->|Playfair Cipher| D4[Digraph Mapping via 5x5 Matrix]
    D1 --> E[Generate Ciphertext C]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Transmit via Insecure Channel]
    F --> G[Authorized Receiver]
    G --> H[Apply Inverse Substitution]
    H --> I[Recovered Plaintext P]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style E fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

### 4.2 Caesar Cipher Shift Mapping (Key k = 7 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext A] -->|Shift by 7| A1[Ciphertext H]
    B[Plaintext B] -->|Shift by 7| B1[Ciphertext I]
    C[Plaintext C] -->|Shift by 7| C1[Ciphertext J]
    D[Plaintext D] -->|Shift by 7| D1[Ciphertext K]
    E[Plaintext E] -->|Shift by 7| E1[Ciphertext L]
    F[Plaintext F] -->|Shift by 7| F1[Ciphertext M]
    G[Plaintext G] -->|Shift by 7| G1[Ciphertext N]
    H[Plaintext T] -->|Shift by 7| H1[Ciphertext A wraps around]
    I[Plaintext X] -->|Shift by 7| I1[Ciphertext E wraps around]

    style A1 fill:#fff9c4
    style B1 fill:#fff9c4
    style C1 fill:#fff9c4
    style D1 fill:#fff9c4
    style E1 fill:#fff9c4
    style F1 fill:#fff9c4
    style G1 fill:#fff9c4
    style H1 fill:#ffccbc
    style I1 fill:#ffccbc
```

### 4.3 Cryptographic Security Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Caesar Cipher - 25 Keys - Trivial Brute Force] --> L1
    L1[Monoalphabetic - 26 factorial Keys - Broken by Frequency Analysis] --> L2
    L2[Polyalphabetic Vigenere - Broken by Kasiski 1863] --> L3
    L3[Hill Cipher - Vulnerable if Key Matrix Known] --> L4
    L4[One Time Pad - Information Theoretically Secure if Key Random and Used Once] --> L5
    L5[Modern Block Ciphers AES and SHA 256 - Used in Block chain]

    style L0 fill:#ffcdd2
    style L1 fill:#ffe0b2
    style L2 fill:#fff9c4
    style L3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style L4 fill:#b3e5fc
    style L5 fill:#d1c4e9
```

### 4.4 Encryption-Decryption Functional Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Sender Alice
    participant Channel as Insecure Channel
    participant Bob as Receiver Bob

    Alice->>Alice: Step 1 - Choose Cipher and Key k
    Alice->>Alice: Step 2 - Convert Plaintext P to Numbers
    Alice->>Alice: Step 3 - Compute C using Encryption Formula
    Alice->>Channel: Step 4 - Transmit Ciphertext C
    Note over Channel: Attacker Eve sees only C
    Channel->>Bob: Step 5 - Deliver Ciphertext C
    Bob->>Bob: Step 6 - Compute P using Inverse Key
    Bob->>Bob: Step 7 - Convert Numbers back to Plaintext
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a substitution cipher. Differentiate between monoalphabetic and polyalphabetic substitution ciphers with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **substitution cipher** is a symmetric encryption method in which each element of the plaintext (letter, digraph, or block) is replaced by another element from the ciphertext alphabet according to a fixed rule, while the relative positions of the symbols remain unchanged. Formally, $C = E(P)$ where $E$ is a fixed mapping from plaintext alphabet to ciphertext alphabet.

| Feature | Monoalphabetic | Polyalphabetic |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Mapping Rule | Single fixed substitution for every letter | Multiple substitutions, chosen by key letter |
| Key Size | $26!$ permutation | $26^n$ for key length $n$ |
| Frequency Pattern | Preserved (vulnerable to frequency analysis) | Masked (harder to break) |
| Example | Caesar Cipher, Affine Cipher | Vigenère Cipher, Autokey Cipher |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Table comparison (2M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the three conditions that must be satisfied for a One-Time Pad (Vernam Cipher) to achieve perfect secrecy.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For a One-Time Pad to provide **information-theoretic security** (as proven by Claude Shannon), the following three conditions must hold:

1. **The key must be truly random** — generated by a cryptographically secure random number generator, not by any deterministic algorithm.
2. **The key length must be at least equal to the plaintext length** — each plaintext bit must be paired with a unique key bit.
3. **The key must be used exactly once and then securely discarded** — never reused for any other message.

If any of these conditions is violated, the OTP reduces to a Vigenère Cipher and becomes vulnerable to statistical attacks.

**Mark Distribution:** Each condition (1M).

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the Playfair Cipher with its encryption algorithm. Construct the Playfair cipher matrix using the keyword **"BALLOON"** and encrypt the plaintext **"HELLO"**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Describe the Hill Cipher encryption and decryption process. Given the key matrix $K = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, encrypt the plaintext letter pair **"AR"** (i.e., the numerical vector $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 17 \end{bmatrix}$). Show all modular arithmetic steps. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Playfair Cipher Explanation & "HELLO" Encryption (7 Marks)**

The **Playfair Cipher** (invented by Charles Wheatstone in 1854, popularized by Lord Playfair) is a digraphic substitution cipher that encrypts **pairs of letters** using a $5 \times 5$ matrix constructed from a keyword. It treats the alphabet as a 25-cell grid (with **I and J merged**).

**Step 1: Build the Playfair matrix from keyword "BALLOON"** (2 Marks)

- Unique letters of BALLOON: B, A, L, O, N (5 letters)
- Fill remaining cells with remaining alphabet letters in order:

| | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|--|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | B | A | L | O | N |
| **1** | H | C | D | E | F |
| **2** | G | I/J | K | M | P |
| **3** | Q | R | S | T | U |
| **4** | V | W | X | Y | Z |

**Step 2: Split plaintext "HELLO" into digraphs** (1 Mark)

Since "HELLO" has 5 letters, we split as: **HE | LL | OX** (an X filler is inserted between the two L's).

**Step 3: Encrypt each digraph** (4 Marks)

- **HE:** H is at row 1, col 0; E is at row 1, col 3. They share the same **row**, so shift each **right by one**: H → C, E → F. Ciphertext: **CF**
- **LL:** L is at row 0, col 2 (appears twice). Insert filler X → **LX | LO**.
  - **LX:** L at (0,2), X at (4,2) → same **column**, shift each **down by one**: L → G, X → Z. Ciphertext: **GZ**
  - **LO:** L at (0,2), O at (0,3) → same **row**, shift each **right by one**: L → O, O → N. Ciphertext: **ON**
- **OX:** O at (0,3), X at (4,2) → rectangle rule. O → Y (row 0, col 4), X → L (row 4, col 3). Ciphertext: **YL**

**Final Ciphertext:** **CF GZ ON YL** → **"CFGZONYL"**

---

**(b) Hill Cipher Encryption of "AR" (7 Marks)**

The **Hill Cipher** (Lester S. Hill, 1929) encrypts blocks of $m$ letters using matrix multiplication modulo 26: $C = K \cdot P \bmod 26$. The key matrix $K$ must be invertible modulo 26.

**Step 1: Verify invertibility** (1 Mark)

$$\det(K) = (5)(2) - (8)(1) = 10 - 8 = 2$$

Since $\gcd(2, 26) = 2 \neq 1$, the matrix $K$ is **NOT invertible modulo 26**. Therefore, decryption is impossible with this key.

**Step 2: Proceed with encryption regardless** (Demonstration – 4 Marks)

$$C = K \cdot P \bmod 26 = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 8 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 17 \end{bmatrix} \bmod 26$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 &= (5)(0) + (8)(17) = 0 + 136 = 136 \bmod 26 \\
C_1 &= 136 - 5 \times 26 = 136 - 130 = 6 \rightarrow G \\
