$$\text{Tx per block} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \times 10^{6} \;\text{bytes}}{250 \;\text{bytes/tx}} \;=\; 8{,}000 \;\text{tx/block}$$

$$\text{TPS}_{\text{blockchain}} \;=\; \dfrac{8{,}000 \;\text{tx}}{15 \;\text{s}} \;\approx\; 533.3 \;\text{TPS}$$

**Solution — Step 2 (Shared DB TPS).**

$$\text{TPS}_{\text{shared\_db}} \;=\; 5{,}000 \;\text{TPS}$$

**Solution — Step 3 (Ratio).**

$$\dfrac{\text{TPS}_{\text{shared\_db}}}{\text{TPS}_{\text{blockchain}}} \;=\; \dfrac{5{,}000}{533.3} \;\approx\; 9.375$$

**Conclusion:** The shared database is approximately **9.4× faster** in raw throughput, but does not provide Byzantine fault tolerance or decentralised trust. The student must always comment on this trade-off in the conclusion — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if the conclusion is missing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `blockA`, `dbMaster`) and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to guarantee correct rendering.

### 4.1 Diagram A — Blockchain (Peer-to-Peer) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[NODE A\nFULL COPY]:::peer
    node2[NODE B\nFULL COPY]:::peer
    node3[NODE C\nFULL COPY]:::peer
    node4[NODE D\nFULL COPY]:::peer
    node5[NODE E\nFULL COPY]:::peer

    blockA[BLOCK 0\nGENESIS]:::genesis
    blockB[BLOCK 1]:::block
    blockC[BLOCK 2]:::block
    blockD[BLOCK 3]:::block

    node1 --- blockA
    node2 --- blockA
    node3 --- blockA
    node4 --- blockA
    node5 --- blockA

    blockA -->|HASH LINK| blockB
    blockB -->|HASH LINK| blockC
    blockC -->|HASH LINK| blockD

    node1 <-->|GOSSIP| node2
    node2 <-->|GOSSIP| node3
    node3 <-->|GOSSIP| node4
    node4 <-->|GOSSIP| node5
    node5 <-->|GOSSIP| node1

    classDef peer fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef genesis fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef block fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Shared Database (Master–Replica) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[CLIENT APP 1]:::client
    client2[CLIENT APP 2]:::client
    client3[CLIENT APP 3]:::client

    dbMaster[(PRIMARY MASTER\nWRITES + READS)]:::master
    dbReplica1[(REPLICA 1\nREAD ONLY)]:::replica
    dbReplica2[(REPLICA 2\nREAD ONLY)]:::replica
    dbReplica3[(REPLICA 3\nREAD ONLY)]:::replica

    dbAdmin[DATABASE ADMIN\nDBA + ACL]:::admin

    client1 -->|SQL WRITE| dbMaster
    client2 -->|SQL READ| dbReplica1
    client3 -->|SQL READ| dbReplica2

    dbMaster -->|BINLOG SHIP| dbReplica1
    dbMaster -->|BINLOG SHIP| dbReplica2
    dbMaster -->|BINLOG SHIP| dbReplica3

    dbReplica1 -->|READ REPLICA| client1
    dbReplica2 -->|READ REPLICA| client3

    dbAdmin -->|SCHEMA DDL\nACL CONFIG| dbMaster
    dbAdmin -->|MONITORING| dbReplica1
    dbAdmin -->|MONITORING| dbReplica2
    dbAdmin -->|MONITORING| dbReplica3

    classDef client fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef master fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef replica fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef admin fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Failure & Trust Domain Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[SYSTEM EVENT]:::event
    q1{IS THE PARTICIPANT\nKNOWN AND TRUSTED?}:::qst

    path1[SHARED DATABASE PATH]:::path
    path2[BLOCKCHAIN PATH]:::path

    box1[TRUST BASED\nADMIN AUTHORISES]\n[CFT 2F+1]:::detail
    box2[TRUST MINIMISED\nCONSENSUS ENFORCES]\n[BFT 3F+1]:::detail

    res1[OUTCOME:\nMUTABLE + FAST + CHEAP]:::out
    res2[OUTCOME:\nIMMUTABLE + SLOW + DECENTRALISED]:::out

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|YES| path1
    q1 -->|NO| path2

    path1 --> box1 --> res1
    path2 --> box2 --> res2

    classDef event fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef qst fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef path fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detail fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Hash Chain Tamper-Evidence (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    txPool[TX POOL\nUNCONFIRMED TXS]:::pool
    block0[BLOCK N-1]:::blk
    block1[BLOCK N]:::blk
    block2[BLOCK N+1]:::blk
    merkle[MERKLE TREE\nROOT HASH]:::merkle
    pow[POW PUZZLE\nNONCE SEARCH]:::pow
    tamper[ATTACKER\nMODIFIES BLOCK N-1]:::bad
    cascade[CASCADE FAILURE\nBLOCKS N N+1 N+2...]:::bad

    block0 -->|PREV HASH| block1
    block1 -->|PREV HASH| block2
    txPool --> merkle --> block1
    block1 --> pow

    tamper -->|BIT FLIP| block0
    block0 -.->|AVALANCHE| cascade

    classDef pool fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef blk fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef merkle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pow fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECCT412]**
> *Define a shared database. List any two architectural differences between a shared database and a blockchain.*

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

A **shared database** is a *centrally administered, replicated data store* spread across multiple nodes that coordinate via a trusted primary-replica protocol. Two architectural differences:
1. **Topology:** Shared database follows a *master–replica* hierarchy with a privileged primary; blockchain uses a *flat peer-to-peer* topology with no privileged node.
2. **Mutability:** Shared database supports *full CRUD* (Insert, Update, Delete) operations; blockchain is *append-only* — only new transactions can be added, and old data cannot be edited or deleted.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECCT412]**
> *What is the Byzantine Fault Tolerance threshold, and why does a typical shared database not achieve it?*

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

The **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) threshold** states that a system can tolerate up to $f$ arbitrarily malicious (Byzantine) nodes provided the total node count satisfies $n \geq 3f + 1$. A typical shared database does *not* achieve this because it relies on a *trusted primary* and assumes *crash-only* failures. Its consensus protocols (Paxos, Raft, 2PC) tolerate only *f < n/2* crash faults and fail catastrophically if a node begins sending *inconsistent / malicious* messages — which is precisely the threat model BFT was designed to defeat.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for confusing *consensus* (agreement on order) with *fault tolerance* (ability to keep operating under failure).
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for stating *"blockchain is faster than databases"* — it is the opposite.
> - Students **lose 2 marks** for failing to mention the **CAP theorem** in the conclusion.
> - Students **lose 3 marks** for omitting the *engineering decision* of *when to use which*.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q3a. [7 Marks]** *Explain the architecture of a blockchain. With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a block, the hash-linking mechanism, and the role of the Merkle tree.*

**Model Answer:**

**A1. Architecture of Blockchain (≈ 200 words):**

A blockchain is a *distributed, append-only ledger* replicated across a peer-to-peer network of nodes. Every node holds a *full or pruned* copy of the ledger. New transactions are broadcast via a **gossip protocol**, gathered into a **transaction pool (mempool)**, and bundled into **blocks** by special nodes (miners in PoW, validators in PoS).

**A2. Block Structure:**

Each block consists of two parts:
- **Block Header** (≈ 80 bytes): contains `version`, `previous_block_hash`, `merkle_root`, `timestamp`, `difficulty_target`, and `nonce`.
- **Block Body:** contains a list of transactions $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k$ organised as a **Merkle tree**.

**A3. Hash-Linking Mechanism:**

The block header of block $B_i$ includes the SHA-256 hash of $B_{i-1}$'s header as the `previous_block_hash` field. Formally:

$$H_i \;=\; \text{SHA256}\big( \text{prev\_hash}_{i-1} \;\Vert\; \text{merkle\_root}_{i} \;\Vert\; \text{timestamp}_{i} \;\Vert\; \text{nonce}_{i} \big)$$

This creates a **tamper-evident chain** — modifying any historical block invalidates all subsequent blocks.

**A4. Merkle Tree Role:**

A Merkle tree recursively hashes pairs of transaction hashes until a single **Merkle root** is obtained, which is stored in the block header. This allows **$O(\log n)$ Simple Payment Verification (SPV)**: a light client can prove a transaction is included by presenting a *Merkle path* of $\log_2 n$ hashes, without downloading the entire block.

**[Diagram — refer to Section 4.1, Diagram A]**

> **Q3b. [7 Marks]** *Compare blockchain with a traditional shared database along the dimensions of (i) control and trust model, (ii) data mutability, (iii) consensus and fault tolerance. Conclude with a CAP-theorem-based justification.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Control and Trust Model:** Blockchain is **decentralised** with no single administrator; participants are mutually distrusting strangers, and trust is replaced by cryptography + economic incentives. Shared database is **centralised** with a DBA controlling schema, access control lists, and replication policy; participants are pre-authenticated and trust the DBMS as the trusted computing base.

**(ii) Data Mutability:** Blockchain is **append-only / immutable** — old data cannot be edited or deleted; errors are corrected by appending a compensating transaction. Shared database is **fully mutable** with full SQL CRUD support; rows can be inserted, updated, deleted, or truncated at any time.

**(iii) Consensus and Fault Tolerance:** Blockchain uses **BFT consensus** (PoW, PoS, PBFT, Tendermint) tolerating $f < n/3$ arbitrary malicious failures. Shared database uses **CFT consensus** (Paxos, Raft, 2PC) tolerating only $f < n/2$ crash failures, with no defence against malicious behaviour.

**CAP-Theorem Justification:** Public blockchains (e.g., Bitcoin) choose **AP** — *Availability + Partition-tolerance*, sacrificing strong consistency (forks are resolved probabilistically). Permissioned blockchains and traditional distributed databases typically choose **CP** — *Consistency + Partition-tolerance*, sacrificing availability during network partitions. Hence the choice is fundamentally a CAP trade-off, not a technological superiority of one over the other.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

> **Q4a. [7 Marks]** *Describe the architecture of a shared (distributed) database with a suitable diagram. Explain the role of the primary node, replication, and the database administrator.*

**Model Answer:**

**B1. Definition (≈ 60 words):** A *shared / distributed database* is a logically unified database whose data is partitioned and replicated across multiple physical nodes connected by a network. It provides a single-system image to applications while internally coordinating writes through a designated **primary** node.

**B2. Primary Node Role:** All write operations (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) are routed to the **primary (master)** node. The primary serialises writes into a **write-ahead log (WAL)** or **binary log (binlog)**, then propagates these changes to secondaries. This *single-writer* model simplifies consistency at the cost of a **single point of failure** (mitigated by HA clustering using heartbeat protocols like Pacemaker/Corosync).

**B3. Replication Strategies:**
- **Synchronous replication:** Primary waits for all replicas to acknowledge before committing. Strong consistency, higher latency.
- **Asynchronous replication:** Primary commits locally, then ships log asynchronously. Lower latency, risk of data loss.
- **Semi-synchronous:** At least one replica acknowledges synchronously.
- **Quorum-based (Dynamo/Cassandra style):** $R + W > N$ tunable consistency.

**B4. Database Administrator (DBA) Role:** The DBA owns schema definition (DDL), access control (GRANT/REVOKE), backup/recovery, performance tuning, index management, and partitioning strategy. The DBA is the *single point of human trust* — a stark contrast to blockchain's algorithmic trust.

**[Diagram — refer to Section 4.2, Diagram B]**

> **Q4b. [7 Marks]** *Identify and justify five engineering scenarios where blockchain is preferable to a shared database, and three scenarios where a shared database is preferable. Use a decision matrix.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Scenario | Preferred System | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cross-border remittance (e.g., Ripple) | **Blockchain** | Multiple untrusted financial institutions; no single trusted intermediary; 24/7 settlement. |
| 2 | Pharmaceutical supply-chain provenance | **Blockchain** | Counterfeit prevention; immutable audit trail; multi-party (manufacturer, distributor, pharmacy, regulator) without central authority. |
| 3 | Land registry / property title | **Blockchain** | Eliminates title fraud; permanent public record; tamper-evident. |
| 4 | Decentralised identity (DID) | **Blockchain** | User-controlled identity without a central identity provider. |
| 5 | DeFi / tokenised assets | **Blockchain** | Programmable money; trustless lending/exchange; censorship resistance. |
| 6 | E-commerce OLTP (e.g., Amazon orders) | **Shared DB** | High TPS (10,000+), low latency, full CRUD, GDPR compliance (right-to-erasure). |
| 7 | Banking core ledger (in-house) | **Shared DB** | Trusted internal parties, regulatory requirements for data correction. |
| 8 | Hospital patient records (single hospital) | **Shared DB** | Single administrative authority; HIPAA / DPDP Act compliance; high availability. |

**Engineering Decision Principle (concluding statement):** Use **blockchain** when the system spans *multiple untrusted administrative domains* and requires *provable immutability and auditability*; use a **shared database** when the system is contained within *one trusted administrative domain* and demands *high throughput, low latency, and full mutability* (including regulatory right-to-erasure).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorise Before Exam):**

- **Definition Triad:** Blockchain = *Distributed* + *Immutable* + *Trust-minimised* ledger. Shared DB = *Replicated* + *Mutable* + *Trust-based* data store.
- **Five Pillars of Comparison:** *Architecture | Trust Model | Mutability | Consensus | Performance*.
- **BFT threshold:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ — tolerates arbitrary malicious behaviour.
- **CFT threshold:** $n \geq 2f + 1$ — tolerates only crash failures.
- **Hash chain equation:** $H_i = \text{SHA256}(H_{i-1} \Vert \text{MerkleRoot}_i \Vert \text{nonce})$.
- **Throughput order:** Bitcoin (~7 TPS) < Ethereum (~15–30 TPS) ≪ Visa centralised DB (~65,000 TPS).
- **Finality types:** *Probabilistic* (PoW, after $z$ confirmations) vs *Deterministic* (PBFT, Tendermint).
- **CAP theorem application:** Public blockchain → **AP**; Permissioned blockchain / distributed DB → **CP**.
- **GDPR Conflict:** Blockchain's immutability clashes with Article 17 right-to-erasure; prefer shared DB for personal data unless off-chain storage + on-chain hash is used.
- **Consensus Algorithms to remember:** *PoW* (Nakamoto), *PoS* (Ethereum 2.0), *PBFT* (Hyperledger Fabric), *Raft* (etcd, CockroachDB), *Paxos* (Google Spanner), *2PC* (cross-shard).
- **Merkle tree benefit:** $O(\log n)$ membership proof (SPV).
- **Decision rule:** *Trusted + single admin + high TPS* → Shared DB. *Untrusted + multi-party + audit trail* → Blockchain.
- **Common 2-mark terms:** "gossip protocol", "mempool", "fork", "non-fungible token", "smart contract", "ACID", "BASE", "write-ahead log", "sharding".
- **Examiner's favourite trick question:** *"Can blockchain scale?"* — Answer: Layer-1 (sharding, bigger blocks) and Layer-2 (rollups, state channels, Lightning Network) solutions are active research areas; pure Layer-1 throughput remains limited.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Real Need for Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Real Need for Mining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In blockchain technology, **Mining** is the process by which specialized nodes (miners) compete to validate transactions, bundle them into a new block, append that block to the distributed ledger, and propagate it across the peer-to-peer network. Mining is the operational backbone of **Proof of Work (PoW)** consensus mechanisms and serves three simultaneous, indispensable purposes:

1. **Decentralized Consensus** — Provides agreement among mutually distrusting nodes on the canonical state of the ledger.
2. **Security Enforcement** — Makes tampering with historical blocks computationally and economically infeasible.
3. **Controlled Currency Issuance** — Acts as the algorithmic minting process that introduces new coins into the system at a predictable rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "real need" for mining is *not* just earning rewards. It is the elegant solution to the **Byzantine Generals Problem** in an open, permissionless, trustless environment where no central authority exists.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Loudest Town Crier

Imagine a medieval village where hundreds of villagers trade goods, but no one trusts the village scribe. Suddenly, a brilliant rule is invented:

> *"Whoever shouts the loudest (solves the hardest puzzle) wins the right to record the next page of the ledger, and everyone accepts their version — BUT, the loudest shouter must prove their shout by solving a ridiculously difficult riddle every time."*

This is exactly what mining is:

| Real World Analogy | Blockchain Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Village ledger | Distributed Blockchain |
| Loudest shouter | Winning Miner Node |
| Difficult riddle | Proof of Work Hash Puzzle |
| Page in the ledger | A New Block |
| Village gossip network | Peer-to-Peer Propagation |
| Reward (gold coins) | Block Reward + Transaction Fees |

## 1.3 Core Entities Involved

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Actors in Mining:**
> - **Miner** — A node running mining software (e.g., ASIC hardware, GPU rigs).
> - **Candidate Block** — A block containing unconfirmed transactions waiting for validation.
> - **Nonce** — A random 32-bit number that miners iterate over to find a valid hash.
> - **Target / Difficulty** — A threshold value the block hash must fall below.
> - **Hash Function** — SHA-256 in Bitcoin's case (produces a fixed 256-bit output).

## 1.4 Why "Real Need"?

The phrase emphasizes that mining's necessity goes beyond monetary rewards. Without mining:

- ❌ No way to reach consensus without a trusted third party.
- ❌ No way to issue new currency fairly.
- ❌ No way to prevent double-spending attacks.
- ❌ No Sybil resistance (one entity creating millions of fake identities).

Thus, mining is the **thermodynamic commitment** a miner makes to the network — converting electricity and computation into immutable truth.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Mining Necessity

### Pillar 1 — Consensus (Solving the Byzantine Generals Problem)

In a decentralized network, all honest nodes must agree on the same history. Mining achieves this via the **Longest Chain Rule (Nakamoto Consensus)**:

> *Nodes always consider the longest valid chain (i.e., the one with the most accumulated Proof of Work) as the truthful chain.*

### Pillar 2 — Security Through Computational Cost

For an attacker to alter a block $b$ buried $n$ blocks deep, they must:
1. Re-mine block $b$.
2. Re-mine every subsequent block faster than the honest network.
3. Outpace the entire global hash rate.

The cumulative Proof of Work required grows **exponentially** with depth.

### Pillar 3 — Monetary Policy Without Central Banks

The block reward acts as a **pre-programmed, predictable monetary supply schedule**, e.g., Bitcoin's cap of **21,000,000 BTC**.

## 2.2 Mining Operational Workflow

1. **Transaction Pool (Mempool)**: Miners collect pending, unconfirmed transactions.
2. **Candidate Block Construction**: Verified transactions are assembled, a Merkle root is computed, and the block header is partially formed.
3. **Proof of Work Puzzle**: Miners iterate over the `nonce` field, computing:
$$H = \text{SHA-256}\big(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Block Header})\big)$$
4. **Difficulty Check**: The miner checks if $H < T$, where $T$ is the current **target threshold**.
5. **Block Broadcast**: Upon success, the block is broadcast. Other nodes verify and accept it.
6. **Reward Issuance**: The miner receives:
   - **Block Subsidy** (newly minted coins)
   - **Transaction Fees** (sum of fees from included transactions)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Block Hash | $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}))$ | 256-bit output |
| 2 | Difficulty | $D = \dfrac{T_{\max}}{T_{\text{current}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Target Threshold | $T_{\text{current}} = \dfrac{T_{\max}}{D}$ | 256-bit value |
| 4 | Expected Hashes per Block | $E[H] = D \cdot 2^{32}$ | Hash attempts |
| 5 | Block Reward (Halving) | $R_n = R_0 \cdot 2^{-n}$ | BTC |
| 6 | Total Supply Limit | $S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=0}^{N} R_n$ | Approaching 21M BTC |
| 7 | Hash Rate to Time | $T_{\text{block}} = \dfrac{E[H]}{\text{HashRate}_{\text{network}}}$ | Seconds |
| 8 | Mining Probability | $P_{\text{find}} = \dfrac{\text{HashRate}_{\text{miner}}}{\text{HashRate}_{\text{network}}}$ | Per attempt |
| 9 | Attack Cost (51% Block Rewind) | $C_{\text{attack}} \propto \sum_{i=0}^{n} D \cdot 2^{32} \cdot c_e$ | Electricity × time |
| 10 | Cumulative Difficulty at Depth $n$ | $D_{\text{cum}} = \sum_{i=0}^{n} D_i$ | Determines chain weight |

> [!NOTE]
> In Formula 9, $c_e$ is the **marginal cost of energy per hash** (in $/hash). This is why KTU examiners often link mining economics to energy consumption debates.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Beyond Cryptocurrency

- **Decentralized Identity**: Mining-style PoW can secure DID (Decentralized Identifier) networks.
- **Supply Chain Provenance**: Energy-as-commitment defends against counterfeit record injection.
- **Time-Stamping Authority**: Mining creates a publicly verifiable temporal ordering of events.
- **Anti-Spam Mechanisms**: Hashcash (precursor to Bitcoin mining) used in email systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Number of Hashes to Find a Valid Block

The mining puzzle requires the resulting hash to be a 256-bit value less than target $T$. Since hash outputs are uniformly distributed, the probability of any single hash being valid is:

$$p = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

Each hash attempt is an independent Bernoulli trial. The number of trials $X$ needed follows a **Geometric Distribution**:

$$P(X = k) = (1 - p)^{k-1} \cdot p$$

The expected number of hashes is therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{2^{256}}{T}$$

Substituting the difficulty definition $D = T_{\max} / T_{\text{current}}$ where $T_{\max} = 2^{224}$ for Bitcoin:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{2^{256} \cdot D}{2^{224}} = D \cdot 2^{32}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}[X] = D \cdot 2^{32} \text{ hash attempts per block}}$$

> **Interpretation:** A difficulty $D = 1$ requires ~$4.29$ billion hashes; $D = 90{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ (Bitcoin's typical value) requires $\sim 3.86 \times 10^{23}$ hashes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Block Reward Halving Schedule

Bitcoin's protocol enforces a halving event every **210,000 blocks** (≈ 4 years):

$$\boxed{R_n = R_0 \cdot 2^{-n}}$$

where:
- $R_0 = 50$ BTC (genesis reward, 2009)
- $n$ = number of halvings elapsed
- $R_n$ = reward at the $n$-th era

**Step-by-step evaluation:**

| Era ($n$) | Block Height | Reward $R_n$ (BTC) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 – 209,999 | $50 \cdot 2^{0} = 50$ |
| 1 | 210,000 – 419,999 | $50 \cdot 2^{-1} = 25$ |
| 2 | 420,000 – 629,999 | $50 \cdot 2^{-2} = 12.5$ |
| 3 | 630,000 – 839,999 | $50 \cdot 2^{-3} = 6.25$ |
| 4 | 840,000 – 1,049,999 | $50 \cdot 2^{-4} = 3.125$ |

The total supply is computed as a geometric series:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot R_0 \cdot 2^{-n} = 210{,}000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of an Honest Miner Finding the Next Block

Let $H_m$ = hash rate of miner $m$, and $H_{\text{net}}$ = total network hash rate.

$$\boxed{P_m = \frac{H_m}{H_{\text{net}}}}$$

**Verification using Poisson approximation:** If blocks arrive at rate $\lambda = H_{\text{net}} / \mathbb{E}[X]$ blocks per second, then the probability miner $m$ finds a block within time $t$ is:

$$P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda \cdot P_m \cdot t} = 1 - e^{-\frac{H_m}{\mathbb{E}[X]} \cdot t}$$

At $t = 600$ s (Bitcoin's target block interval):

$$P_{\text{at } t=600} = 1 - e^{-P_m \cdot 10} \approx 1 - e^{-10 P_m}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Honest vs Attacker Mining

| Parameter | Honest Mining | 51% Attack Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| Hash Rate Required | Any fraction | $> 50\%$ of $H_{\text{net}}$ |
| Expected Time per Block | $10$ min (Bitcoin) | Re-mines at attacker speed |
| Cost of $n$-Block Rewind | Proportional to $n$ | Exponential in $n$ |
| Economic Verdict | Profitable (block reward) | Mostly unprofitable (>$1B cost) |
| Network Effect | Strengthens chain | Destroys trust, price crashes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The real genius of mining is that honest behavior is the **Nash Equilibrium** of the game. Attacking is rational only if the attack reward exceeds energy costs — but the destruction of trust collapses the cryptocurrency's market value, making the attack self-defeating.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Energy-to-Trust Conversion Ratio

Let $E_{\text{block}}$ be the energy spent per block. The thermodynamic cost of rewriting a block buried $n$ blocks deep is:

$$C_{\text{rewrite}}(n) = n \cdot E_{\text{block}} \cdot c_e$$

where $c_e$ is the marginal electricity cost per unit energy. For Bitcoin (~$150$ TWh/year, ~144 blocks/day):

$$E_{\text{block}} \approx \frac{150 \times 10^{12} \text{ Wh}}{365 \cdot 144} \approx 2.85 \text{ GWh/block}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{rewrite}}(n) \approx 2.85n \text{ GWh} \cdot c_e}$$

This is why a **6-block confirmation** typically requires attackers to expend energy equivalent to a small city's daily consumption — economically prohibitive.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mining Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Miner Node Receives Transactions] --> B[Validate TX Signatures and Balances]
    B --> C[Assemble Candidate Block]
    C --> D[Compute Merkle Root]
    D --> E[Construct Block Header]
    E --> F[Set Nonce to Zero]
    F --> G[Compute SHA-256 Double Hash]
    G --> H{Hash Less Than Target T?}
    H -- No --> I[Increment Nonce]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Valid Proof of Work Found]
    J --> K[Broadcast New Block to Peers]
    K --> L[Other Nodes Verify and Accept]
    L --> M[Miner Receives Block Reward and Fees]
    M --> N[Network Difficulty Recalculated Every 2016 Blocks]
```

## 4.2 Why Mining is Needed — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph problemA[Problem 1: Consensus Without Trust]
        P1A[Mutually Distrusting Nodes] --> P1B[Need Common Truth]
        P1B --> P1C[Solved By Longest Chain Rule]
    end

    subgraph problemB[Problem 2: Sybil Resistance]
        P2A[Anyone Can Join Network] --> P2B[Need Cost Per Identity]
        P2B --> P2C[Solved By Proof of Work Cost]
    end

    subgraph problemC[Problem 3: Fair Currency Issuance]
        P3A[No Central Bank] --> P3B[Need Predictable Supply]
        P3B --> P3C[Solved By Halving Schedule Reward]
    end

    subgraph problemD[Problem 4: Double Spend Prevention]
        P4A[Same Coin Can Be Spent Twice] --> P4B[Need Time Ordering]
        P4B --> P4C[Solved By Block Confirmation Depth]
    end

    MINE[MINING Provides Solutions to All Four]
    P1C --> MINE
    P2C --> MINE
    P3C --> MINE
    P4C --> MINE
```

## 4.3 Honest Miner vs Attacker Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Block to Mine] --> Choice{Choose Strategy}
    Choice -- Honest --> H1[Work on Current Chain Tip]
    H1 --> H2[Follow Protocol Rules]
    H2 --> H3[Receive Block Reward]
    H3 --> H4[Profit Stable and Network Healthy]

    Choice -- Malicious --> A1[Secretly Mine on Fork]
    A1 --> A2[Require 51 percent Hash Rate]
    A2 --> A3{Catch Up With Honest Chain?}
    A3 -- No --> A4[Energy Wasted, No Reward]
    A3 -- Yes --> A5[Broadcast Rewritten Chain]
    A5 --> A6[Market Crash and Value Loss]
    A6 --> A7[Attack Becomes Self Defeating]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Why is mining essential in a permissionless blockchain like Bitcoin?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Mining is essential because it simultaneously provides three critical functions in a trustless environment:
1. **Consensus Mechanism (1 mark)** — Enables distributed agreement on the ledger state via the longest-chain rule, solving the Byzantine Generals Problem.
2. **Security Through Cost (1 mark)** — Makes rewriting history computationally and economically prohibitive by requiring real-world energy expenditure.
3. **Controlled Currency Issuance (1 mark)** — Acts as the only mechanism to introduce new coins into the system in a predictable, algorithmically fixed schedule.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is the role of the nonce in Bitcoin mining?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The **nonce (number used once)** is a 32-bit field in the block header that miners increment iteratively (1 mark).
2. Miners keep changing the nonce and re-hashing the block header via SHA-256 until the resulting hash falls below the network's current **target threshold** $T$ (1 mark).
3. It is the variable that introduces randomness in the mining process, ensuring that finding a valid proof of work requires brute-force trial and error, which constitutes the "work" in Proof of Work (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**A (a)** Explain in detail the Proof of Work consensus mechanism. Describe the steps involved in mining a new block, with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Proof of Work (PoW) is a consensus algorithm that requires miners to solve a computationally intensive puzzle before adding a new block. The puzzle involves finding a nonce such that the SHA-256 double hash of the block header is less than a target threshold $T$.

**Steps in Mining (7 marks):**

1. **Transaction Collection (1 mark):** Miners gather unconfirmed transactions from the mempool, verifying each transaction's digital signature, sender balance, and adherence to protocol rules (e.g., no double spend).
2. **Candidate Block Assembly (1 mark):** Valid transactions are organized into a Merkle Tree; the Merkle Root is computed and placed in the block header alongside the previous block's hash, timestamp, difficulty bits, and nonce.
3. **Proof of Work Computation (2 marks):** The miner iterates over the nonce from $0$ to $2^{32}-1$, recomputing $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}))$ each time. The goal is to find $H < T$ where $T$ is derived from the current difficulty $D$.
4. **Block Broadcast and Verification (1 mark):** Upon finding a valid nonce, the miner broadcasts the block. Other nodes independently verify the PoW by hashing the header once with the disclosed nonce.
5. **Reward Distribution (1 mark):** The winning miner receives the block subsidy (newly minted BTC) plus all transaction fees from included transactions via a special **coinbase transaction**.
6. **Difficulty Adjustment (1 mark):** Every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks), the protocol recalibrates $T$ to maintain an average 10-minute block interval regardless of total network hash rate fluctuations.

**Block Header Structure:**
- Version (4 bytes)
- Previous Block Hash (32 bytes)
- Merkle Root (32 bytes)
- Timestamp (4 bytes)
- Difficulty Bits (4 bytes)
- Nonce (4 bytes)

---

**A (b)** Discuss the economic incentives and security guarantees provided by mining. Why is honest mining the dominant strategy? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Economic Incentives (3.5 marks):**
1. **Block Reward:** Newly minted coins, halving every 210,000 blocks, create a predictable revenue stream.
2. **Transaction Fees:** As subsidies decrease over time, fees will become the primary incentive (forecast by ~2140 for Bitcoin).
3. **Capital Investment:** Specialized ASIC hardware represents a sunk cost, motivating miners to act honestly to protect their investment's value.
4. **Network Effect:** A secure, healthy network increases the cryptocurrency's value, indirectly benefiting all stakeholders.

**Security Guarantees (2 marks):**
1. **Costly Attack Vector:** Rewriting history requires outpacing the entire honest network's hash rate — prohibitively expensive (>₹50,000 crore for Bitcoin).
2. **Probabilistic Finality:** Each additional block confirmation exponentially increases attack cost by factor $D$.

**Why Honest Mining Dominates (1.5 marks):**
According to game theory, the Nash Equilibrium of mining is honest participation. A 51% attacker would:
- Spend enormous energy (unprofitable if block reward > attack cost).
- Crash the currency's market value by destroying trust, making stolen coins worthless.
- Lose future block rewards permanently.

Hence, rational actors choose honesty — Bitcoin's mining is a beautiful example of *cryptoeconomics*.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B (a)** With suitable derivations, explain how mining difficulty is calculated and how it adjusts over time in Bitcoin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Difficulty $D$ is the ratio of the maximum target $T_{\max}$ to the current target $T_{\text{current}}$:

$$D = \frac{T_{\max}}{T_{\text{current}}}, \quad T_{\max} = 2^{224} \text{ (Bitcoin)}$$

**Expected Hashes Derivation (2 marks):**

Probability a random hash is valid: $p = T_{\text{current}} / 2^{256}$

Expected attempts from geometric distribution: $\mathbb{E}[X] = 1/p = 2^{256} / T_{\text{current}}$

Substituting $T_{\text{current}} = T_{\max}/D$:

$$\mathbb{E}[X] = \frac{2^{256} \cdot D}{2^{224}} = D \cdot 2^{32}$$

**Adjustment Algorithm (3 marks):** Every 2016 blocks:

1. Measure actual time $T_{\text{actual}}$ spent mining last 2016 blocks.
2. Compute new target: $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times (T_{\text{actual}} / 2 \text{ weeks})$
3. Clamp adjustment to factor of 4 (max 4× increase or decrease) to prevent extreme volatility.
4. If $T_{\text{actual}} > 2$ weeks → difficulty decreases (miners too slow).
5. If $T_{\text{actual}} < 2$ weeks → difficulty increases (miners too fast).

**Numerical Example (1 mark):** If 2016 blocks were mined in 1 week instead of 2:

$$T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times \frac{1 \text{ week}}{2 \text{ weeks}} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{2}$$

Difficulty doubles, ensuring next epoch targets ~2 weeks.

---

**B (b)** What is a 51% attack? Derive the cost of launching such an attack on a block buried $n$ blocks deep. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A 51% attack occurs when a single entity controls more than 50% of the network's total hash rate, enabling it to:
- Reverse recently confirmed transactions (double spend).
- Prevent other miners from finding blocks.
- Exclude or modify transactions in their blocks.

However, the attacker **cannot** steal coins from arbitrary addresses or change the total supply.

**Cost Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $H_{\text{net}}$ = honest network hash rate, $H_{\text{att}}$ = attacker hash rate > $0.5 H_{\text{net}}$.

Expected hashes per honest block: $E[H] = D \cdot 2^{32}$.

To rewrite $n$ blocks, attacker must:
1. Re-mine target block + $n$ subsequent blocks.
2. Total hashes needed: $(n+1) \cdot D \cdot 2^{32}$.

Cost:

$$C_{\text{attack}} = (n+1) \cdot D \cdot 2^{32} \cdot c_e \cdot P_{\text{hardware}}$$

where $c_e$ = energy cost per hash, $P_{\text{hardware}}$ = hardware amortization factor.

**Numerical Estimate (1 mark):** For Bitcoin ($D \approx 9 \times 10^{13}$, $n = 6$):

$$C_{\text{attack}} \approx 7 \times 9 \times 10^{13} \times 2^{32} \times c_e \approx 2.7 \times 10^{23} \text{ hashes}$$

At $c_e \approx 10^{-10}$ USD/hash → **~$27 million** in electricity alone, before hardware.

**Why Attack Fails Long-Term (1 mark):**
Even if successful, the visible double-spend destroys market confidence, crashing BTC price by 50–80%, making the attacker's stolen coins worth far less than attack costs. Mining's real need is this *self-enforcing economic deterrence*.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Writing "mining is needed only to earn rewards" — loses 2–3 marks. Always emphasize consensus + security.
> - ❌ Forgetting SHA-256 is a **double hash**, not single — common 1-mark deduction.
> - ❌ Confusing target $T$ with difficulty $D$ (they are inverse). Examiner will deduct.
> - ❌ In derivation questions, skipping the geometric distribution step costs full 2 marks.
> - ❌ Not mentioning the **4× adjustment clamp** in difficulty questions — important KTU-specific detail.
> - ❌ Failing to mention **Nash Equilibrium** in incentive questions — high-value concept.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — The Real Need for Mining:**

- ✅ Mining is **not** primarily about earning rewards — it solves **consensus, security, and issuance**.
- ✅ It is the practical solution to the **Byzantine Generals Problem** in trustless networks.
- ✅ The mining puzzle: find a nonce such that $\text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header})) < T$.
- ✅ Expected hashes per block = $D \cdot 2^{32}$ (derived from geometric distribution).
- ✅ Difficulty adjusts every **2016 blocks** with a **4× clamp** to maintain 10-minute intervals.
- ✅ Block reward halves every **210,000 blocks**: $R_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-n}$ BTC.
- ✅ Total Bitcoin supply cap: **21,000,000 BTC** (geometric series sum).
- ✅ **Longest Chain Rule** = Nakamoto Consensus; honest miners always build on the heaviest chain.
- ✅ 51% attack cost grows **linearly with depth $n$** but exponentially in energy expenditure.
- ✅ Honest mining is the **Nash Equilibrium** — attacking self-destroys asset value.
- ✅ Real-world analogies: village crier, gold prospecting, competitive exam with lottery element.
- ✅ Beyond crypto: mining-style PoW secures time-stamping, anti-spam (Hashcash), supply chains.
- ✅ Energy consumption is not waste — it is the **thermodynamic cost of trust**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consensus - Definition, Types, Consensus in Blockchain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Consensus - Definition, Types, Consensus in Blockchain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Consensus

In distributed systems and blockchain technology, **Consensus** refers to a fault-tolerant mechanism that allows a network of distributed, mutually distrusting nodes to agree on a single, canonical state of the shared ledger (or a single value/decision) even in the presence of faulty or malicious participants.

Mathematically, a consensus protocol $\mathcal{C}$ ensures that all honest (non-Byzantine) nodes in a network $\mathcal{N}$ eventually agree on the same output $v$ such that:

$$\forall n_i, n_j \in \mathcal{N}_{honest} : \text{State}(n_i) = \text{State}(n_j) = v$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Consensus is the backbone of trustless distributed ledgers. Without consensus, there is no single source of truth in a decentralized network. KTU examiners expect the *three core properties* — **Safety, Liveness, and Fault Tolerance** — to be tied to every consensus definition.

### Three Fundamental Properties of Consensus

1. **Safety (Agreement):** All honest nodes agree on the same value. No two honest nodes commit conflicting decisions.
   $$\text{Safety} : \text{No two honest nodes decide differently}$$
2. **Liveness (Termination):** Every valid proposal is eventually included. The system does not stall.
   $$\text{Liveness} : \forall \text{ valid } tx \Rightarrow \text{ eventually committed}$$
3. **Fault Tolerance:** The system continues to operate correctly despite a bounded number of failures.
   $$\text{Resilience} \leq f \text{ where } f \text{ is the number of tolerable faulty nodes}$$

---

## 1.2 Intuition: The "Friends Deciding Dinner" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
>
> Imagine **10 friends** sitting in 10 different rooms across a city. They must unanimously decide on **one restaurant** for dinner without meeting face-to-face. Some friends may be unreachable (offline), some may lie (malicious), and the phone lines may drop randomly. **Consensus is the rulebook** that lets them all arrive at *the same restaurant name* despite all this chaos.
>
> In **Blockchain**, the friends are **nodes**, the restaurant is the **next block**, and the rulebook is the **consensus protocol** (e.g., Proof of Work, Proof of Stake).

---

## 1.3 The Byzantine Generals Problem — Origin of Consensus

The theoretical foundation of blockchain consensus traces back to the **Byzantine Generals Problem (BGP)** formalized by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982). Several divisions of the Byzantine army surround a city; they must launch a **coordinated attack** but can only communicate via messengers, and some generals may be **traitors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The BGP proves that consensus is only achievable if the number of traitors $T$ is strictly less than one-third of the total generals $N$:
> $$N \geq 3T + 1$$
> This inequality is the **mathematical ceiling** of fault tolerance in asynchronous consensus.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $N$ | Total nodes in the network | **Variable (network-specific)** |
| $T$ / $f$ | Number of faulty/malicious nodes tolerated | $f < N/3$ (BFT) |
| $v$ | Agreed value (block / decision) | Block hash |
| $\mathcal{N}_{honest}$ | Set of non-malicious nodes | $\geq 2f + 1$ |

---

## 1.4 Why Consensus is Indispensable in Blockchain

In a centralized system (like a bank), a single authority dictates truth. Blockchain removes this authority, so a **decentralized agreement mechanism** becomes mandatory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Roles Consensus Plays in Blockchain:**
> 1. **Determinism of State** — Ensures every node has an identical copy of the ledger.
> 2. **Double-Spend Prevention** — Ensures a single coin is not spent twice.
> 3. **Sybil Resistance** — Prevents one entity from creating fake identities to dominate the network.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of a Generic Consensus Protocol

A blockchain consensus protocol generally executes the following structured logic:

- **Step 1 — Proposal:** A node (leader/validator/miner) proposes a candidate block $\mathcal{B}$ containing a set of valid transactions $\{tx_1, tx_2, ..., tx_k\}$.
- **Step 2 — Broadcast:** The proposal is propagated to all peer nodes in the network via a gossip protocol.
- **Step 3 — Validation:** Each node $n_i$ independently verifies the proposal against protocol rules (signature, format, double-spend checks).
- **Step 4 — Voting/Proof:** Nodes cast votes or submit cryptographic proofs (work, stake, etc.) to support the proposal.
- **Step 5 — Aggregation:** Votes/proofs are aggregated. A quorum threshold is checked:
  $$\text{Quorum Reached} \iff \text{Votes}_{\text{honest}} \geq 2f + 1$$
- **Step 6 — Commit:** If quorum is satisfied, the block is appended to the chain; the new state root is broadcast.
- **Step 7 — Finality:** Depending on the protocol, finality is *probabilistic* (PoW) or *absolute* (PBFT, Tendermint).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' behind each step:** Proposal initiates; Broadcast ensures visibility; Validation enforces honesty; Voting aggregates truth; Quorum prevents minority attacks; Commit locks the state; Finality defines whether reversal is possible.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Consensus Mechanisms

Consensus algorithms are broadly classified along two axes:

### A. By **Resource** Used for Sybil Resistance
- **Compute-based:** Proof of Work (PoW)
- **Capital-based:** Proof of Stake (PoS), Delegated PoS (DPoS)
- **Reputation/Identity-based:** Proof of Authority (PoA), Proof of Burn (PoB)
- **Voting-based:** Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT), Raft, Paxos

### B. By **Fault Model** Tolerated
- **Crash Fault Tolerant (CFT):** Tolerates nodes that stop (e.g., Raft, Paxos). Assumes $f < N/2$.
- **Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT):** Tolerates arbitrary/malicious behavior. Assumes $f < N/3$.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Major Consensus Algorithms

### 2.3.1 Proof of Work (PoW) — Used by Bitcoin

Miners compete to solve a computationally hard puzzle: find a nonce $n$ such that:

$$H(\text{Block}_{header} \parallel n) < \text{Target}$$

The probability of finding a valid nonce follows a **Poisson distribution**:

$$P(\text{solve in time } t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $\lambda$ is the network's combined hash rate. Difficulty is retargeted every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) in Bitcoin to maintain a **10-minute** block interval.

### 2.3.2 Proof of Stake (PoS) — Used by Ethereum (post-Merge)

Validators lock (stake) cryptocurrency $\mathcal{S}$. A pseudo-random selection function $\sigma$ picks the next proposer weighted by stake:

$$P(n_i \text{ is chosen}) = \frac{\mathcal{S}_{n_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \mathcal{S}_{n_j}}$$

Slashing penalizes dishonest validators: $\mathcal{S}_{n_i} \leftarrow \mathcal{S}_{n_i} \cdot (1 - \beta)$ where $\beta$ is the slashing ratio.

### 2.3.3 Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — Used by EOS, Tron

Token holders vote for a fixed number $k$ of **delegates** (block producers). Only these $k$ delegates produce blocks, achieving high throughput.

### 2.3.4 Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)

A leader-based protocol operating in three phases: **Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit**. Tolerates $f$ Byzantine nodes where $N \geq 3f + 1$.

### 2.3.5 Proof of Authority (PoA)

A small set of pre-approved, identified validators (authorities) produce blocks. Used in private/consortium chains (e.g., VeChain, BNB Chain's older sidechains).

### 2.3.6 Other Noteworthy Algorithms
- **Raft / Paxos:** Crash-fault tolerant, used in Hyperledger Fabric ordering service.
- **Tendermint:** BFT PoS used in Cosmos.
- **Avalanche:** Probabilistic BFT using repeated sub-sampled voting.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Typical Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N \geq 3f + 1$ | Byzantine fault tolerance bound | PBFT, Tendermint |
| 2 | $N \geq 2f + 1$ | Crash fault tolerance bound | Raft, Paxos |
| 3 | $H(\text{Block} \parallel n) < T$ | PoW mining puzzle | Bitcoin, Litecoin |
| 4 | $P(\text{select } n_i) = \frac{\mathcal{S}_{n_i}}{\sum \mathcal{S}_j}$ | PoS leader election | Ethereum, Cardano |
| 5 | $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{expected}}$ | Difficulty retargeting | Bitcoin (every 2016 blocks) |
| 6 | $P(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Block discovery probability | PoW analysis |
| 7 | $f < N/3$ | Safety threshold in BFT | All BFT protocols |
| 8 | Finality type: Probabilistic vs Absolute | Reversal possibility | PoW vs PBFT |
| 9 | CAP: pick 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance} | Distributed systems trilemma | All distributed DBs |
| 10 | Block time $T_b$ | Average time between blocks | Bitcoin: 10 min, ETH: 12 s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Consensus algorithms are not just for cryptocurrency. They power supply chain traceability (Hyperledger), decentralized identity (DID), IoT coordination (IOTA), and cross-border settlement (Ripple).

---

## 2.5 The CAP Theorem & Blockchain Trilemma

The **CAP Theorem** (Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed system can simultaneously provide only **two of three** guarantees:

$$\text{Consistency} \cap \text{Availability} \cap \text{Partition Tolerance} \leq 2$$

In blockchain, this manifests as the **Blockchain Trilemma** — the trade-off between:
- **Decentralization**
- **Security**
- **Scalability**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Bitcoin chooses Decentralization + Security (sacrifices Scalability). Solana chooses Scalability + Security (sacrifices some Decentralization). Ethereum 2.0 attempts all three via sharding + PoS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Byzantine Fault Tolerance Bound ($N \geq 3f + 1$)

We derive the classic result that BFT consensus requires at least $3f + 1$ nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults.

**Step 1: Setup**
Let $N$ = total generals, $f$ = traitors, $N - f$ = loyal generals. Loyal generals must reach the same decision.

**Step 2: Divide loyal generals into two camps**
The loyal generals can be split by traitors' messages such that one camp sees $N - 2f$ loyal messages (excluding self and traitors), and the other also sees $N - 2f$ but with conflicting content.

**Step 3: Apply majority requirement**
For a loyal general to outvote traitors, the loyal majority must be strictly greater than the traitor count:

$$N - f - f > f$$

The first $f$ represents the traitors on one side, the second $f$ represents traitors on the other side.

**Step 4: Solve the inequality**

$$N - 2f > f$$

$$N > 3f$$

$$N \geq 3f + 1 \quad \text{(since N is an integer)}$$

**Step 5: Conclusion**

$$\boxed{N \geq 3f + 1}$$

This means a network of **4 nodes** can tolerate only **1** Byzantine node. A network of **10** nodes can tolerate up to **3** Byzantine nodes. This is why PBFT requires a minimum of 4 nodes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bitcoin Difficulty Retargeting

Bitcoin retargets difficulty every **2016 blocks** to maintain a **10-minute** average block time (total target time $\approx$ 2 weeks = 1,209,600 seconds).

**Step 1: Define variables**
- $T_{actual}$ = Time taken to mine the last 2016 blocks (in seconds)
- $T_{expected}$ = Expected time = $2016 \times 600 = 1,209,600$ seconds
- $D_{old}$ = Old difficulty
- $D_{new}$ = New difficulty

**Step 2: Inverse proportionality**
If blocks came too fast, more hashes were tried per second; difficulty must increase. If too slow, difficulty must decrease.

$$D_{new} \propto \frac{1}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 3: Ratio formulation**

$$D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \frac{T_{expected}}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 4: Clamping**
Bitcoin caps the adjustment to a factor of **4** in either direction to prevent extreme volatility:

$$D_{new} = \min\left( D_{old} \cdot 4, \max\left( \frac{D_{old}}{4}, D_{old} \cdot \frac{T_{expected}}{T_{actual}} \right) \right)$$

**Step 5: Worked Example**
Suppose $T_{actual} = 604,800$ seconds (1 week — too fast).

$$D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \frac{1,209,600}{604,800} = D_{old} \cdot 2$$

Difficulty **doubles**. Miners must find nonces that satisfy a target that is **half** as large, requiring roughly **twice** the expected hash attempts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PoS Validator Selection Probability

**Step 1: Define the stake pool**
Let $N$ validators with stakes $\{\mathcal{S}_1, \mathcal{S}_2, ..., \mathcal{S}_N\}$. Total stake:

$$\mathcal{S}_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{S}_i$$

**Step 2: Apply proportional probability**
Validator $n_i$ is selected with probability proportional to its stake:

$$P(n_i) = \frac{\mathcal{S}_i}{\mathcal{S}_{total}}$$

**Step 3: Verify normalization**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} P(n_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\mathcal{S}_i}{\mathcal{S}_{total}} = \frac{\mathcal{S}_{total}}{\mathcal{S}_{total}} = 1$$

**Step 4: Example**
Suppose 4 validators with stakes $\{100, 200, 300, 400\}$ ETH. Total = 1000 ETH.

$$P(n_1) = 0.10, \quad P(n_2) = 0.20, \quad P(n_3) = 0.30, \quad P(n_4) = 0.40$$

**Step 5: Add randomness caveat**
Pure stake-weighting leads to **rich-get-richer**. Modern PoS protocols (e.g., Ethereum's RANDAO + VDF) add verifiable randomness:

$$P(n_i \text{ in slot } s) = f(\sigma_s, \mathcal{S}_i, \text{UTXO}_{age})$$

where $\sigma_s$ is the slot-specific random seed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Consensus Algorithms

| Property | PoW | PoS | DPoS | PBFT | PoA | Raft |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource** | Compute (Hashrate) | Capital (Stake) | Vote-based | Voting | Identity | Leader election |
| **Fault Model** | Byzantine | Byzantine | Byzantine | Byzantine | Crash/Byzantine | Crash |
| **Tolerates $f$** | $f < 0.5N$ (probabilistic) | $f < N/3$ | $f < k/2$ (delegates) | $f < N/3$ | $f < N/2$ | $f < N/2$ |
| **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7 (BTC) | ~30 (ETH) | ~1000+ (EOS) | ~1000 (private) | High | Very high |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Mixed | Absolute (after voting) | Absolute | Absolute | Absolute |
| **Energy Use** | **Very High** | Low | Low | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Examples** | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum 2.0 | EOS, Tron | Hyperledger | VeChain | Fabric Orderer |
| **Decentralization** | High | Medium-High | Medium | Low | Low | Very Low |
| **Permissioned?** | No | No | No | Optional | **Yes** | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Answers:** When comparing consensus, always justify using **at least 3 dimensions**: security model, finality type, and decentralization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Blockchain Consensus Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the universal flow of any blockchain consensus protocol, mapping interactions between nodes, validators, and the ledger.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transaction Pool - Mempool] --> B[Proposer / Leader Node]
    B --> C[Construct Candidate Block]
    C --> D[Broadcast Block to Network]
    D --> E{Validator Node 1}
    D --> F{Validator Node 2}
    D --> G{Validator Node N}
    E --> H[Validate Transactions]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Consensus Rule Check}
    I -->|PoW| J[Solve Cryptographic Puzzle]
    I -->|PoS| K[Stake-Weighted Voting]
    I -->|PBFT| L[PrePrepare / Prepare / Commit]
    J --> M{Quorum Reached?}
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N[Append Block to Chain]
    M -->|No| O[Discard / Re-propose]
    N --> P[Update State Root]
    P --> Q[Broadcast New State]
    Q --> R[Finality Achieved]
    R --> S[Probabilistic Finality]
    R --> T[Absolute Finality]
```

---

## 4.2 Byzantine Generals Decision Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Commander - Loyal] -->|Attack| B[Lieutenant 1 - Loyal]
    A -->|Attack| C[Lieutenant 2 - Loyal]
    A -->|Retreat| D[Lieutenant 3 - Traitor]
    D -->|Attack| B
    D -->|Retreat| C
    B --> E{Majority Vote}
    C --> E
    E -->|2 Attack vs 1 Retreat| F[Consensus: Attack]
    B -.->|Cross-check| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **Byzantine Generals Problem** visually. Lieutenant 3 (the traitor) sends conflicting orders. Lieutenants 1 and 2 cross-check by exchanging messages. The majority (Attack × 2 vs Retreat × 1) wins, achieving consensus despite the traitor.

---

## 4.3 Comparison of Consensus Algorithms (Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blockchain Consensus Mechanisms] --> B[Public / Permissionless]
    A --> C[Private / Permissioned]
    B --> D[Proof of Work - PoW]
    B --> E[Proof of Stake - PoS]
    B --> F[Delegated PoS - DPoS]
    C --> G[PBFT]
    C --> H[Proof of Authority - PoA]
    C --> I[Raft / Paxos]
    D --> D1[Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin]
    E --> E1[Ethereum 2.0, Cardano, Polkadot]
    F --> F1[EOS, Tron, BitShares]
    G --> G1[Hyperledger Fabric, Stellar]
    H --> H1[VeChain, Palm Network]
    I --> I1[Fabric Orderer, etcd]
```

---

## 4.4 PoW vs PoS — Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POW[Proof of Work - Bitcoin]
        A1[Miners compete] --> A2[Race to find nonce]
        A2 --> A3{H Hash Valid?}
        A3 -->|Yes| A4[Miner broadcasts block]
        A3 -->|No| A2
        A4 --> A5[Other nodes verify and accept]
    end
    subgraph POS[Proof of Stake - Ethereum]
        B1[Validators stake ETH] --> B2[Random selection by stake]
        B2 --> B3[Selected validator proposes block]
        B3 --> B4[Other validators attest]
        B4 --> B5{2/3 Attestations?}
        B5 -->|Yes| B6[Block finalized]
        B5 -->|No| B3
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define consensus in distributed systems. List any two properties of a consensus protocol.**

**Model Answer:**
Consensus is a fault-tolerant agreement mechanism that enables all honest nodes in a distributed network to agree on a single, consistent state of the shared ledger or a common decision, even when some nodes fail or act maliciously.

**Two Properties:**
1. **Safety (Agreement):** All honest nodes decide on the same value. No two honest nodes commit conflicting values.
2. **Liveness (Termination):** Every valid request is eventually decided upon; the system does not halt indefinitely.

*(Award full marks only if both properties are correctly defined; partial credit for one correct property.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 1]`
**Q: What is the Byzantine Generals Problem? State the fault-tolerance condition.**

**Model Answer:**
The Byzantine Generals Problem is a logical dilemma describing how a group of generals commanding divisions of the Byzantine army can reach a unanimous decision (attack or retreat) when some generals may be traitors who send false information. It abstracts the challenge of achieving agreement in distributed systems with untrusted participants.

**Fault Tolerance Condition:**
For consensus to be possible:
$$N \geq 3f + 1$$
where $N$ is the total number of nodes and $f$ is the number of faulty/malicious nodes.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Proof of Work (PoW) consensus mechanism. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and how difficulty is adjusted in Bitcoin.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: PoW Mechanism & Diagram

**Step 1: Definition**
Proof of Work is a consensus mechanism where miners compete to solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a valid solution broadcasts the block and is rewarded.

**Step 2: Puzzle Formulation**
Miners search for a nonce $n$ such that the SHA-256 double-hash of the block header is less than a target $T$:

$$H(\text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Block}_{header} \parallel n))) < T$$

**Step 3: Workflow**
1. Miners collect transactions from the mempool.
2. They form a Merkle tree of transactions and build a block header.
3. They iterate nonces (and extra-nonce) until the hash condition is satisfied.
4. The winning miner broadcasts the block.
5. Other nodes verify and add it to their chain.
6. The miner receives the **block subsidy** + **transaction fees**.

**Step 4: Diagram**
*(Refer to the PoW vs PoS architecture diagram in Section 4.4.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Advantages, Limitations, Difficulty Adjustment

**Advantages:**
- **Sybil-resistant:** Cost of attack is proportional to hardware + electricity.
- **Proven security:** Bitcoin has operated securely since 2009.
- **Permissionless:** Anyone can mine.

**Limitations:**
- **Energy-intensive:** Bitcoin consumes ~0.5% of global electricity.
- **51% Attack risk:** A single entity controlling >50% hashrate can double-spend.
- **Probabilistic finality:** Blocks can be orphaned; ~6 confirmations recommended.
- **Centralization of mining pools.**

**Difficulty Adjustment (refer Section 3.2):**
$$D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \frac{T_{expected}}{T_{actual}}, \quad \text{clamped to } [\frac{1}{4}, 4]$$
Retargeted every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) to maintain 10-minute block intervals.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]`

**Q: Compare Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), and PBFT consensus mechanisms with respect to security model, finality, throughput, and use cases. Derive the Byzantine fault tolerance bound.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis

| Dimension | PoW | PoS | PBFT |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Security Model** | Computational puzzle | Economic stake | Multi-round voting |
| **Tolerated Faults** | $f < 0.5N$ (probabilistic) | $f < N/3$ | $f < N/3$ |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Mixed (depends on impl.) | Absolute |
| **Throughput** | Low (~7 TPS) | Medium (~30-100 TPS) | High (~1000 TPS) |
| **Energy** | Very High | Low | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Public cryptocurrencies | Public smart-contract platforms | Consortium chains |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: BFT Bound Derivation

*(Refer to Section 3.1 for full step-by-step derivation.)*

**Summary of derivation:**
1. Loyal generals split into two camps by traitor messages.
2. To outvote traitors: $N - f - f > f$
3. Solving: $N > 3f \Rightarrow N \geq 3f + 1$

**Conclusion:** Consensus is possible only if loyal nodes outnumber traitors by more than 3:1.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the fault model:** Students often compare PoW and PoS without mentioning that PoW tolerates $f < 0.5N$ probabilistically, while PoS targets $f < N/3$ under BFT assumptions. **-2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing probabilistic vs absolute finality:** PoW has *probabilistic* finality (chance of reversal decreases exponentially with confirmations). PBFT has *absolute* finality. **-2 marks** if not stated.
> 3. **Not mentioning CAP / Blockchain Trilemma:** KTU examiners love this. A "Why is consensus hard?" answer that omits CAP loses **-1 to -2 marks.**
> 4. **Missing the $N \geq 3f + 1$ inequality in PBFT answers:** This is a **mandatory** formula. Without it, the fault-tolerance section is incomplete.
> 5. **No diagram = -2 marks:** Always include a labeled block diagram in 14-mark questions. Even a hand-drawn flow will score partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 PECCT412 Module 2**

- **Consensus = Fault-tolerant agreement** among distributed, mutually distrusting nodes.
- **Three pillars:** Safety (Agreement) + Liveness (Termination) + Fault Tolerance.
- **Byzantine Generals Problem (Lamport et al., 1982):** Foundation of blockchain consensus.
- **BFT bound:** $N \geq 3f + 1$ (mandatory formula for 14-mark questions).
- **CFT bound:** $N \geq 2f + 1$ (used by Raft, Paxos).
- **PoW equation:** $H(\text{Block} \parallel n) < T$ — Bitcoin uses double SHA-256.
- **PoS probability:** $P(n_i) = \mathcal{S}_i / \mathcal{S}_{total}$ — stake-weighted selection.
- **Bitcoin difficulty retargets every 2016 blocks**; formula $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot (T_{expected} / T_{actual})$, clamped to factor 4.
- **PoW finality = Probabilistic; PBFT finality = Absolute.**
- **DPoS:** Token holders elect $k$ delegates; only delegates produce blocks (high TPS).
- **PoA:** Pre-approved identities as validators; used in private/consortium chains.
- **PBFT phases:** Pre-prepare → Prepare → Commit; tolerates $f < N/3$ Byzantine faults.
- **CAP Theorem:** Pick 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance}.
- **Blockchain Trilemma:** Decentralization ↔ Security ↔ Scalability (can optimize 2 of 3).
- **Energy comparison:** PoW = High; PoS/DPoS/PBFT/PoA = Low.
- **Real-world examples:** BTC = PoW, ETH 2.0 = PoS, EOS = DPoS, Hyperledger = PBFT/Raft, VeChain = PoA.
- **Common 3-mark Qs:** Define consensus, list properties, explain BGP, state BFT bound.
- **Common 14-mark Qs:** Compare any two consensus algorithms, explain PoW + difficulty, derive $N \geq 3f+1$, discuss CAP/BTC trilemma in blockchain.
- **Always draw a diagram in 14-mark answers** — it is worth 2-3 marks.
- **Memorize the table in Section 3.4** — it covers 80% of comparison questions.

> **End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Consensus: Definition, Types, Consensus in Blockchain**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
