- **Trust Centralization Index** $T_c = 1 - H(D) / H_{max}$ measures decentralization; lower is better.
- The 2019 **sKRW Synthetix incident** and 2020 **bZx flash loan oracle attack** are canonical case studies referenced in KTU answers.
- For KTU 2024, expect either a 3-mark *classification* question or a 14-mark *architecture + numerical aggregation* question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deploying Smart Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Deploying Smart Contracts # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Smart Contract Deployment** is the immutable, one-time on-chain process of uploading compiled contract **bytecode** (and its accompanying constructor logic) to a specific address on a target blockchain network, after which a unique contract account is generated and a transaction receipt is permanently recorded on the distributed ledger. The deployment transaction is broadcast, validated by network consensus nodes, and finalized in a block — resulting in a globally accessible, tamper-proof, deterministic executable whose address is deterministically derived from the deployer's wallet address and their sequentially incrementing **nonce**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Deployment is the *bridge* between the **off-chain development environment** (Solidity source code → Solidity compiler → EVM bytecode) and the **on-chain execution layer** (Ethereum Virtual Machine state, storage, and code). Without deployment, a contract is merely a `.sol` text file with zero economic or computational value. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model Think of deploying a smart contract like **registering a company with the Registrar of Companies**: | Real-World Analogy | Smart Contract Equivalent | |---|---| | Drafting the **Memorandum of Association (MoA)** | Writing the Solidity (`.sol`) source code | | **Notarization** of the document | Compilation via `solc` (Solidity compiler) | | Paying the **registration fee** at the registrar | Paying **Gas Fee** in ETH/gWei | | Getting a **Company CIN (Corporate Identity Number)** | Receiving a unique **Contract Address** (`0x...`) | | The company is now a **legal entity** | The contract is now an **on-chain account** with code & storage | | Once registered, MoA **cannot be changed** | Once deployed, contract bytecode is **immutable** | The contract's address is *born* the moment the miner/validator includes the deployment transaction inside a block. The contract then becomes a **first-class citizen** of the blockchain — capable of holding a balance, executing logic, and being called by any externally owned account (EOA) or other contract. ## 1.3 Core Components of a Deployment Transaction A deployment transaction differs slightly from a regular value-transfer transaction. The key fields are: - **`from`**: The EOA (Externally Owned Account) initiating the deployment — pays gas. - **`data`**: The **compiled bytecode** of the contract (the entire payload). Empty `to` field indicates a *contract creation* transaction. - **`value`**: Optional ETH transferred to the contract at the moment of creation (forwarded to the `constructor`). - **`gasLimit` / `gasPrice` / `maxFeePerGas`**: The gas economics governing execution. - **`nonce`**: Strictly incremented integer; prevents replay and determines the address via `CREATE` opcode rules. > [!NOTE] > A **regular transaction** has `to = 0xRecipient` and `data = callData`. A **deployment transaction** has `to = null/empty` and `data = contractBytecode`. This distinction is enforced at the **RLP encoding** layer of the Ethereum protocol. ## 1.4 Why Deployment Matters in Blockchain Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Scheme places deployment at the *intersection* of three engineering domains: > 1. **Software Engineering** — compilation, dependency management, versioning. > 2. **Cryptographic Security** — immutable bytecode, address derivation. > 3. **Distributed Systems** — consensus finality, gas economics, mempool propagation. > > Examiners love this topic because it tests **end-to-end understanding** — from `.sol` source to live dApp interaction. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Smart Contract Deployment Lifecycle (Seven-Stage Pipeline) The deployment of a Solidity smart contract follows a strictly ordered pipeline. Each stage produces an artifact that feeds the next. ### Stage 1 — Authoring Source Code The developer writes logic in a high-level language. The dominant choice is **Solidity** (`.sol` file), with Vyper and Yul as alternatives. The file declares: - `pragma solidity ^0.8.x;` (compiler version lock) - `contract ContractName { ... }` (blueprint for the EVM object) ### Stage 2 — Compilation (`solc`) The Solidity compiler (`solc`) ingests the `.sol` file and emits two critical artifacts: | Artifact | Purpose | |---|---| | **Bytecode** | EVM-executable, low-level opcodes (hex string) — what gets sent in the `data` field of the tx | | **ABI (Application Binary Interface)** | JSON descriptor of public functions/events — used by clients to encode/decode calls | > [!NOTE] > The compiler also produces **Metadata** (Swarm hash, compiler settings) and an **AST**. The metadata's IPFS/Swarm hash is appended to the deployed bytecode, which is critical for **source verification** on block explorers (Etherscan). ### Stage 3 — Local Testing (Optional but Mandatory in Industry) Before spending real ETH, developers test on local simulated chains: - **Ganache** (Truffle Suite) - **Hardhat Network** (in-process EVM) - **Foundry / Anvil** This verifies constructor logic, gas estimation, and reverts **without** financial risk. ### Stage 4 — Testnet Deployment Deployment to a public testnet (Sepolia, Holesky, Mumbai, etc.) using **test ETH** from faucets. This validates the deployment under *real network conditions* — variable gas prices, block times, mempool congestion. ### Stage 5 — Wallet & Signing The deployment transaction is signed using a wallet: - **MetaMask** (browser extension, ECDSA signing) - **Ledger / Trezor** (hardware, cold-storage security) - **Web3 libraries** (`ethers.js`, `web3.js`) — programmatic signing via private key ### Stage 6 — Mainnet Broadcast The signed raw transaction is broadcast to the peer-to-peer network. It enters the **mempool**, where validators select it (usually by highest `maxPriorityFeePerGas`). The transaction is included in a block and reaches **finality** (≈ 12–15 min on Ethereum post-Merge for full economic finality). ### Stage 7 — Contract Verification The deployed bytecode's metadata hash is cross-referenced with the published source code on **Etherscan/Polygonscan** to prove the contract is open-source and behaves as claimed. ## 2.2 Address Derivation — The `CREATE` Opcode Formula When a contract creates another contract (or when an EOA deploys a contract), the EVM executes the **`CREATE` opcode**, which deterministically computes the new contract's address: $$ \text{Address} = \text{Keccak256}\Big(\,\text{RLP}\big(\,[\,\text{deployerAddress},\,\text{nonce}\,]\,\big)\,\Big)[\,12..31\,] $$ **Where:** - `deployerAddress` = the **160-bit** address of the EOA or parent contract - `nonce` = the integer transaction count of the deployer (for EOAs) or the `CREATE` counter (for contracts) - `RLP` = Recursive Length Prefix encoding - `[12..31]` = take the **last 20 bytes** of the 32-byte hash > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pre-computing a contract address is a KTU favorite.** Always note: the same `deployer + nonce` **always** produces the same address. This enables the **"counterfactual deployment"** pattern used by gas-efficient protocols like **Gnosis Safe** and **Uniswap V4**. The companion opcode **`CREATE2`** (introduced in EIP-1014) uses a different, salt-based derivation: $$ \text{Address}_{\text{CREATE2}} = \text{Keccak256}\Big(\,0xFF \;\Vert\; \text{deployer} \;\Vert\; \text{salt}_{32} \;\Vert\; \text{keccak256(initCode)}\,\Big)[\,12..31\,] $$ This guarantees the same address across chains (Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum) given the same salt and init code. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Format | KTU Weightage | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Contract Address (CREATE) | `keccak256(RLP([deployer, nonce]))[12:]` | 20-byte hex (`0x...`) | ★★★ High | | 2 | Contract Address (CREATE2) | `keccak256(0xFF ‖ deployer ‖ salt ‖ keccak256(init))[12:]` | 20-byte hex | ★★★ High | | 3 | Gas Cost — Tx Base | **21,000** gas | gas units | ★★ Medium | | 4 | Gas Cost — Per Zero Byte of Data | **4** gas | gas/byte | ★★ Medium | | 5 | Gas Cost — Per Non-Zero Byte of Data | **16** gas (post-Istanbul 2020; was 68) | gas/byte | ★★ Medium | | 6 | Gas Cost — Contract Creation | **32,000** gas | gas units | ★★★ High | | 7 | Gas Cost — Per Byte of Deployed Code | **200** gas | gas/byte | ★★ Medium | | 8 | Total Deployment Cost | $21{,}000 + 16{,}000 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4{,}000 \cdot n_{\text{zero}} + 32{,}000 + 200 \cdot n_{\text{code}}$ | gas | ★★★ High | | 9 | ETH to gWei | $1\;\text{ETH} = 10^{9}\;\text{gWei} = 10^{18}\;\text{Wei}$ | unit conversion | ★★ Medium | | 10 | EVM Stack Depth Limit | **1024** items | items | ★ Low | | 11 | Contract Code Size Limit (Ethereum) | **24,576 bytes** (post-Spurious Dragon) | bytes | ★★ Medium | | 12 | EVM Word Size | **256 bits** (32 bytes) | bits | ★★ Medium | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Use `\vert` (LaTeX) — never the pipe symbol `\vert` inside markdown tables. The total deployment gas formula is the *most-tested* numerical question in Module 4. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Smart contract deployment is the foundation of **Decentralized Finance (DeFi)**, **Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs)**, **Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs)**, and **Web3 identity systems**. Mastery of deployment is non-negotiable for roles in: - Smart Contract Auditor (CertiK, OpenZeppelin, Trail of Bits) - Solidity Developer (Ethereum Foundation, Consensys) - dApp Architect (Uniswap, Aave, Compound) - Web3 DevOps Engineer (Hardhat, Foundry CI/CD pipelines) > [!IMPORTANT] > Every **multimillion-dollar exploit** in blockchain history (e.g., the **DAO Hack 2016**, **Parity Wallet 2017**) traces back to a flawed deployment pattern — either unprotected initializer functions, unprotected `selfdestruct`, or unverified bytecode. **Deployment is where security begins, not ends.** --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Total Gas Cost of a Smart Contract Deployment ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement Compute the **total gas consumed** when deploying a contract whose compiled bytecode occupies $n_{\text{code}}$ bytes (of which $n_{\text{zero}}$ are zero bytes and $n_{\text{non-zero}} = n_{\text{code}} - n_{\text{zero}}$ are non-zero bytes) to the Ethereum mainnet. Express the result in gWei and USD, given a gas price of $G$ gWei and ETH price of $P$ USD. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation We split the gas cost into **four additive components** as per the Ethereum Yellow Paper and EIP-2028 (Istanbul upgrade): **Component 1 — Intrinsic Transaction Cost (Base Cost)** Every transaction, regardless of purpose, pays a base cost. $$ G_{\text{base}} = 21{,}000 \quad \text{gas} $$ **Component 2 — Calldata Cost (Data Field Cost)** The `data` field contains the deployment bytecode. Per EIP-2028: $$ G_{\text{data}} = 16 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4 \cdot n_{\text{zero}} \quad \text{gas} $$ **Reasoning for the asymmetry:** Non-zero bytes increase the *bandwidth* and *storage* burden of the network more than zero bytes (which compress better). The Istanbul upgrade reduced the non-zero cost from **68 → 16** to lower L1 rollup fees. **Component 3 — Contract Creation Fixed Cost** The EVM charges a fixed surcharge for instantiating a new account. $$ G_{\text{create}} = 32{,}000 \quad \text{gas} $$ **Component 4 — Code Storage Cost (Per-Byte Deployment)** The deployed bytecode is stored on-chain (state). Each byte costs: $$ G_{\text{code}} = 200 \cdot n_{\text{code}} \quad \text{gas} $$ **Total Deployment Gas:** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{total}} &= G_{\text{base}} + G_{\text{data}} + G_{\text{create}} + G_{\text{code}} \\[6pt] &= 21{,}000 + \big(16 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4 \cdot n_{\text{zero}}\big) + 32{,}000 + 200 \cdot n_{\text{code}} \\[6pt] &= 53{,}000 + 16 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4 \cdot n_{\text{zero}} + 200 \cdot n_{\text{code}} \end{aligned} $$ **Substitution** $n_{\text{code}} = n_{\text{zero}} + n_{\text{non-zero}}$: $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{total}} &= 53{,}000 + 16 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4 \cdot n_{\text{zero}} + 200 \cdot (n_{\text{zero}} + n_{\text{non-zero}}) \\[6pt] &= 53{,}000 + (16 + 200) \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + (4 + 200) \cdot n_{\text{zero}} \\[6pt] &= 53{,}000 + 216 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 204 \cdot n_{\text{zero}} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example **Given:** - $n_{\text{non-zero}} = 8{,}000$ bytes - $n_{\text{zero}} = 4{,}000$ bytes - Gas price $G = 30\;\text{gWei}$ - ETH price $P = \$2{,}000\;\text{USD}$ **Step 1 — Compute Total Gas:** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{total}} &= 53{,}000 + 216 \cdot 8{,}000 + 204 \cdot 4{,}000 \\[6pt] &= 53{,}000 + 1{,}728{,}000 + 816{,}000 \\[6pt] &= 2{,}597{,}000\;\text{gas} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to ETH:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{ETH}} = G_{\text{total}} \cdot G = 2{,}597{,}000 \times 30 = 77{,}910{,}000\;\text{gWei} $$ Converting gWei → ETH (divide by $10^{9}$): $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{ETH}} = \frac{77{,}910{,}000}{10^{9}} = 0.00007791\;\text{ETH} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to USD:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{USD}} = 0.00007791 \times 2{,}000 = \$0.15582 \approx \$0.16 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This $0.16 figure is the *raw deployment cost* — it does **not** include constructor execution cost (e.g., setting storage variables, minting initial supply). Constructor logic that writes to storage adds **20,000 gas per SSTORE** (cold slot) or **5,000 gas** (warm/modified slot). ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Deployment Tools Comparison | # | Tool | Type | Best For | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Remix IDE** | Browser-based IDE | Beginners, quick prototypes | Zero setup, integrated compiler, debugger | Not scalable, browser-dependent | | 2 | **Hardhat** | Local dev framework | Production dApps, TypeScript projects | Solidity stack traces, mainnet forking, plugin ecosystem | Steeper learning curve | | 3 | **Truffle Suite** | Local dev framework | Legacy projects, Ganache integration | Mature, mature migration system | Slower development, deprecated trends | | 4 | **Foundry** | Rust-based CLI | Auditors, gas-optimization work | Blazing fast, Fuzz testing (Forge), Solidity tests | Less beginner-friendly | | 5 | **Brownie** | Python framework | Python developers | Pythonic syntax, pytest integration | Slower, smaller community | | 6 | **OpenZeppelin Defender** | Deployment platform | Enterprise mainnet deploys | Relayer, multisig integration, audit trail | Paid tier for advanced features | | 7 | **Thirdweb / Moralis** | SDK + dashboard | No-code / low-code dApps | GUI deployment, templates | Vendor lock-in, less control | ## 3.3 Exhaustive Solidity Deployment Walkthrough (Remix IDE) ```solidity // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^0.8.19; /** * @title Counter * @dev A minimal contract to demonstrate end-to-end deployment */ contract Counter { // State variable stored in contract storage uint256 public count; // Event emitted on state change event CountIncremented(uint256 newCount, address indexed by); // Owner set at deployment time (immutable) address public immutable owner; // Constructor runs EXACTLY ONCE at deployment constructor(uint256 _initialCount) { count = _initialCount; owner = msg.sender; emit CountIncremented(_initialCount, msg.sender); } function increment() external { count += 1; emit CountIncremented(count, msg.sender); } function decrement() external { require(count > 0, "Counter: underflow"); count -= 1; emit CountIncremented(count, msg.sender); } } ``` ### Deployment Walkthrough (Remix) | Step | Action | Remix UI Element | |---|---|---| | 1 | Paste code into `contracts/Counter.sol` | File explorer | | 2 | Select compiler `0.8.19+commit...` | Solidity Compiler tab → "Compile Counter.sol" | | 3 | Switch to **Deploy & Run Transactions** tab | Sidebar icon (Ethereum logo) | | 4 | Choose **Environment**: `Injected Web3` (MetaMask) or `JavaScript VM` (local) | Environment dropdown | | 5 | Enter constructor argument `_initialCount = 0` | Input field next to "Deploy" | | 6 | Click **Deploy** | Orange "Deploy" button | | 7 | MetaMask popup → confirm transaction | Browser extension | | 8 | Contract appears in "Deployed Contracts" panel with address & buttons | Bottom panel | ### Programmatic Deployment (Hardhat + Ethers.js v6) ```javascript // scripts/deploy.js const hre = require("hardhat"); async function main() { // 1. Get the contract factory (compiled artifact) const Counter = await hre.ethers.getContractFactory("Counter"); // 2. Deploy with constructor argument const counter = await Counter.deploy(0); // 3. Wait for deployment transaction to be mined await counter.waitForDeployment(); // 4. Retrieve deployed address const address = await counter.getAddress(); console.log("Counter deployed to:", address); } main().catch((error) => { console.error(error); process.exitCode = 1; }); ``` ### Hardhat Configuration (`hardhat.config.js`) ```javascript require("@nomicfoundation/hardhat-toolbox"); const SEPOLIA_RPC_URL = process.env.SEPOLIA_RPC_URL; const PRIVATE_KEY = process.env.PRIVATE_KEY; module.exports = { solidity: "0.8.19", networks: { sepolia: { url: SEPOLIA_RPC_URL, accounts: [PRIVATE_KEY], chainId: 11155111 }, polygon: { url: "https://polygon-rpc.com", accounts: [PRIVATE_KEY], chainId: 137 } } }; ``` ### Deployment Command ```bash npx hardhat run scripts/deploy.js --network sepolia ``` ## 3.4 Constructor Function — The Sole Mutable Opportunity The `constructor` is the **only function** that runs during deployment. It: - Receives constructor arguments - Initializes **immutable** variables (stored in code, not storage) - Initializes state variables - Mints initial tokens (e.g., ERC-20 total supply) - Emits an initial event - **Cannot** be called again — the contract cannot be "re-deployed" > [!NOTE] > **Critical security pattern:** If your contract uses an **upgradeable proxy** (e.g., UUPS, Transparent Proxy), the constructor logic is moved to an `initialize()` function protected by an `initializer` modifier from OpenZeppelin. This prevents re-initialization attacks — a known KTU Module 4 exam question. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Deployment Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Developer writes .sol source] --> B[Compile via solc] B --> C{Compilation
successful?} C -- No --> D[Fix syntax
and type errors] D --> A C -- Yes --> E[Generate Bytecode + ABI] E --> F[Local Testing
Hardhat or Foundry] F --> G{All tests
pass?} G -- No --> H[Debug and
refactor logic] H --> A G -- Yes --> I[Testnet Deploy
Sepolia or Holesky] I --> J{Manual QA
on testnet?} J -- No --> H J -- Yes --> K[Audit by
third party] K --> L{Audit
passed?} L -- No --> H L -- Yes --> M[Prepare Mainnet
Deployment Script] M --> N[Connect Wallet
MetaMask or Ledger] N --> O[Sign Deployment
Transaction] O --> P[Broadcast to P2P
Network via RPC] P --> Q[Validators include
in Block] Q --> R[Contract Address
is Finalized] R --> S[Verify Source
on Etherscan] S --> T[Contract Live
and Operational] ``` ## 4.2 EVM Memory Architecture During Deployment ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Transaction Data Field] --> B[EVM Calldata
Buffer] B --> C[Constructor
Execution] C --> D[Stack
1024 items max] C --> E[Memory
Volatile bytes] C --> F[Storage
Persistent SSTORE] C --> G[Code Section
Immutable bytecode] G --> H[Contract Account
on World State] H --> I[State Trie Update] I --> J[Block Finalized] ``` ## 4.3 CREATE vs CREATE2 Opcode Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD Start{Need to pre-compute
address?} -->|Yes| Create2[Use CREATE2
Salt-based] Start -->|No| Standard{Need same address
on multiple chains?} Standard -->|Yes| Create2 Standard -->|No| Classic[Use CREATE
Nonce-based] Create2 --> Note1[Same deployer + salt + initCode
always yields same address] Classic --> Note2[Same deployer + nonce
yields same address on one chain] ``` ## 4.4 Deployment Tool Ecosystem ```mermaid flowchart TD Dev[Developer] --> IDE{Choose Tool} IDE --> Remix[Remix IDE
Browser] IDE --> Hardhat[Hardhat
JavaScript] IDE --> Foundry[Foundry
Rust CLI] IDE --> Truffle[Truffle
Legacy] Remix --> Chain1[Injected Web3
MetaMask] Hardhat --> Chain2[Programmatic
Scripts] Foundry --> Chain3[Forge Script
Solidity tests] Truffle --> Chain4[Truffle Migrate
JS-based] Chain1 --> Net{Target Network} Chain2 --> Net Chain3 --> Net Chain4 --> Net Net --> Local[Local
Hardhat or Anvil] Net --> Test[Testnet
Sepolia or Holesky] Net --> Main[Mainnet
Ethereum or L2] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between `CREATE` and `CREATE2` opcodes used during smart contract deployment. State one practical use case where `CREATE2` is preferred over `CREATE`.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | CREATE | CREATE2 | |---|---|---| | Address formula | `keccak256(RLP([deployer, nonce]))[12:]` | `keccak256(0xFF ‖ deployer ‖ salt ‖ keccak256(init))[12:]` | | Determinism | Depends on nonce (changes per chain) | Salt-based — same address on all EVM chains | | Dependence on deployer history | Yes — changing nonce changes address | No — independent of nonce history | | Use case | Standard single-chain deployment | **Counterfactual deployment** in Layer-2 rollups, cross-chain bridges, and gas-efficient meta-transactions | > **Use Case (1 Mark):** **Gnosis Safe** uses `CREATE2` to deploy a Safe wallet at a pre-computed address *before* the user has paid the gas. The user can then send funds to that address, and the actual deployment only occurs when the user first interacts — saving gas. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **List the four mandatory fields of a smart contract deployment transaction and explain the role of the `data` field.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **`from`** — Address of the EOA paying gas (1 Mark) 2. **`nonce`** — Transaction counter of the sender; prevents replay and influences contract address via CREATE (1 Mark) 3. **`value`** (optional) — ETH sent to the contract at construction, forwarded to the constructor (0.5 Marks) 4. **`data`** — Contains the **compiled bytecode** of the contract; this is the executable code that the EVM will interpret and store in the contract account's `code` section. Empty `to` field + non-empty `data` field uniquely identifies a deployment transaction (0.5 Marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the complete lifecycle of a smart contract deployment from source code authoring to mainnet verification. List all intermediate artifacts and the tools used at each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Solidity contract's compiled bytecode is **12,000 bytes** long, with **7,500 non-zero bytes** and **4,500 zero bytes**. The current gas price is **25 gWei**, and 1 ETH = **₹1,80,000**. Calculate the total deployment cost in gWei, ETH, and INR. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Lifecycle (7 Marks)** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the visual flow.)* The seven stages are: | Stage | Input | Tool / Artifact | Output | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Authoring | — | VS Code / Remix | `.sol` source file | | 2. Compilation | `.sol` | `solc` compiler | Bytecode + ABI + Metadata | | 3. Local Test | Bytecode + ABI | Hardhat / Foundry | Test report, gas estimates | | 4. Testnet Deploy | Bytecode | MetaMask + Sepolia ETH | Testnet contract address | | 5. Wallet Sign | Tx object | MetaMask / Ledger | Signed raw transaction | | 6. Mainnet Broadcast | Signed tx | Infura / Alchemy RPC | Block inclusion, finality | | 7. Verification | Source + bytecode | Etherscan | Green-tick verified contract | **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)** **Given:** - $n_{\text{code}} = 12{,}000\;\text{bytes}$ - $n_{\text{non-zero}} = 7{,}500\;\text{bytes}$ - $n_{\text{zero}} = 4{,}500\;\text{bytes}$ - Gas price $G = 25\;\text{gWei}$ - 1 ETH = ₹1,80,000 **Step 1 — Apply the deployment gas formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} G_{\text{total}} &= 21{,}000 + (16 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 4 \cdot n_{\text{zero}}) + 32{,}000 + 200 \cdot n_{\text{code}} \\[4pt] &= 21{,}000 + (16 \cdot 7{,}500 + 4 \cdot 4{,}500) + 32{,}000 + 200 \cdot 12{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 21{,}000 + (120{,}000 + 18{,}000) + 32{,}000 + 2{,}400{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 21{,}000 + 138{,}000 + 32{,}000 + 2{,}400{,}000 \\[4pt] &= 2{,}591{,}000\;\text{gas} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert gas to gWei (cost):** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{gWei}} = 2{,}591{,}000 \times 25 = 64{,}775{,}000\;\text{gWei} $$ **Step 3 — Convert gWei to ETH (divide by $10^9$):** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{ETH}} = \frac{64{,}775{,}000}{10^{9}} = 0.064775\;\text{ETH} $$ **Step 4 — Convert ETH to INR:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{INR}} = 0.064775 \times 1{,}80{,}000 = \text{₹}\,11{,}659.50 $$ **Final Answer:** Total deployment cost = **2,591,000 gas ≈ 0.0648 ETH ≈ ₹11,659.50** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Explain the `CREATE2` opcode with its address derivation formula. Describe a real-world scenario where `CREATE2` enables **gas-efficient counterfactual deployment**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the security implications of deploying smart contracts. List **at least five best practices** that must be followed before a mainnet deployment. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) CREATE2 Opcode & Counterfactual Deployment (7 Marks)** **Definition (2 Marks):** `CREATE2` (EIP-1014) is an EVM opcode that computes a contract's address based on four inputs: a fixed `0xFF` byte prefix, the deployer's address, a 32-byte salt chosen by the deployer, and the `keccak256` hash of the init code. The same combination of inputs always yields the same address, regardless of when or on which EVM-compatible chain the deployment occurs. **Address Derivation Formula (2 Marks):** $$ \text{Address}_{\text{CREATE2}} = \text{keccak256}\big(0xFF \;\Vert\; \text{deployer} \;\Vert\; \text{salt}_{32} \;\Vert\; \text{keccak256(initCode)}\big)[12..31] $$ **Real-World Scenario — Counterfactual Deployment (3 Marks):** **Scenario: Layer-2 Rollup Wallet Creation (e.g., Argent Wallet on StarkNet / zkSync).** In L2 environments, every on-chain operation costs gas, but the goal is to onboard users even *before* they have funds. The flow is: 1. The wallet provider **pre-computes** the user's wallet address using `CREATE2` with a unique salt (e.g., the user's email or UUID). 2. The user is shown their future wallet address and can **receive funds** to it — *without* the contract existing on-chain. 3. When the user makes their **first transaction** (e.g., a transfer or swap), the wallet contract is deployed on-chain using `CREATE2` with the same salt. The user's pre-existing balance is now accessible. **Benefit:** Gas is paid only when the user is ready to transact, not at signup. The address remains deterministic and the user experience is seamless. **(b) Security Best Practices Before Mainnet Deployment (7 Marks)** 1. **Third-Party Security Audit** — Engage firms like OpenZeppelin, Trail of Bits, or CertiK to perform a manual and automated review of the contract code. 2. **Extensive Test Coverage** — Aim for >95% line and branch coverage using Hardhat, Foundry, or Truffle. Include fuzz tests (Foundry's `forge fuzz`) to detect edge cases. 3. **Use Established Libraries** — Prefer audited implementations from **OpenZeppelin** (ERC-20, ERC-721, AccessControl) over custom code. 4. **Deploy to Testnet First** — Use Sepolia or Holesky to validate real network behavior; do not skip this stage. 5. **Verify Source Code on Etherscan** — Upload flattened source code with the exact compiler version and optimization settings. This enables public scrutiny and transparency. 6. **Use a Multi-Signature Wallet for Ownership** — Set the contract `owner` to a Gnosis Safe multi-sig (e.g., 3-of-5) to prevent single-point-of-failure in admin functions. 7. **Implement a Bug Bounty Program** — Platforms like **Code4rena** and **Immunefi** offer pre-deployment bug bounty campaigns to crowdsource vulnerability discovery. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `gas` and `gWei`** — Gas is a *unit of computational work*; gWei is a *denomination of ETH*. The full sentence must read *"gas price in gWei"*, not *"gas in gWei"*. (–1 Mark deduction) > 2. **Forgetting the `32,000` contract creation surcharge** — Many students compute only the calldata cost and forget the EVM's fixed `CREATE` cost. (–2 Marks deduction) > 3. **Using pre-Istanbul gas constants** — The non-zero byte cost is **16** (post-2020), not 68. Writing 68 is a fatal KTU error. (–1 Mark deduction) > 4. **Confusing deployment with invocation** — Deploying a contract is a *transaction*; calling a function is a *message call*. The gas math is **different**. (–1 Mark deduction) > 5. **Skipping the `RLP` step in CREATE derivation** — The address is the last 20 bytes of `keccak256(RLP([deployer, nonce]))`, not `keccak256(deployer + nonce)`. (–1 Mark deduction) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Deploying Smart Contracts** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - [x] **Smart Contract Deployment** = uploading compiled bytecode to a blockchain, creating a permanent contract account. - [x] **Deployment transaction** has `to = empty` and `data = bytecode`. - [x] **Constructor** runs exactly **once** at deployment; cannot be re-invoked. - [x] **Bytecode is immutable** post-deployment — upgrades require proxy patterns. ### 📐 Key Formulas - [x] **CREATE Address** = `keccak256(RLP([deployer, nonce]))[12..31]` - [x] **CREATE2 Address** = `keccak256(0xFF ‖ deployer ‖ salt ‖ keccak256(init))[12..31]` - [x] **Total Deployment Gas** = $53{,}000 + 216 \cdot n_{\text{non-zero}} + 204 \cdot n_{\text{zero}}$ - [x] **Ethereum Code Size Limit** = 24,576 bytes - [x] **EVM Word Size** = 256 bits ### 🛠️ Tools & Frameworks - [x] **Remix IDE** → Beginners, browser-based - [x] **Hardhat** → Industry-standard, TypeScript - [x] **Foundry** → Auditors, gas optimization, Solidity tests - [x] **Truffle** → Legacy projects - [x] **MetaMask** → Wallet for signing deployment transactions ### 🔐 Security Pillars - [x] Audit before mainnet - [x] Use OpenZeppelin libraries - [x] Multi-sig ownership - [x] Source code verification on Etherscan - [x] Bug bounty programs ### ⚠️ Common KTU Traps - [x] Distinguish gas (unit) from gWei (currency) - [x] Post-Istanbul non-zero byte cost = **16** (not 68) - [x] Always include the **32,000** CREATE surcharge - [x] CREATE2 ≠ CREATE — different formula, different determinism - [x] Deployment ≠ Function Call — different gas models ---

topics.title: Ethereum - The Ethereum Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethereum - The Ethereum Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum** is an open-source, decentralized, **Turing-complete** blockchain platform introduced by **Vitalik Buterin** in late **2013** and launched on **July 30, 2015**. It extends the core innovation of the Bitcoin blockchain — a decentralized, trustless consensus system — by adding a generalized **state-transition** function via a quasi-Turing-complete virtual machine (the **EVM**). Unlike Bitcoin, which is primarily a peer-to-peer electronic cash system, Ethereum's design intent is to function as a *world computer* capable of executing arbitrary, user-defined programs known as **smart contracts**.

### 1.1.1 The Ethereum Network — Formal Definition

The **Ethereum Network** is the peer-to-peer overlay network of **nodes** that:
1. Maintain a synchronized copy of the Ethereum blockchain ledger.
2. Execute and validate transactions through the **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)**.
3. Reach consensus on the global state using a consensus protocol (**Ethash / Proof-of-Work** originally; transitioned to **Proof-of-Stake** via *The Merge* in **September 2022**).
4. Use an internal accounting unit called **Ether (ETH)** and a computational pricing unit called **Gas**.

The network is governed by a protocol specification (the **Yellow Paper** by **Dr. Gavin Wood**, with ongoing updates through **EIPs — Ethereum Improvement Proposals**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Bitcoin:** Bitcoin's Script language is intentionally *not* Turing-complete (no unbounded loops). Ethereum achieves Turing-completeness by introducing a metering mechanism (Gas) that terminates infinite loops by running out of funds, ensuring the system remains *quasi*-Turing-complete and deterministic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Ethereum as a Global, Decentralized Vending Machine**

Imagine a **giant vending machine** that exists simultaneously in every city on Earth, with no single owner:

| Component | Real-World Analogy | Ethereum Equivalent |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Vending machine | Physical device | **EVM (Ethereum Virtual Machine)** |
| Items inside | Products for sale | **Smart Contracts / dApps** |
| Money inserted | Coins | **Ether (ETH)** |
| Energy/KwH meter | Power consumption dial | **Gas (computational cost)** |
| Rules of operation | Programmed inventory rules | **Smart Contract Code (Solidity)** |
| Customer's identity | Anonymous buyer | **Externally Owned Account (EOA)** |
| Network of machines | Franchise chain | **Peer-to-peer Node Network** |

**How it works intuitively:**
- A customer (user with an EOA) inserts money (ETH) into the vending machine (sends a transaction).
- They pay a small fee proportional to the electricity used (Gas).
- The machine checks its own rules (executes the smart contract).
- All other franchise machines (nodes) verify the same transaction happened, ensuring no cheating.
- The product is dispensed (state is updated) and the global inventory is synchronized across all locations.

Just as you trust the vending machine because of *its own logic* (not the vendor), Ethereum users trust transactions because of *mathematical consensus*, not because of any single authority.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Ethereum

The following metrics are **standardized in the Yellow Paper** and are essential for KTU examinations:

- **Average Block Time:** **~12 seconds** (post-Merge; previously ~13–15 s with Ethash/PoW)
- **Block Gas Limit:** Variable, but target ≈ **30,000,000 Gas** (as of the London Upgrade)
- **Base Fee Floor:** Determined by EIP-1559 dynamics
- **Native Currency:** **Ether (ETH)** — smallest unit is **Wei (1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei)**
- **Gas Unit:** Smallest unit of computational effort
- **Total ETH Supply:** No hard cap (issues ~4.5% annually pre-Merge, reduced post-Merge)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **Block Gas Limit** (maximum Gas allowed in one block) with the **Gas Limit** field a user sets on a transaction (maximum Gas the sender is willing to spend on that transaction).

---

## 1.4 Logical Architecture of the Ethereum Network (Top-Level View)

The Ethereum network is structured into several interacting layers:

1. **Application Layer** — dApps, wallets (MetaMask), web interfaces
2. **Smart Contract Layer** — Solidity/Vyper code deployed as bytecode
3. **Execution Layer (EL)** — EVM runs smart contract bytecode on each node
4. **Consensus Layer (CL)** — Beacon chain / PoS validators (post-Merge)
5. **Networking Layer** — DevP2P protocol for node discovery & gossiping
6. **Data Layer** — The blockchain itself (blocks, transactions, state trie)

This **layered separation** is a critical KTU exam topic, especially after The Merge.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ethereum State Machine — Formal Model

Ethereum is mathematically modeled as a **transaction-based state machine**, identical in spirit to Bitcoin's UTXO model but generalized to support arbitrary contract execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Model:**
> $$\sigma_{t+1} \;=\; \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$$
> where:
> - $\sigma_t$ = the global Ethereum state at block height $t$
> - $T$ = a *set* of valid transactions in block $t+1$
> - $\Upsilon$ = the **state-transition function** (executed by the EVM)
> - $\sigma_{t+1}$ = the resulting new state

The **state** $\sigma$ is formally a tuple:
$$\sigma \;=\; (\mu, \; \text{Accounts})$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = machine state (memory, stack, program counter, gas remaining)
- **Accounts** = a mapping from **160-bit address** $\rightarrow$ account object

---

## 2.2 The Two Account Types (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> Ethereum has **two fundamentally different account types** — this is one of the most frequently asked topics in KTU Module 4.

| Feature | **Externally Owned Account (EOA)** | **Contract Account** |
|---------|-----------------------------------|----------------------|
| Controlled by | Private key (human/user) | Contract code (logic) |
| Has associated code? | **No** | **Yes** (EVM bytecode) |
| Can initiate transactions? | **Yes** (always) | **No** (only as a *result* of an internal call/message) |
| Has Ether balance? | Yes | Yes |
| Has storage? | No | Yes (persistent key-value store) |
| Triggered by | EOA owner action | Message call / transaction |

Each account contains four fields:
$$\text{Account} \;=\; (\text{nonce},\; \text{balance},\; \text{storageRoot},\; \text{codeHash})$$

| Field | EOA | Contract |
|-------|-----|----------|
| `nonce` | Number of transactions sent | Number of contracts created |
| `balance` | Wei owned | Wei held by contract |
| `storageRoot` | Hash of empty trie | Hash of contract's storage trie |
| `codeHash` | Hash of empty string | Hash of EVM bytecode |

---

## 2.3 The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)

The EVM is a **simple, stack-based, 256-bit word machine** that runs bytecode compiled from high-level languages like **Solidity** or **Vyper**.

### 2.3.1 EVM Execution Context

$$\mu \;=\; (g, \; pc, \; mem, \; s, \; \text{stack})$$

Where:
- $g$ = available Gas (decrements with each opcode)
- $pc$ = Program Counter
- $mem$ = Volatile byte-addressed memory
- $s$ = Stack (max 1024 elements, each 256-bit)
- $\text{stack}$ = the LIFO operand stack

### 2.3.2 Three Types of Storage

1. **Stack** — Free to read/write, max 1024 deep, 256-bit words.
2. **Memory** — Volatile, byte-addressable, expanded in 32-byte words (costs quadratic Gas).
3. **Storage** — Persistent, key-value (256-bit → 256-bit), the most expensive (≈ **20,000 Gas** for new slot, **5,000 Gas** for modification after Berlin upgrade).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** *"**S**tack is free, **M**emory is medium, **S**torage is **S**alty (expensive)."*

---

## 2.4 Gas Mechanism — The Heart of Ethereum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas** is the unit measuring the computational effort required to execute specific operations on the Ethereum network. Every opcode has a fixed Gas cost (defined in the Yellow Paper).

### 2.4.1 Gas Formula (Pre-EIP-1559 — Legacy)

$$\text{Tx Fee} \;=\; \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$$

### 2.4.2 Gas Formula (Post-EIP-1559 — London Upgrade, August 2021)

$$\text{Tx Fee} \;=\; \text{Gas Used} \times (\text{Base Fee} + \text{Tip})$$

$$\text{Tip (Priority Fee)} \;=\; \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Max Priority Fee Per Gas}$$

$$\text{Max Fee Per Gas} \;=\; \text{Base Fee} + \text{Max Priority Fee Per Gas}$$

Where:
- **Base Fee** = algorithmically determined (burned 🔥)
- **Tip / Priority Fee** = paid to the validator
- **Max Fee** = user's upper bound to prevent overpaying

### 2.4.3 Base Fee Adjustment (EIP-1559)

$$\text{Base Fee}_{n+1} \;=\; \text{Base Fee}_n \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{8} \times \frac{\text{Block Used} - \text{Target}}{\text{Target}}\right)$$

- If blocks are > 50% full → Base Fee increases (max +12.5% per block)
- If blocks are < 50% full → Base Fee decreases (max -12.5% per block)
- The **15,000,000 Gas** target per block is hardcoded.

---

## 2.5 Transaction Structure (High-Yield)

A standard **EIP-1559 transaction** contains:

| Field | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `nonce` | Count of txs sent from sender's EOA |
| `to` | Recipient address (20 bytes) |
| `value` | Amount of Wei to send |
| `data` | Optional payload (used for contract calls/deploys) |
| `gasLimit` | Maximum Gas the user authorizes |
| `maxPriorityFeePerGas` | Tip to validator |
| `maxFeePerGas` | Total max price per Gas unit |
| `v, r, s` | ECDSA signature (secp256k1) |

> [!NOTE]
> A transaction with a `to` field equal to `null/zero address` is a **Contract Creation Transaction**; the `data` field contains the init bytecode.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Print/save this table — these are the equations most commonly asked in KTU ESE questions for this module.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | State transition | $\sigma_{t+1} = \Upsilon(\sigma_t, T)$ | Global state update |
| 2 | Wei to Ether | $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei}$ | Smallest indivisible unit |
| 3 | Legacy tx fee | $\text{Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times \text{GasPrice}$ | Wei |
| 4 | EIP-1559 fee | $\text{Fee} = \text{GasUsed} \times (\text{BaseFee} + \text{Tip})$ | Wei |
| 5 | Base fee growth (max) | $\text{BaseFee}_{n+1} \leq 1.125 \times \text{BaseFee}_n$ | Per block cap |
| 6 | Base fee decline (max) | $\text{BaseFee}_{n+1} \geq 0.875 \times \text{BaseFee}_n$ | Per block floor |
| 7 | Block target | $15{,}000{,}000$ Gas | Post London upgrade |
| 8 | Block gas limit (avg) | $\approx 30{,}000{,}000$ Gas | Network-wide constraint |
| 9 | SSTORE cold (new slot) | $20{,}000$ Gas | Berlin EIP-2929 |
| 10 | SSTORE warm (modify) | $5{,}000$ Gas | Berlin EIP-2929 |
| 11 | Average block time (post-Merge) | $\approx 12 \text{ seconds}$ | Slot time (1 epoch = 32 slots) |
| 12 | Account address size | $160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ | Keccak-256 of public key (last 20 bytes) |
| 13 | EVM word size | $256 \text{ bits}$ | Stack/memory/word |
| 14 | Stack depth | $\leq 1024$ items | Causes `StackOverflow` if exceeded |
| 15 | Transaction throughput | $\approx 30 \text{ TPS}$ | Theoretical maximum |
| 16 | Issuance (post-Merge) | $\approx 0.4 - 0.7$ ETH/day | Variable via EIP-1559 burn offset |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The Ethereum network enables:

- **Decentralized Finance (DeFi):** Lending, DEXs (Uniswap), stablecoins (DAI).
- **NFTs:** ERC-721 / ERC-1155 standards for digital art, identity.
- **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations):** On-chain governance.
- **Supply Chain Tracking:** VeChain, IBM Food Trust.
- **Digital Identity:** Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) via ENS, Spruce ID.
- **Layer-2 Scaling:** Rollups (Optimism, Arbitrum) that settle on Ethereum L1.
- **Tokenization:** Real-world assets (RWA) — bonds, real estate, carbon credits.

In academic/research engineering, Ethereum serves as a **trustless compute layer**, a **cryptographic settlement layer**, and a **public data availability substrate**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transaction Fee Calculation (EIP-1559)

> This is the **most frequently asked numerical problem** in KTU Module 4. We work through it end-to-end.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A user sends a transaction on Ethereum post-London upgrade. The user sets:
- `gasLimit = 100,000`
- `maxFeePerGas = 50 Gwei`
- `maxPriorityFeePerGas = 3 Gwei`
- The current network `baseFee = 20 Gwei`
- The actual `gasUsed = 87,000` (after execution)

Find: (i) the **effective gas price**, (ii) the **total transaction fee** in ETH, (iii) the **amount burned**, (iv) the **validator tip**, and (v) the **refund to the user**.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Validate the user's maxFee covers baseFee + tip**

We require:
$$\text{maxFeePerGas} \;\geq\; \text{baseFee} + \text{maxPriorityFeePerGas}$$

Substitute:
$$50 \;\geq\; 20 + 3 \;=\; 23 \;\;\checkmark$$

The transaction is valid. Proceed.

---

**Step 2: Compute the effective gas price**

The user pays the *lesser* of:
- `maxFeePerGas` (50 Gwei)
- `baseFee + maxPriorityFeePerGas` (20 + 3 = 23 Gwei)

$$\text{Effective Gas Price} \;=\; \min(50,\; 23) \;=\; 23 \text{ Gwei}$$

**Reason:** Ethereum refunds the difference between `maxFee` and the actual cost. Paying the lower value prevents the user from overpaying when baseFee is low.

---

**Step 3: Compute total transaction fee (in Wei)**

$$\text{Total Fee} \;=\; \text{gasUsed} \times \text{Effective Gas Price}$$

$$\text{Total Fee} \;=\; 87{,}000 \times 23 \text{ Gwei}$$

$$\text{Total Fee} \;=\; 2{,}001{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

Convert Gwei → Wei (1 Gwei = 10⁹ Wei):
$$\text{Total Fee} \;=\; 2{,}001{,}000 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} \;=\; 2.001 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

Convert Wei → ETH (1 ETH = 10¹⁸ Wei):
$$\text{Total Fee} \;=\; \frac{2.001 \times 10^{15}}{10^{18}} \text{ ETH} \;=\; 2.001 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total Fee} = 0.002001 \text{ ETH}}$$

---

**Step 4: Compute burned amount (Base Fee portion)**

$$\text{Burned} \;=\; \text{gasUsed} \times \text{baseFee}$$
$$\text{Burned} \;=\; 87{,}000 \times 20 \text{ Gwei} \;=\; 1{,}740{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

Convert:
$$\text{Burned} \;=\; 1{,}740{,}000 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} \;=\; 1.74 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Burned} = 0.00174 \text{ ETH}}$$

---

**Step 5: Compute validator tip (Priority Fee portion)**

$$\text{Tip} \;=\; \text{gasUsed} \times \text{maxPriorityFeePerGas}$$
$$\text{Tip} \;=\; 87{,}000 \times 3 \text{ Gwei} \;=\; 261{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

Convert:
$$\text{Tip} \;=\; 261{,}000 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} \;=\; 2.61 \times 10^{14} \text{ Wei}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Tip} = 0.000261 \text{ ETH}}$$

---

**Step 6: Compute refund to user**

The user authorized a max fee of `100,000 gas × 50 Gwei = 5,000,000 Gwei`. They only used `2,001,000 Gwei`. The remainder is refunded to the EOA.

$$\text{Refund} \;=\; 5{,}000{,}000 - 2{,}001{,}000 \;=\; 2{,}999{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

Convert:
$$\text{Refund} \;=\; 2{,}999{,}000 \times 10^{9} \text{ Wei} \;=\; 2.999 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Refund} = 0.002999 \text{ ETH}}$$

---

**Step 7: Verification — Conservation of value**

$$\text{Burned} + \text{Tip} + \text{Refund} \;=\; 0.00174 + 0.000261 + 0.002999$$
$$= 0.005000 \text{ ETH} \;\;\checkmark$$

This equals the user's **maximum authorized payment** (100,000 × 50 Gwei = 0.005 ETH). 

> [!NOTE]
> The identity `Burned + Tip + Refund = maxFeePerGas × gasLimit` is the **conservation-of-funds invariant** that KTU examiners often test.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Consensus Mechanism Comparison

A frequent KTU question is: *"Compare Ethereum's pre-Merge and post-Merge consensus."* Use this matrix.

| Parameter | **Pre-Merge (Ethash / PoW)** | **Post-Merge (Casper FFG / PoS)** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Mechanism | Miners solve cryptographic puzzle | Validators stake 32 ETH |
| Energy consumption | ~ **78 TWh/year** | ~ **0.01 TWh/year** (~99.95% reduction) |
| Block producer | Miner who finds nonce first | Validator selected by RANDAO + fork-choice rule |
| Finality | Probabilistic (~6 confirmations ≈ ~78 s) | **Economic finality** (2 epochs ≈ **12.8 min**) |
| Issuance | ~ 2 ETH per block | ~ 0.4–0.7 ETH/day (network-wide) |
| Slashing | Not applicable | Validators lose stake for malicious behavior |
| Consensus algorithm | Ethash (DAG-based) | Casper FFG + LMD GHOST |
| Block time | ~ 13.3 s | **12 s** (slot) |
| Attack cost | 51% hashpower (~$ billions) | 51% of staked ETH (~$ billions) |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Network Nodes

| Node Type | Storage Requirement | Function | Who runs it? |
|-----------|--------------------|----------|--------------|
| **Full Node** | ~ **1 TB+** (post-Merge) | Stores entire blockchain + verifies all rules | Independent operators, exchanges |
| **Archive Node** | ~ **12–15 TB+** | Full node + historical state at every block | Block explorers, analytics |
| **Light Node** | < 1 GB | Stores only block headers; relies on full nodes for state | Wallets (mobile/light clients) |
| **Validator Node** | ~ 1 TB + 32 ETH stake | Produces/attests blocks; earns rewards | Stakers, staking pools |

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Block Validation Economics (PoS Reward)

The validator reward (simplified) is computed as:

$$R_{\text{validator}} \;=\; R_{\text{base}} + R_{\text{attestation}} + R_{\text{tip}}$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{base}}$ = base issuance for the block proposal
- $R_{\text{attestation}}$ = reward for inclusion in next epoch
- $R_{\text{tip}}$ = priority fees from transactions in the block

The validator's **effective balance** is bounded above by **32 ETH**:
$$B_{\text{effective}} \;=\; \min(B_{\text{actual}},\; 32 \text{ ETH})$$

Excess above 32 ETH remains in the validator's account but does *not* contribute to active stake. This is a non-trivial detail often tested.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Ethereum Network Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **block-level functional architecture** of the Ethereum network, illustrating how users, applications, smart contracts, the EVM, and the consensus layer interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APPLAYER["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        wallet1["MetaMask Wallet"]
        dapp1["Uniswap DEX dApp"]
        dapp2["OpenSea NFT dApp"]
    end

    subgraph CONTRACLAYER["SMART CONTRACT LAYER"]
        contractA["ERC-20 Token Contract"]
        contractB["ERC-721 NFT Contract"]
        contractC["DeFi Lending Pool"]
    end

    subgraph EXECLAYER["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        evm1["Ethereum Virtual Machine EVM"]
        statetrie1["World State Trie"]
        memp1["Mempool Transaction Pool"]
    end

    subgraph CONSLAYER["CONSENSUS LAYER POST MERGE"]
        beacon1["Beacon Chain Casper FFG"]
        val1["Validator Node Staked 32 ETH"]
        val2["Validator Node Staked 32 ETH"]
    end

    subgraph NETLAYER["NETWORKING LAYER P2P"]
        gossip1["DevP2P Gossip Protocol"]
        discov1["Node Discovery Disc v5"]
    end

    subgraph DATALAYER["DATA LAYER BLOCKCHAIN"]
        block1["Block N Block Header"]
        block2["Block N+1 Block Header"]
        block3["Block N+2 Block Header"]
    end

    wallet1 -->|"Signs Tx"| memp1
    dapp1 -->|"Calls"| contractA
    dapp2 -->|"Calls"| contractB
    contractA -->|"Runs Bytecode"| evm1
    contractB -->|"Runs Bytecode"| evm1
    contractC -->|"Runs Bytecode"| evm1
    memp1 -->|"Pick Top Gas Txs"| evm1
    evm1 -->|"Reads / Writes"| statetrie1
    evm1 -->|"New State Root"| beacon1
    val1 -->|"Attests Block"| beacon1
    val2 -->|"Attests Block"| beacon1
    beacon1 -->|"Finalizes"| block2
    gossip1 -.->|"Propagates Txs"| memp1
    discov1 -.->|"Finds Peers"| gossip1
    statetrie1 -->|"Persisted In"| block2
    block1 --> block2
    block2 --> block3
```

---

## 4.2 Transaction Lifecycle Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **end-to-end transaction lifecycle** — from a user signing a transaction to finalization in the Beacon chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Step A: User Signs Tx With Private Key ECDSA"]
    stepB["Step B: Tx Broadcasted To P2P Network DevP2P"]
    stepC["Step C: Tx Enters Mempool Of Other Nodes"]
    stepD["Step D: Validator Selects Highest Paying Tx"]
    stepE["Step E: EVM Executes Tx Updates State Trie"]
    stepF["Step F: Block Proposed With New State Root"]
    stepG["Step G: Validators Attest In Current Epoch"]
    stepH["Step H: Epoch Justified After 2 Epochs"]
    stepI["Step I: Block Finalized 12.8 Minutes"]
    stepJ["Step J: Tx Irreversible"]

    stepA -->|"Propagate"| stepB
    stepB -->|"Queue"| stepC
    stepC -->|"Order by Base Fee Plus Tip"| stepD
    stepD -->|"Run EVM Bytecode"| stepE
    stepE -->|"Propose"| stepF
    stepF -->|"Attest"| stepG
    stepG -->|"2 Epochs"| stepH
    stepH -->|"Finalize"| stepI
    stepI -->|"Permanent"| stepJ
```

---

## 4.3 Account State Transition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stateA["State Before Tx sigma_t"]
    stateB["EOA Sender: nonce n to n+1, balance minus amount minus fee"]
    stateC["EOA Recipient: balance plus amount"]
    stateD["Contract Account: storage updated, balance minus gas"]
    stateE["State After Tx sigma_t plus 1"]
    valA["Validator: balance plus tip, base fee burned"]

    stateA --> stateB
    stateA --> stateC
    stateA --> stateD
    stateB --> stateE
    stateC --> stateE
    stateD --> stateE
    stateB -->|"Base Fee Burned"| valA
    stateD -->|"Tip Paid"| valA
```

---

## 4.4 EVM Execution Stack Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA["Solidity Source Code .sol"]
    inputB["Compiler solc"]
    inputC["EVM Bytecode Stack Based 256 bit"]
    inputD["Execution Context mu = g pc mem stack"]
    opA["Stack LIFO Max 1024 Items"]
    opB["Memory Volatile Byte Addressable"]
    opC["Storage Persistent KV 256 bit"]
    outA["State Transition sigma t plus 1 equals Upsilon of sigma t T"]

    inputA -->|"Compiled By"| inputB
    inputB -->|"Produces"| inputC
    inputC -->|"Runs Inside"| inputD
    inputD -->|"Uses"| opA
    inputD -->|"Uses"| opB
    inputD -->|"Reads Writes"| opC
    inputD -->|"Produces"| outA
```

---

## 4.5 Gas Lifecycle Diagram (EIP-1559)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1["User Specifies gasLimit and maxFeePerGas and maxPriorityFeePerGas"]
    baseF["Network Base Fee Algorithmically Set"]
    effF["Effective Fee = min maxFeePerGas baseFee + maxPriority"]
    burnF["Base Fee Portion Burned ETH Removed Forever"]
    tipF["Priority Fee Portion Paid To Validator Proposer"]
    refF["Remainder = maxFee minus effective Fee Refunded To User"]

    user1 --> effF
    baseF --> effF
    effF -->|"Split"| burnF
    effF -->|"Split"| tipF
    user1 --> refF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution Note:** Part A carries 2 × 3 = 6 marks total per university exam, and 1 × 3 = 3 marks in internals. Answers should be 1 short paragraph (3–5 lines) with a labeled diagram where possible.

---

### Q1. Define the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM). Explain how it achieves Turing-completeness safely. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)** is a **quasi-Turing-complete, stack-based, 256-bit virtual machine** that executes bytecode compiled from high-level smart contract languages (e.g., Solidity). It is the runtime environment of the Ethereum network.

While Bitcoin's Script is intentionally **not** Turing-complete (no loops), the EVM allows arbitrary computation including loops and recursion. To prevent infinite loops (the *halting problem*), Ethereum introduces **Gas** — every opcode costs a fixed amount of Gas, and the transaction sets a hard upper limit (`gasLimit`). When Gas runs out, execution halts with an `Out-of-Gas` exception and the state is reverted. Thus, the EVM is **bounded-quasi-Turing-complete** — Turing-complete in theory, finite in practice.

*(Examiner allocates: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Turing-completeness explanation, 1 mark for Gas safety mechanism.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between EOA and Contract Accounts in Ethereum. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **EOA (Externally Owned Account)** | **Contract Account** |
|---------|-------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Controlled by | Private key of a human/user | Code deployed on blockchain |
| Can initiate transactions? | **Yes** | **No** (only as a message call) |
| Has code? | **No** | **Yes** (EVM bytecode) |
| Has storage? | **No** | **Yes** (persistent storage) |
| nonce | Number of transactions sent | Number of contracts created |

*Each contrastive point: 0.5 marks × 5 = 2.5 marks; 0.5 marks for crisp conclusion.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Part B Rule:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE full question** (both sub-parts) from a choice of **2** — meaning you only answer the question you choose, but BOTH sub-parts of it. Below, Question A and Question B are TWO independent 14-mark choices.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks): Ethereum Architecture & Gas `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the Ethereum Network**. Differentiate between the **Execution Layer** and **Consensus Layer** post The Merge.

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of Ethereum Network (post-Merge):**

The Ethereum network is organized as a **multi-layered stack**:

1. **Application Layer** — End-user dApps, wallets, and frontends that interact with smart contracts.
2. **Smart Contract Layer** — Self-executing contracts written in Solidity/Vyper, deployed as EVM bytecode at specific addresses.
3. **Execution Layer (EL)** — Houses the EVM, mempool, transaction processing, and the world state trie. Replaces the old "Ethash + EVM" engine.
4. **Consensus Layer (CL)** — Houses the Beacon Chain running **Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST** Proof-of-Stake protocol. Validators are organized into committees per slot/epoch.
5. **Networking Layer** — Implements **DevP2P** for peer discovery and transaction/block gossiping.
6. **Data Layer** — The replicated chain of blocks containing transactions and state roots.

**Execution vs Consensus Layer:**

| Aspect | **Execution Layer** | **Consensus Layer** |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Primary role | Executes transactions, computes state | Orders blocks, finalizes chain |
| Consensus algo | None (relies on CL) | Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST |
| Components | EVM, mempool, state trie | Beacon chain, validators, fork-choice rule |
| Block producer | Sequencer (validator selected) | Validator chosen by RANDAO |
| Communication | Engine API (to CL) | Engine API (to EL) |

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

```
[Application Layer: Wallets, dApps]
            ↓
[Smart Contract Layer: Solidity contracts]
            ↓
[Execution Layer: EVM + Mempool + State Trie]  ⇄  [Consensus Layer: Beacon Chain + Validators]
            ↓                                          ↓
[Networking Layer: DevP2P Gossip]   [Data Layer: Blockchain]
```

*(Mark allocation: 3 marks layered description, 2 marks EL vs CL table, 2 marks block diagram.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A user submits an **EIP-1559 transaction** with `gasLimit = 210,000`, `maxFeePerGas = 100 Gwei`, `maxPriorityFeePerGas = 5 Gwei`. The current `baseFee = 30 Gwei`. After execution, only `180,000` Gas was used. Calculate:
(i) Effective gas price, (ii) Total fee in ETH, (iii) Burned amount, (iv) Validator tip, (v) Refund to user.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Validate:**
$$100 \text{ Gwei} \;\geq\; 30 + 5 = 35 \text{ Gwei} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Effective gas price:**
$$\text{Effective} = \min(100,\; 30 + 5) = \min(100,\; 35) = 35 \text{ Gwei}$$

**Step 3 — Total fee:**
$$\text{Total} = 180{,}000 \times 35 \text{ Gwei} = 6{,}300{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$
$$= 6.3 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei} = 6.3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Total Fee} = 0.0063 \text{ ETH}}$$

**Step 4 — Burned:**
$$180{,}000 \times 30 \text{ Gwei} = 5{,}400{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 5.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ ETH}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Burned} = 0.0054 \text{ ETH}}$$

**Step 5 — Tip:**
$$180{,}000 \times 5 \text{ Gwei} = 900{,}000 \text{ Gwei} = 9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ ETH}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Tip} = 0.0009 \text{ ETH}}$$

**Step 6 — Refund:**
$$\text{Refund} = (210{,}000 - 180{,}000) \times 100 \text{ Gwei} = 30{,}000 \times 100 \text{ Gwei} = 3 \times 10^{15} \text{ Wei}$$
$$\boxed{\text{Refund} = 0.003 \text{ ETH}}$$

**Step 7 — Verification:**
$$0.0054 + 0.0009 + 0.003 = 0.0093 \text{ ETH} \;=\; 210{,}000 \times 100 \text{ Gwei} = 0.0093 \text{ ETH} \;\; \checkmark$$

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for setup/effective price, 3 marks for total/burned/tip, 2 marks for refund + verification.)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks): Accounts, EVM, and State `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the **Ethereum account model** in detail. Describe the fields of an account state and the **world state trie** structure.

**Model Answer:**

**Ethereum Account Model (4 marks):**

Ethereum uses an **account-based model** (in contrast to Bitcoin's UTXO model). The state of the system is the set of all accounts. There are **two types**:

1. **Externally Owned Account (EOA):** Controlled by a private key, no code, can initiate transactions.
2. **Contract Account:** Controlled by code stored on-chain, has associated bytecode, executes when called.

**Account State Fields (2 marks):**

Each account is a 4-tuple:
$$\text{Account} = (n, \; b, \; s, \; c)$$

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `n` (nonce) | Tx count for EOA; contracts created for CA |
| `b` (balance) | Wei held |
| `s` (storageRoot) | 256-bit hash of the account's storage trie root |
| `c` (codeHash) | Hash of EVM bytecode (empty for EOA) |

**World State Trie (1 mark):**

The world state is a cryptographic **Merkle Patricia Trie** mapping **160-bit addresses → account states**. Its root hash (`stateRoot`) is stored in every block header, providing:
- **Tamper-evidence:** Any change in any account alters the root.
- **Merkle proofs:** Light clients can verify account contents with $O(\log n)$ proof size.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Describe the **EVM execution model** including the **stack, memory, and storage**. With an example, explain how the `SSTORE` opcode works and why it is the most expensive operation.

**Model Answer:**

**EVM Execution Model (3 marks):**

The EVM is a **stack machine**. The execution context is:
$$\mu = (g,\; pc,\; mem,\; stack)$$

- **Stack:** LIFO, max depth 1024, each element 256-bit. Opcodes pop operands, push results.
- **Memory:** Volatile, byte-addressable, expandable in 32-byte words. Cleared after each tx call. Expansion is quadratically priced.
- **Storage:** Persistent, key-value (256-bit), tied to a contract's address. The only state that survives across transactions.

**SSTORE in detail (3 marks):**

`SSTORE(key, value)` writes a 256-bit value at a 256-bit key in the contract's storage.

**Cost structure (post-Berlin / EIP-2929):**

| Operation | Gas Cost |
|-----------|----------|
| SSTORE on **cold** slot, first-time set | **22,100** (20,000 base + 2,100 cold access) |
| SSTORE on **warm** slot, modify | **5,000** |
| SSTORE on warm slot, same value | **200** |
| SLOAD on cold slot | **2,100** |
| SLOAD on warm slot | **100** |

**Example:**
A contract `Counter` does:
```
SSTORE(0x0, 0x5)   // first time: 22,100 Gas
SSTORE(0x0, 0xA)   // modify same slot: 5,000 Gas
SSTORE(0x1, 0x2)   // new slot: 22,100 Gas
```

**Why SSTORE is most expensive (1 mark):**
1. It writes **persistently to the state trie**, requiring Merkle Patricia Trie updates — $O(\log n)$ disk/database writes.
2. The state root in the block header must be recomputed.
3. Every full node on the network must update its state database.
4. This discourages on-chain storage abuse, which is critical for blockchain scalability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 — Ethereum Network:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Gas Limit vs Block Gas Limit.** A transaction's `gasLimit` is the *user's* cap; the *block* gas limit is network-wide (≈ 30M). Mixing them = -2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Base Fee is BURNED, not paid.** A frequent mistake is adding base fee to validator tip. Only the *priority fee* goes to the validator. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Wrong EIP-1559 formula.** Some students write `Fee = gasUsed × maxFeePerGas` instead of `Fee = gasUsed × effective_gas_price`. The user does **not** pay the maximum; they pay the *minimum* that satisfies inclusion. (-3 marks)
> 4. **Mistaking PoW for PoS in post-Merge questions.** Always state that post-September 2022, Ethereum uses **Proof-of-Stake**, not Proof-of-Work. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting the world state trie is a Merkle Patricia Trie**, not a binary trie or hash list. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Calling accounts "wallets."** A wallet is a *client interface*; the account is the on-chain data structure. (-1 mark)
> 7. **Unit errors:** $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 10^{9} \text{ Gwei}$. A missed Gwei→Wei conversion is a common silent error. (-1 to -2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute exam revision sheet for Module 4 — Ethereum Network.**

- ✅ **Ethereum** is a decentralized, **Turing-complete** blockchain introduced in 2015 by **Vitalik Buterin**; spec in the **Yellow Paper** by Gavin Wood.
- ✅ **EVM** = stack-based, 256-bit, quasi-Turing-complete virtual machine. Safety via **Gas**.
- ✅ **Two account types:** **EOA** (private-key controlled, no code, can initiate txs) and **Contract Account** (code-controlled, has bytecode & storage, cannot initiate txs).
- ✅ **Account fields:** `nonce, balance, storageRoot, codeHash` (160-bit address).
- ✅ **World state** = Merkle Patricia Trie of all accounts; root stored in block header.
- ✅ **Gas** = unit of computational effort. Pre-EIP-1559: `Fee = gasUsed × gasPrice`. Post-EIP-1559: `Fee = gasUsed × (BaseFee + Tip)`.
- ✅ **Base Fee** is **burned** (deflationary pressure). **Tip** goes to the validator.
- ✅ **Base Fee** adjusts ±12.5% per block toward a **15M Gas target**.
- ✅ **Block time:** ~**12 seconds** (post-Merge). **Finality:** ~**12.8 minutes** (2 epochs).
- ✅ **Post-Merge consensus:** **Proof-of-Stake** via **Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST**. Validators stake **32 ETH**.
- ✅ **EIP-1559** (London Upgrade, Aug 2021) introduced base fee + tip + max-fee model.
- ✅ **The Merge** (Sept 15, 2022) merged Beacon Chain (CL) with mainnet (EL) → energy ↓ ~99.95%.
- ✅ **Node types:** Full, Archive, Light, Validator. Validator reward: `R = R_base + R_attest + R_tip`.
- ✅ **Ether denominations:** $1 \text{ ETH} = 10^{18} \text{ Wei} = 10^{9} \text{ Gwei}$.
- ✅ **SSTORE cold = 22,100 Gas; warm modify = 5,000 Gas** (Berlin upgrade). Storage is the **most expensive** resource.
- ✅ **EVM stack max depth = 1024 items; word size = 256 bits; memory = byte-addressable, volatile.**
- ✅ **Transaction throughput:** ~30 TPS (L1); Layer-2 rollups scale to thousands of TPS.
- ✅ **Yellow Paper** = formal specification; **EIPs** = improvement proposals; **EVM** = execution runtime.
- ✅ **Consensus shift implications:** issuance ↓, ETH became deflationary under sustained high demand (burn > issuance).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of the Ethereum Ecosystem - Keys and Addresses, Accounts, Transactions, and Messages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT412 - INTRODUCTION TO BLOCK-CHAIN TECHNOLOGIES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Components of the Ethereum Ecosystem - Keys and Addresses, Accounts, Transactions, and Messages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Keys and Addresses — The Cryptographic Foundation

### Formal Academic Definition
In the Ethereum protocol, the cryptographic identity of a user is established through an **asymmetric key pair** generated using **Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**, specifically the **secp256k1** curve. The system employs three distinct cryptographic artifacts:

1. **Private Key** ($k_{priv}$): A randomly generated **256-bit (32-byte)** integer that must remain secret.
2. **Public Key** ($K_{pub}$): A 512-bit (64-byte) coordinate point $(x, y)$ on the secp256k1 elliptic curve, mathematically derived from the private key through **Elliptic Curve Multiplication** ($K_{pub} = k_{priv} \times G$, where $G$ is the generator point).
3. **Ethereum Address** ($A$): A **160-bit (20-byte)** identifier generated by taking the **Keccak-256** hash of the public key and extracting the rightmost (least significant) **20 bytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ethereum Address Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$A = \text{Keccak256}(K_{pub})[12:32]$$
> The address is conventionally represented as a **42-character hexadecimal string** prefixed with `0x` (e.g., `0x5aAeb6053F3E94C9b9A09f33669435E7Ef1BeAed`).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of the Ethereum account system like a **bank locker system**:
- The **Private Key** is the *physical master key* to your locker. Whoever holds it has total control. **Lose it, and the locker is forever inaccessible. Share it, and the locker is no longer yours.**
- The **Public Key** is the *serial number of your master key* — derived mathematically but mathematically infeasible to reverse-engineer into the original key.
- The **Ethereum Address** is the *visible locker number* stamped on the outside — what the world uses to send you assets, but useless for opening the locker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Keccak-256 and not SHA-256?**
> Ethereum uses the **Keccak-256** algorithm (the original submission to the NIST SHA-3 competition), which differs slightly from the finalized **FIPS-202 SHA-3 standard**. This is a frequently tested KTU distinction.

### Key Cryptographic Properties
| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Curve Used** | secp256k1 ($y^2 = x^3 + 7 \pmod{p}$) |
| **Private Key Range** | $1 \leq k_{priv} \leq n-1$, where $n \approx 2^{256}$ |
| **Public Key Size** | **64 bytes** (uncompressed: 512 bits) |
| **Address Size** | **20 bytes** (160 bits) |
| **Address Display** | Hexadecimal with `0x` prefix |
| **Checksum Mechanism** | EIP-55 (mixed-case checksum) |

---

## 1.2 Accounts — The State Holders

### Formal Academic Definition
An **Ethereum Account** is a fundamental state object in the Ethereum state tree (a modified Merkle Patricia Trie) that holds the data necessary to participate in the network. Every account is referenced by a **160-bit address** and contains four essential fields:

- **nonce**: A scalar counter representing the number of transactions sent (for EOAs) or the number of contracts created (for contract accounts).
- **balance**: The amount of **Wei** ($10^{-18}$ Ether) owned by the account.
- **codeHash**: The immutable hash of the EVM bytecode (empty hash for EOAs).
- **storageRoot**: The **256-bit root hash** of the account's storage trie.

### Two Account Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs)** are controlled by humans via private keys. They can **initiate** transactions but cannot contain executable code.
>
> **Contract Accounts** are governed by their immutable EVM bytecode. They have no private key and can only execute in response to a received transaction or message call.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
- **EOA** = A *person with a checkbook*. They decide when, where, and how much to spend. They act autonomously.
- **Contract Account** = A *vending machine*. It has no will of its own; it only "acts" when someone triggers it (by inserting a coin and pressing a button), and its behavior is rigidly determined by its internal logic (code).

| Feature | EOA | Contract Account |
|---------|-----|------------------|
| Controlled by | Private Key | EVM Code |
| Can initiate transactions | **Yes** | **No** |
| Has associated code | No (empty) | Yes (immutable) |
| Has private key | Yes | No |
| Storage | Empty trie | Non-empty storage trie |
| Created by | Key generation | Transaction (special type with `to = null`) |

---

## 1.3 Transactions — The State Transition Triggers

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Transaction** in Ethereum is a **cryptographically signed data package** that instructs the Ethereum network to perform a state transition. It is the **only mechanism** by which an EOA can broadcast intent to the network, and it is **serialized using Recursive Length Prefix (RLP) encoding** before propagation.

> [!NOTE]
> Every transaction contains **nine fields** (per the EIP-1559 / Legacy format combined view), and is signed using the **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm (ECDSA)** with the secp256k1 curve.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
A transaction is like a **certified postal letter with a tracking number and prepaid postage stamp**:
- **The content** is the instruction (send 1 ETH to address X, or deploy contract Y).
- **The signature** is your official seal — proves authenticity.
- **The nonce** is the tracking number — prevents replay attacks.
- **The gas** is the postage fee — must be paid upfront for processing.

### Transaction Field Reference

| Field | Purpose | Type |
|-------|---------|------|
| `nonce` | Sequence number of sender's transactions | uint64 |
| `gasPrice` | Price per unit of gas (Wei) | big integer |
| `gasLimit` | Maximum gas units consumable | uint64 |
| `to` | Recipient address (160-bit) | address (or null for contract creation) |
| `value` | Wei to transfer | big integer |
| `data` / `input` | Optional payload (call data or contract bytecode) | byte array |
| `v`, `r`, `s` | ECDSA signature components | three big integers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EIP-1559 Update (London Hard Fork, August 2021):** Modern transactions replace `gasPrice` with `maxPriorityFeePerGas` and `maxFeePerGas`. The actual fee burned is the **base fee**, which is dynamically adjusted. **This is a frequently tested KTU concept.**

---

## 1.4 Messages — The Inter-Contract Communication

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Message** in Ethereum is an **internal, virtual data object** passed between contract accounts during EVM execution. Unlike a transaction, a message is **never serialized, never signed, and never transmitted over the peer-to-peer network**. It exists exclusively within the EVM's execution environment as a function call parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> Messages are produced by **opcode instructions** in the EVM:
> - `CALL` (0xF1)
> - `DELEGATECALL` (0xF4)
> - `STATICCALL` (0xFA)
> - `CALLCODE` (deprecated since the Tangerine Whistle hard fork)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
If a **transaction** is a *phone call* you make (external → external), then a **message** is an *inter-office memo* passed inside the company (contract → contract). No one outside the EVM ever sees it; it's purely an internal coordination mechanism.

| Aspect | Transaction | Message |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Serialized to network | **Yes** | **No** |
| Requires ECDSA signature | **Yes** | **No** |
| Initiated by | EOA | Contract (via EVM opcode) |
| Has its own nonce | Yes (network nonce) | No (uses parent execution context) |
| Triggers state changes | Yes (atomic) | Yes (within the parent transaction) |
| Gas accounting | Sender pays | Deducted from parent's gas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Transaction vs. Message" distinction is one of the most heavily tested KTU 2024 concepts** in this module. Memorize the table above for Part A short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cryptographic Pipeline: From Private Key to Address

Ethereum's identity system can be understood as a **three-stage deterministic pipeline** where each stage is a one-way (trapdoor) function.

### Stage 1: Private Key Generation
The private key is generated as a cryptographically secure random 256-bit integer.

$$k_{priv} \xleftarrow{\$} \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, n-1\}$$

where $n$ is the order of the secp256k1 generator point $G$, given by:

$$n = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEBAAEDCE6AF48A03BBFD25E8CD0364141$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, wallets use the **OS-provided CSPRNG** (e.g., `/dev/urandom` on Linux, `CryptGenRandom` on Windows) to seed entropy. The KTU-relevant fact: a 256-bit space yields approximately $1.16 \times 10^{77}$ possible keys, making brute-force infeasible.

### Stage 2: Public Key Derivation via Elliptic Curve Multiplication
The public key is computed as:

$$K_{pub} = k_{priv} \cdot G$$

This is **not** standard multiplication; it is **Elliptic Curve Point Scalar Multiplication** — a sequence of point doublings and additions along the curve:

$$E: y^2 \equiv x^3 + 7 \pmod{p}$$

with prime field:

$$p = 2^{256} - 2^{32} - 977$$

**The Hardness Assumption:** Given $K_{pub}$ and $G$, finding $k_{priv}$ requires solving the **Elliptic Curve Discrete Logarithm Problem (ECDLP)**, which has no known polynomial-time classical algorithm and is estimated to require $\approx 2^{128}$ operations (best Pollard's rho attack).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ECC over RSA?**
> A 256-bit ECC key provides security equivalent to a **3072-bit RSA key**, with much smaller signature and key sizes. This efficiency is critical for blockchain storage and bandwidth.

### Stage 3: Address Derivation via Keccak-256
The address is the trailing 20 bytes of the Keccak-256 hash of the public key.

$$\text{PubKeyBytes} = x \parallel y \quad \text{(64 bytes, omitting the 0x04 prefix)}$$

$$H = \text{Keccak256}(\text{PubKeyBytes}) \in \{0,1\}^{256}$$

$$A = H[12 \text{ to } 31] \in \{0,1\}^{160}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **EIP-55 Checksum Address:** To prevent typos in addresses, Ethereum introduced a mixed-case checksum scheme. The capitalization of each hex digit depends on the hash of the address itself, allowing wallets to detect single-character typos. **This is a KTU 2024 syllabus highlight.**

---

## 2.2 Account State Model — The World State Trie

Ethereum maintains a global state known as the **World State**, structured as a **modified Merkle Patricia Trie** mapping **160-bit addresses → account state objects**.

### Account State Equation
The world state at block height $N$ is:

$$\sigma_{N} = \text{MerklePatriciaTrie}(\text{root}, \{A_i \mapsto \text{acct}(nonce, balance, codeHash, storageRoot)\})$$

The state transition function is defined as:

$$\sigma_{N+1} \equiv \Upsilon(\sigma_N, T)$$

where $\Upsilon$ is the EVM state transition function and $T$ is the set of transactions in block $N+1$.

### Why Two Account Types? Engineering Utility
This bifurcated design serves critical engineering purposes:

- **Separation of concerns**: EOAs represent *human intent*; Contract Accounts represent *programmatic logic*.
- **Determinism guarantee**: Since contract accounts have no private keys, their behavior is provably deterministic based solely on input data and prior state.
- **Composability**: Contracts calling other contracts (via messages) enable **DeFi composability** — the "money legos" architecture.

---

## 2.3 Transaction Lifecycle & Fee Model

### EIP-1559 Fee Equation (Modern Ethereum)

$$\text{EffectiveGasPrice} = \text{min}(\text{maxFeePerGas},\ \text{baseFee} + \text{maxPriorityFeePerGas})$$
